| **Power & Energy** | Tariff determination by CERC/SERC uses CAS principles |
| **Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs)** | MOU performance evaluation uses CAS figures |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Real-World Connector:** As a B.Tech engineer, you may work in a PSU or manufacturing firm where **cost data drives pricing, tenders, and performance bonuses**. CAS ensures the numbers you see are credible and auditable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although Cost Accounting Standards are largely **principle-based rather than formula-heavy**, KTU expects students to demonstrate **derivation logic** — i.e., *how a particular cost figure is built up from raw data* following CAS principles. Below are the most examiner-relevant derivations.

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Material Purchase to "Material Cost" per CAS 6

**Problem Context:** A Kerala-based engineering firm purchased 10,000 kg of copper rods during Q1.

**Given Data:**
- Purchase price: ₹500 per kg
- Freight inwards: ₹20,000 (lump sum)
- Loading/unloading charges: ₹10 per kg
- Trade discount: 5% on purchase price (deducted before billing)
- GST Input Tax Credit (refundable): ₹90 per kg
- Insurance in transit: ₹30,000
- Customs duty (non-refundable): ₹50 per kg

**Step 1 — Net Purchase Price**

$$
\text{Net Purchase Price} = \text{List Price} - \text{Trade Discount}
$$

$$
\text{Net Purchase Price} = 500 - (0.05 \times 500) = 500 - 25 = \text{\₹}475 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Add Directly Attributable Procurement Costs (CAS 6 Rule)**

These are costs that bring the material to the location of use.

$$
\text{Per kg freight} = \frac{20{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{\₹}2 \text{ per kg}
$$

$$
\text{Per kg insurance} = \frac{30{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{\₹}3 \text{ per kg}
$$

$$
\text{Loading/unloading} = \text{\₹}10 \text{ per kg}
$$

$$
\text{Customs duty (non-refundable)} = \text{\₹}50 \text{ per kg}
$$

$$
\text{Add} = 2 + 3 + 10 + 50 = \text{\₹}65 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Refundable Taxes**

GST input credit is refundable, hence **excluded** from material cost.

$$
\text{Subtract GST} = \text{\₹}90 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Material Cost per kg**

$$
\text{Material Cost per kg} = 475 + 65 - 90 = \text{\₹}450 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Material Cost**

$$
\text{Total Material Cost} = 450 \times 10{,}000 = \text{\₹}45{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key CAS 6 Takeaway:** Trade discount → **deduct before valuation**. GST input credit → **exclude** (refundable). Customs duty → **include** (non-refundable). Freight inward + insurance + handling → **include**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capacity-Based Overhead Absorption per CAS 2 & CAS 23

**Problem Context:** A precision engineering unit has:
- Installed capacity: 20,000 machine hours/year
- Planned maintenance downtime: 2,000 hours/year
- Expected idle time (market-related): 3,000 hours/year
- Budgeted sales volume: 12,000 machine hours
- Total annual manufacturing overheads: ₹1,00,00,000

**Step 1 — Compute Installed Capacity**

$$
\text{Installed Capacity} = 20{,}000 \text{ machine hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Practical Capacity (CAS 2)**

$$
\text{Practical Capacity} = \text{Installed} - \text{Planned Downtime}
$$

$$
\text{Practical Capacity} = 20{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 18{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Normal Capacity**

$$
\text{Normal Capacity} = 12{,}000 \text{ hours (based on long-term demand)}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose Recovery Base**

CAS 23 mandates that the **denominator of the absorption rate** be the higher of:
- Practical Capacity, OR
- Normal Capacity

Whichever is **higher** is used (this ensures under-recovery is minimized for fixed overheads).

$$
\text{Recovery Base} = \max(18{,}000,\ 12{,}000) = 18{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)**

$$
\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Total Manufacturing Overheads}}{\text{Recovery Base}}
$$

$$
\text{OAR} = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{18{,}000} = \text{\₹}555.56 \text{ per machine hour}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply to Actual Production**

Assume a particular order consumed 500 machine hours.

$$
\text{Overheads Allocated to Order} = 500 \times 555.56 = \text{\₹}2{,}77{,}780
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often use **Installed Capacity** as the base, which understates cost. The correct CAS 23 base is **Practical or Normal, whichever is higher**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Application of CAS to a Cost Sheet

The following matrix shows how a single "Cost of Sales" figure is built using multiple CAS principles, which is a typical KTU 14-mark problem.

| Step | Element | CAS Reference | Rule Applied | Example Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct Material | **CAS 6** | Net purchase + freight − refundable taxes | 5,00,000 |
| 2 | Direct Employee Cost | **CAS 7** | Gross wages + statutory benefits | 3,00,000 |
| 3 | Direct Expenses | **CAS 10** | Costs directly attributable (e.g., patent royalty) | 1,00,000 |
| 4 | Prime Cost (1+2+3) | — | Sum of direct costs | 9,00,000 |
| 5 | Production Overheads | **CAS 3, CAS 11, CAS 23** | Absorbed using practical capacity | 2,00,000 |
| 6 | Cost of Production (4+5) | **CAS 4** | Direct + Production OH | 11,00,000 |
| 7 | Add: Opening Stock of FG | — | Period-opening inventory | 50,000 |
| 8 | Less: Closing Stock of FG | — | Period-ending inventory | (70,000) |
| 9 | Cost of Goods Sold (6+7−8) | **CAS 24** | Production cost adjusted for stock | 10,80,000 |
| 10 | Add: Admin Overheads | **CAS 11** | Non-factory overheads | 80,000 |
| 11 | Add: Selling & Dist. Overheads | **CAS 15** | Marketing & distribution cost | 1,20,000 |
| 12 | **Cost of Sales (9+10+11)** | **CAS 24** | Final cost figure | **12,80,000** |

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is **examination gold**. Memorizing the **CAS reference number alongside each cost element** instantly earns you 2–3 bonus marks in the valuation script.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of the CAS Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ICAI Issues Cost Accounting Standards] --> B[CAS 1 to CAS 24]
    B --> C[Standards Categorized By Function]
    C --> D1[Cost Element Standards]
    C --> D2[Cost Presentation Standards]
    C --> D3[Cost Computation Standards]
    D1 --> E1[CAS 6 - Material]
    D1 --> E2[CAS 7 - Employee]
    D1 --> E3[CAS 8 - Utilities]
    D1 --> E4[CAS 16 - Depreciation]
    D1 --> E5[CAS 17 - Interest]
    D1 --> E6[CAS 18 - R and D]
    D2 --> F1[CAS 1 - Classification]
    D2 --> F2[CAS 4 - Cost of Production]
    D2 --> F3[CAS 22 - Manufacturing Cost]
    D2 --> F4[CAS 24 - Cost of Sales]
    D3 --> G1[CAS 2 - Capacity]
    D3 --> G2[CAS 3 - Overheads]
    D3 --> G3[CAS 11 - Admin Overheads]
    D3 --> G4[CAS 15 - Selling Overheads]
    D3 --> G5[CAS 23 - Overhead Absorption]
    E1 --> H[Cost Sheet Construction]
    E2 --> H
    E4 --> H
    F2 --> H
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Auditable Uniform Cost Data]
    I --> J1[Government Price Control]
    I --> J2[Cost Audit Compliance]
    I --> J3[Internal Managerial Decisions]
    I --> J4[Inter Firm Benchmarking]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — From Raw Data to Auditable Cost Figure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Transaction Data] --> B[Identify Cost Element]
    B --> C{Which CAS Applies?}
    C -->|Material Purchase| D[Apply CAS 6]
    C -->|Wages Paid| E[Apply CAS 7]
    C -->|Asset Depreciation| F[Apply CAS 16]
    C -->|Overhead Allocation| G[Apply CAS 3 and CAS 23]
    D --> H[Classified Cost Pool]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Apply CAS 4: Build Cost of Production]
    I --> J[Apply CAS 24: Compute Cost of Sales]
    J --> K[Cost Audit Verification]
    K --> L[Final Audited Cost Statement]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Relationship Between Key Standards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CAS1[CAS 1: Classification of Cost] --> ROOT[Foundation of All CAS]
    CAS2[CAS 2: Capacity Determination] --> CAS23
    CAS3[CAS 3: Overheads] --> CAS11
    CAS3 --> CAS15
    CAS4[CAS 4: Cost of Production] --> CAS22
    CAS4 --> CAS24
    CAS6[CAS 6: Material Cost] --> CAS4
    CAS7[CAS 7: Employee Cost] --> CAS4
    CAS10[CAS 10: Direct Expenses] --> CAS4
    CAS16[CAS 16: Depreciation] --> CAS4
    CAS23[CAS 23: Overhead Absorption] --> CAS4
    CAS11[CAS 11: Admin Overheads] --> CAS24
    CAS15[CAS 15: Selling Overheads] --> CAS24
    CAS22[CAS 22: Manufacturing Cost] --> CAS24
    CAS24[CAS 24: Cost of Sales] --> FINAL[Final Cost Output]
    ROOT --> CAS1
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** The conceptual map above is perfect for revision. Always remember: **CAS 1 (Classification) is the root**, **CAS 4 (Production) is the trunk**, and **CAS 24 (Sales) is the crown** of the cost computation tree.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Cost Accounting Standards. State any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cost Accounting Standards (CAS) are codified principles and methodologies issued by the **Institute of Cost Accountants of India (ICAI)** to ensure uniform and consistent determination of cost of products or services across organizations.

**Four Objectives:**
1. To bring **uniformity** in cost accounting practices across industries.
2. To ensure **comparability** of cost data between organizations and across time periods.
3. To provide a **reliable basis for cost audit** under the Companies Act, 2013.
4. To assist the government in **price regulation** of goods and services in notified sectors.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List any six Cost Accounting Standards issued by ICAI and state the focus area of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| CAS No. | Focus Area |
|---|---|
| CAS 1 | Classification of Cost |
| CAS 2 | Capacity Determination |
| CAS 4 | Cost of Production / Acquisition / Supply of Goods |
| CAS 6 | Material Cost |
| CAS 7 | Employee Cost |
| CAS 16 | Depreciation and Amortisation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU ESE pattern, students answer **one full question out of two internal choices**. Below are two complete alternative questions with full sub-parts and worked solutions.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q. (a) Explain in detail the objectives, scope, and significance of Cost Accounting Standards issued by ICAI. Discuss how they contribute to corporate governance and regulatory compliance.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Cost Accounting Standards (CAS) are issued by the Institute of Cost Accountants of India (ICAI) under the authority of the Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules, 2014. They prescribe uniform methods for cost determination across industries.

**2. Objectives (2.5 marks):**
- **Uniformity:** Standardize cost practices across firms.
- **Consistency:** Enable period-over-period comparison within the same firm.
- **Comparability:** Allow benchmarking between firms.
- **Reliability:** Provide verified data for cost audit.
- **Transparency:** Strengthen disclosure in financial reporting.

**3. Scope (1.5 marks):**
- Applicable to companies engaged in production, manufacturing, mining, processing, or service sectors as notified by the Central Government.
- Covers **all cost elements** — material, employee, utilities, overheads, depreciation, R&D, interest, royalty, pollution control, etc.
- Mandatory for cost audit under Section 148 of the Companies Act, 2013.

**4. Significance for Corporate Governance (2 marks):**
- Enables **independent verification** of costs by cost auditors.
- Reduces **manipulation of cost data** for tax or pricing benefits.
- Strengthens **board-level oversight** through standardized MIS reporting.
- Aligns with **SEBI, MCA, and tax authority** requirements.

---

**Q. (b) Explain CAS 4 (Cost of Production) and CAS 24 (Cost of Sales) in detail with suitable formulas and a comparative note.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**CAS 4 — Cost of Production (3.5 marks):**
CAS 4 prescribes the methodology to compute the comprehensive cost of producing or acquiring a good or providing a service.

$$
\text{Cost of Production} = \text{Direct Material} + \text{Direct Employee Cost} + \text{Direct Expenses} + \text{Production Overheads}
$$

- **Direct Material:** Includes all material that can be directly traced to the product (CAS 6).
- **Direct Employee Cost:** Wages, salaries, and benefits directly attributable to production (CAS 7).
- **Direct Expenses:** Costs like royalties, special tools, hire charges (CAS 10).
- **Production Overheads:** Indirect factory costs like depreciation, indirect labour, factory rent (CAS 3 & CAS 23).

**CAS 24 — Cost of Sales (3.5 marks):**
CAS 24 prescribes the determination of the **final cost figure** that flows into the profit and loss statement.

$$
\text{Cost of Sales} = \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Opening FG Stock} - \text{Closing FG Stock} + \text{Admin OH} + \text{Selling \& Dist. OH}
$$

**Comparative Note:**

| Aspect | CAS 4 | CAS 24 |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage** | Production-stage cost | Sales-stage cost |
| **Includes** | Manufacturing-related costs | Post-production costs |
| **Used for** | Inventory valuation, internal pricing | Pricing decisions, profitability analysis, statutory reporting |
| **Stock adjustment** | No | Yes (opening − closing FG) |

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q. (a) Discuss CAS 2 (Capacity Determination) and CAS 23 (Overhead Absorption) in detail. How does the choice of capacity base affect product cost and profitability reporting?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. CAS 2 — Capacity Determination (3 marks):**
CAS 2 prescribes how to determine the **denominator base** for absorbing fixed overheads.

$$
\text{Installed Capacity} = \text{Maximum rated output of plant}
$$

$$
\text{Practical Capacity} = \text{Installed Capacity} - \text{Planned Downtime}
$$

$$
\text{Normal Capacity} = \text{Long-term average demand (typically 2–3 years)}
$$

**Example:** A plant has 20,000 hours installed capacity and 2,000 hours of planned downtime → Practical capacity = 18,000 hours.

**2. CAS 23 — Overhead Absorption (2.5 marks):**
CAS 23 mandates that the recovery base must be the **higher of Practical or Normal capacity**, ensuring fair absorption of fixed overheads.

$$
\text{OAR} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Overheads}}{\max(\text{Practical, Normal Capacity})}
$$

**3. Impact on Product Cost and Profitability (1.5 marks):**
- A **lower base** (e.g., using budgeted sales of 12,000 hours) inflates OAR → **higher product cost** → **lower reported profit** → lower taxes.
- A **higher base** (e.g., practical capacity of 18,000 hours) lowers OAR → **lower product cost** → **higher reported profit**, but ensures under-recovery is minimized.
- CAS prescribes the **higher base** to prevent management from manipulating profit through optimistic capacity assumptions.

---

**Q. (b) From the following data of an engineering unit, compute the material cost as per CAS 6 and the Cost of Production as per CAS 4.** **(7 Marks)**

**Data:**
- Purchase of raw material: 5,000 kg @ ₹400 per kg
- Trade discount: 5% on list price
- Freight inwards: ₹20,000 (lump sum)
- Loading and unloading: ₹5 per kg
- GST (refundable): ₹72 per kg
- Customs duty (non-refundable): ₹40 per kg
- Direct wages: ₹2,00,000
- Direct expenses: ₹80,000
- Production overheads absorbed: ₹1,50,000

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net Purchase Price**

$$
\text{Net Price} = 400 - (0.05 \times 400) = 400 - 20 = \text{\₹}380 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-kg Attributable Costs**

$$
\text{Per-kg freight} = \frac{20{,}000}{5{,}000} = \text{\₹}4 \text{ per kg}
$$

$$
\text{Loading/unloading} = \text{\₹}5 \text{ per kg}
$$

$$
\text{Customs duty} = \text{\₹}40 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Refundable GST**

$$
\text{Material Cost per kg} = 380 + 4 + 5 + 40 - 72 = \text{\₹}357 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Material Cost**

$$
\text{Total Material Cost} = 357 \times 5{,}000 = \text{\₹}17{,}85{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Cost of Production (CAS 4)**

$$
\text{Cost of Production} = \text{Material} + \text{Wages} + \text{Direct Expenses} + \text{Production OH}
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Production} = 17{,}85{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{\₹}22{,}15{,}000
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct trade discount** before applying GST/refundable taxes.
> 2. **Incorrectly including refundable GST** in material cost (it must be excluded).
> 3. **Forgetting to convert lump-sum freight into per-kg cost** before adding to unit cost.
> 4. **Confusing "Customs Duty" with "GST"** — customs is non-refundable (include), GST is refundable (exclude).
> 5. **Skipping units** in the final answer — always state "per kg" or "total" explicitly.
> 6. **Omitting CAS reference numbers** in the calculation — examiners reward students who cite "As per CAS 6…" or "As per CAS 4…".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Issuing Authority:** CAS is issued by the **Institute of Cost Accountants of India (ICAI)**, not by the Government directly.
- ✅ **Number of Standards:** Currently **24 CAS (CAS 1 to CAS 24)** are in force.
- ✅ **Legal Backing:** Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules, 2014, under the Companies Act, 2013, Section 148.
- ✅ **Foundation Standard:** **CAS 1** is the root — it prescribes cost classification.
- ✅ **Production Standard:** **CAS 4** defines cost of production (the trunk of the tree).
- ✅ **Final Output Standard:** **CAS 24** defines cost of sales (the crown of the tree).
- ✅ **Capacity Hierarchy:** **Installed > Practical > Normal**, and the recovery base for CAS 23 = **max(Practical, Normal)**.
- ✅ **Material Cost Rule (CAS 6):** Include purchase + freight + non-refundable duties; **exclude** refundable taxes and trade discounts.
- ✅ **Employee Cost Rule (CAS 7):** Use **gross emoluments** including all statutory benefits.
- ✅ **Depreciation Rule (CAS 16):** Follow the **Companies Act, 2013, Schedule II** useful life provisions.
- ✅ **CAS Reference is Mandatory:** Always cite the specific CAS number in your exam answer to score higher.
- ✅ **Memory Trick for Top 6 CAS:** *"1 Clas-sify, 2 Capa-city, 4 Produce, 6 Material, 7 Employee, 24 Sales."*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Day Strategy (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - For a 3-mark question, give definition + 4 objectives/list.
> - For a 7-mark question, explain **2 CAS in detail** with formulas and a comparative table.
> - Always conclude with **"as prescribed by ICAI under the Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules, 2014"** — this single line often fetches 0.5–1 grace mark in valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Accounting Records
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cost Accounting Records # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition **Cost Accounting Records** refer to the systematic, organized, and chronological set of books, documents, vouchers, registers, statements, and reports that capture, classify, summarize, and preserve all financial transactions and resource consumptions related to the production of goods or delivery of services within an organization. Under the Indian Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules and the Cost Accounting Standards (CAS) issued by the **Institute of Cost Accountants of India (ICAI-CMA)**, these records form the documentary backbone that enables the ascertainment, allocation, and absorption of material costs, labour costs, and overhead costs to products, services, departments, or cost centres. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of engineering management, *Cost Accounting Records* are the **primary data infrastructure** that converts raw operational data (kilograms of steel used, machine hours operated, man-hours deployed) into structured cost information usable for pricing, budgeting, and managerial decision-making. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are running a small engineering workshop in Kochi that manufactures custom gearboxes. Every day, you buy steel rods, pay your machinist, consume electricity, and use cutting oil. At the end of the month, your accountant asks, *"How much did it actually cost to make gearbox #47?"* Without **Cost Accounting Records**, you would have to sift through a chaotic pile of cash memos, electricity bills, and casual notes — and you would still not know whether the ₹8,000 of steel went entirely into that one gearbox or was shared across three. **Cost Accounting Records** are like a meticulously maintained **"health logbook" of a manufacturing business**: every receipt is categorized, every machine hour is logged, every worker's time is stamped. Just as a doctor needs a patient's medical history to diagnose correctly, a cost accountant needs these records to diagnose the financial health of each product. ## Key Terminology & Standard Metrics | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Cost Centre** | A location, function, or item of equipment in respect of which costs may be ascertained (e.g., Foundry, Assembly Bay) | | **Cost Unit** | A unit of product or service in relation to which costs are ascertained (e.g., 1 gearbox, 1 tonne of cement) | | **Cost Object** | Anything for which a separate measurement of cost is desired (e.g., a product, a project, a department) | | **Material Requisition Note (MRN)** | A document authorizing the issue of materials from stores to production | | **Job Card / Job Ticket** | A record of time spent and operations performed by a worker on a specific job | | **Stores Ledger** | A register maintaining the quantity and value of materials in inventory | > [!NOTE] > **Key Statutory Reference:** Under **Section 148 of the Companies Act, 2013** and the associated Rules, certain classes of companies (those engaged in production, processing, manufacturing, mining, or generation of electricity) are mandatorily required to maintain proper cost accounting records. The threshold limits are revised periodically by the Central Government. ## Why Cost Accounting Records Matter for Engineers For a B.Tech student under the KTU 2024 scheme, understanding cost records is not merely an accountant's concern. Whether you become a production engineer, a project manager, or an entrepreneur, you will: - **Author** material requisitions and job cards on the shop floor. - **Verify** that machine-hour allocations in cost sheets align with your actual production data. - **Use** cost-record outputs (like per-unit cost) for make-or-buy decisions, capacity planning, and bid preparation. - **Comply** with statutory audit requirements that apply to large-scale manufacturing operations. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Objectives of Maintaining Cost Accounting Records The maintenance of cost accounting records is governed by a clear set of objectives. These are not arbitrary — they are designed to fulfil both **statutory compliance** and **managerial information needs**. 1. **Statutory Compliance** — To satisfy the requirements of the Companies Act, 2013, and the Cost Accounting Standards prescribed by the ICAI-CMA. 2. **Cost Ascertainment** — To determine the actual cost of production of each product, service, job, or process. 3. **Cost Control** — To compare actual costs with standard or budgeted costs, identify variances, and take corrective action. 4. **Pricing Decisions** — To provide a rational basis for fixing selling prices, especially in regulated or tender-based industries. 5. **Profitability Analysis** — To determine the profit or loss attributable to each product, department, territory, or customer. 6. **Managerial Decision-Making** — To supply relevant cost data for decisions like outsourcing, product-mix selection, replacement of machinery, and inventory valuation. 7. **Protection Against Losses** — To detect theft, wastage, idle time, and inefficiencies through documented evidence. 8. **Basis for Cost Audit** — To facilitate the statutory or voluntary audit of cost statements by a Cost Accountant in Practice (CMA). ## 2.2 Essential Characteristics of Good Cost Accounting Records For the records to be effective, they must possess certain qualities. KTU examiners frequently test these in 2-mark and 3-mark questions. - **Simplicity** — Easy to understand and operate by shop-floor staff. - **Accuracy** — Free from errors; supported by source documents. - **Completeness** — All transactions must be captured without omission. - **Comparability** — Uniform classification across periods for trend analysis. - **Timeliness** — Available when needed, not after months of delay. - **Verifiability** — Supported by vouchers and evidence, enabling audit. - **Cost-Effectiveness** — The cost of maintaining the records should not exceed the benefit derived. ## 2.3 Classification of Cost Accounting Records The records can be classified into several logical groups. The following structure is the one KTU examiners expect: | Category | Examples of Records | |---|---| | **Material Records** | Stores ledger, bin card, material requisition note, purchase requisition, goods received note, materials return note | | **Labour Records** | Job card, attendance register, payroll register, overtime register, idle time register, piece-work card | | **Overhead Records** | Overhead allocation sheets, machine-hour log, depreciation schedule, factory expense book | | **Output Records** | Finished goods register, production report, scrap and wastage report | | **Cost Ledger & Cost Statements** | Cost ledger, cost sheet, job cost sheet, process cost sheet | | **Auxiliary Records** | Bill of materials (BOM), route sheet, work-in-progress ledger | ## 2.4 Statutory Framework & Cost Accounting Standards (CAS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Topic:** Students often confuse *Cost Records* with *Cost Audit*. The records are the **books of data**; the audit is the **examination** of those books by a qualified Cost Accountant. The **Cost Accounting Standards (CAS)** issued by ICAI-CMA provide uniform principles for the treatment of cost. A few standards directly relevant to records are: - **CAS-1**: Classification of Costs - **CAS-2**: Capacity Determination - **CAS-3**: Production and Operation Overheads - **CAS-6**: Material Cost - **CAS-7**: Employee Cost - **CAS-8**: Cost of Utilities - **CAS-15**: Selling and Distribution Overheads These standards prescribe the **minimum content and format** of records, making the data comparable across companies and industries. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below form the **cost-recording arithmetic** that you will repeatedly apply in KTU Part A and Part B numericals. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Prime Cost} = \text{Direct Materials} + \text{Direct Labour} + \text{Direct Expenses}$ | Cost directly traceable to the product | ₹ | | 2 | $\text{Works Cost} = \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Factory / Manufacturing Overheads}$ | Total factory cost | ₹ | | 3 | $\text{Cost of Production} = \text{Works Cost} + \text{Administrative Overheads} \pm \text{Opening/Closing WIP}$ | Cost of goods produced | ₹ | | 4 | $\text{Cost of Goods Sold} = \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Opening FG} - \text{Closing FG}$ | Cost of units actually sold | ₹ | | 5 | $\text{Total Cost} = \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Selling \& Distribution Overheads}$ | Full cost delivered to customer | ₹ | | 6 | $\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{Total Cost}$ | Absolute profit | ₹ | | 7 | $\text{Profit \%} = \dfrac{\text{Profit}}{\text{Cost of Sales}} \times 100$ | Profitability ratio | % | | 8 | $\text{Per Unit Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{No. of Units Produced}}$ | Unit cost for pricing | ₹/unit | | 9 | $\text{Hourly Machine Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Total Machine Overhead}}{\text{Total Effective Machine Hours}}$ | Overhead absorption rate | ₹/hr | | 10 | $\text{Material Issue Price} = \text{Purchase Price} + \text{Direct Procurement Cost} \pm \text{Inventory Adjustment}$ | Stores valuation | ₹/unit | > [!TIP] > For KTU board exams, always present formulas **before** plugging in numbers. Examiners award step-marks liberally to candidates who show the formula clearly. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields In modern Industry 4.0 manufacturing units, cost accounting records have evolved from paper registers to **integrated ERP-driven databases**. For example: - A **CNC machine shop** automatically logs spindle hours, energy consumption, and tool wear — these feed directly into the cost ledger. - A **construction company** uses job cards to capture crane-hours and concrete consumption per tower floor, enabling per-square-foot cost tracking. - A **start-up manufacturing unit** uses cloud-based accounting software (like Zoho Books or Tally Prime with cost-centre modules) where every transaction is tagged to a cost centre at the entry level itself. Thus, even engineering students will interface with cost records when they author BOMs, approve job cards, or interpret variance reports on the shop floor. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Cost-Sheet Flow A cost sheet is the **aggregated output** of all cost accounting records. Let us derive how each element flows logically from the underlying records. ### Step 1 — Identify and Capture Material Costs Materials are first received in stores. The **Goods Received Note (GRN)** updates the bin card (quantitative) and stores ledger (quantitative + value). When production begins, a **Material Requisition Note (MRN)** triggers the issue of materials to the shop floor at the recorded issue price. $$ \text{Material Consumed} = \text{Opening Stock of Materials} + \text{Materials Purchased} - \text{Closing Stock of Materials} $$ The above figure flows into the cost sheet as **Direct Materials**. ### Step 2 — Capture Direct Labour Each worker is assigned a **Job Card** that records the time spent on each job and the worker's wage rate. The daily **Attendance Register** provides the basis for payroll. $$ \text{Direct Labour Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Hours worked by Worker } i \times \text{Hourly Rate of Worker } i) $$ This amount flows into the cost sheet as **Direct Labour (Direct Wages)**. ### Step 3 — Identify Direct Expenses These are expenses directly chargeable to a specific job, such as hiring of special equipment, royalties, or design fees paid to an outside consultant for that product. They are captured in the **Expense Voucher Register**. ### Step 4 — Compute Prime Cost $$ \text{Prime Cost} = \text{Direct Materials} + \text{Direct Labour} + \text{Direct Expenses} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Prime Cost represents the **total minimum outflow** that must be incurred to put the product in a saleable condition. It is the first major subtotal in any cost sheet. ### Step 5 — Add Factory / Manufacturing Overheads Manufacturing overheads are **indirect costs** that cannot be traced directly to a single unit. They include factory rent, depreciation of machinery, indirect labour, factory consumables, and power. The **Overhead Allocation Sheet** distributes these across cost centres using suitable bases (machine hours, labour hours, etc.). $$ \text{Works Cost} = \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Manufacturing Overheads} $$ ### Step 6 — Add Administrative Overheads & Adjust WIP Administrative overheads are expenses of the office — salaries of office staff, audit fees, depreciation of office furniture. $$ \text{Cost of Production} = \text{Works Cost} + \text{Administrative Overheads} + \text{Opening WIP} - \text{Closing WIP} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** WIP (Work-in-Progress) adjustment is essential because the period's cost sheet covers all units **started**, not all units **completed**. ### Step 7 — Adjust Finished Goods Stock $$ \text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)} = \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Opening FG} - \text{Closing FG} $$ ### Step 8 — Add Selling & Distribution Overheads These include advertising, packing, freight outwards, sales commission, and showroom rent. $$ \text{Total Cost} = \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Selling \& Distribution Overheads} $$ ### Step 9 — Compute Profit $$ \text{Profit} = \text{Sales Revenue} - \text{Total Cost} $$ ### Worked-Out Numerical Illustration > [!NOTE] > The following example mirrors the **exact style** of KTU 2024 Scheme Part B problems (14-mark sub-parts). **Problem Statement:** A manufacturing unit furnishes the following data for the year ended 31st March 2025: | Item | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Opening Stock of Raw Materials | 50,000 | | Closing Stock of Raw Materials | 35,000 | | Purchases of Raw Materials | 4,20,000 | | Direct Wages | 1,80,000 | | Direct Expenses | 25,000 | | Manufacturing Overheads | 95,000 | | Administrative Overheads | 45,000 | | Selling & Distribution Overheads | 60,000 | | Opening WIP | 15,000 | | Closing WIP | 20,000 | | Opening Finished Goods | 30,000 | | Closing Finished Goods | 25,000 | | Sales Revenue | 9,00,000 | **Required:** Prepare a **Cost Sheet** for the year and compute the net profit. --- **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1 — Direct Materials Consumed:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Direct Materials Consumed} &= \text{Opening Stock} + \text{Purchases} - \text{Closing Stock} \\ &= 50{,}000 + 4{,}20{,}000 - 35{,}000 \\ &= 4{,}35{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Materials are issued from the stores ledger throughout the year. The stores ledger starts at ₹50,000, gets additions of ₹4,20,000 (purchases), and ends at ₹35,000. The difference is what was issued to production. **Step 2 — Prime Cost:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Prime Cost} &= \text{Direct Materials} + \text{Direct Wages} + \text{Direct Expenses} \\ &= 4{,}35{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 25{,}000 \\ &= 6{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Works Cost (Factory Cost):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Works Cost} &= \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Manufacturing Overheads} \\ &= 6{,}40{,}000 + 95{,}000 \\ &= 7{,}35{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Cost of Production (with WIP adjustment):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost of Production} &= \text{Works Cost} + \text{Admin Overheads} + \text{Opening WIP} - \text{Closing WIP} \\ &= 7{,}35{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 15{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\ &= 7{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** WIP adjustment is necessary because the ₹7,35,000 of work done includes the unfinished jobs still in the factory at year-end. Those unfinished jobs are valued at ₹20,000; the work done on them at the start of the year (Opening WIP ₹15,000) was completed and is now in the ₹7,35,000. **Step 5 — Cost of Goods Sold (with FG adjustment):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost of Goods Sold} &= \text{Cost of Production} + \text{Opening FG} - \text{Closing FG} \\ &= 7{,}75{,}000 + 30{,}000 - 25{,}000 \\ &= 7{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Total Cost (adding S&D):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Cost} &= \text{COGS} + \text{Selling \& Distribution Overheads} \\ &= 7{,}80{,}000 + 60{,}000 \\ &= 8{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Net Profit:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Profit} &= \text{Sales} - \text{Total Cost} \\ &= 9{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}40{,}000 \\ &= 60{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Profit Percentage on Cost:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Profit \%} &= \dfrac{60{,}000}{8{,}40{,}000} \times 100 \\ &\approx 7.14\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** The denominator is "Cost" (not Sales) because this is a *Cost-Plus* profit margin, the conventional measure in cost accounting. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — How Each Source Record Feeds the Cost Sheet | Source Record (in the books) | Field Captured | Destination in Cost Sheet | |---|---|---| | Stores Ledger + Purchase Register | Material Consumption | Direct Materials | | Job Card + Attendance Register | Direct Labour Hours × Rate | Direct Wages | | Expense Voucher Register | Special hire charges, royalties | Direct Expenses | | Overhead Allocation Sheet (machine-hour basis) | Factory rent, depreciation, indirect labour | Manufacturing Overheads | | Administrative Expense Register | Office salaries, audit fees | Administrative Overheads | | WIP Ledger | Partially completed goods | Opening / Closing WIP | | Finished Goods Stock Register | Goods ready for sale | Opening / Closing FG | | Sales Invoice Register + Dispatch Register | Revenue and dispatch costs | Sales + S&D Overheads | > **Engineering Insight:** The matrix above is, in essence, the **data-flow architecture** of a costing system. Modern ERP systems (SAP, Oracle, Tally with Cost Centres) automate exactly this flow. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cost Accounting Records The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how **Cost Accounting Records** are not a single document but a **layered system** of inputs, processes, and outputs. The flow moves from raw transactional data (Layer 1) to summarised cost sheets (Layer 4). ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Layer 1: Source Documents
Purchase Orders, GRN, Job Cards,
Attendance Register, MRN, Invoices] A2[Layer 2: Subsidiary Records
Stores Ledger, Bin Card,
Wages Register, Overhead Allocation Sheets] A3[Layer 3: Cost Ledgers and Control Accounts
Material Control A/c, Wages Control A/c,
Overhead Control A/c, WIP A/c] A4[Layer 4: Cost Statements
Cost Sheet, Job Cost Sheet,
Process Cost Sheet, Profitability Report] A1 -->|captures| A2 A2 -->|classifies and posts| A3 A3 -->|summarises| A4 A4 --> B1[Management Decisions
Pricing, Make-or-Buy, Cost Control] A4 --> B2[Statutory Compliance
Companies Act 2013 Section 148] A4 --> B3[Cost Audit
Verification by CMA] A4 --> B4[Performance Evaluation
Variance Analysis, KPI Tracking] classDef layer1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef layer2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef layer3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef layer4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef output fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 class A1 layer1 class A2 layer2 class A3 layer3 class A4 layer4 class B1,B2,B3,B4 output ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchy of Costs — The "Cost Tower" The standard **cost-buildup** sequence (Prime → Works → Cost of Production → Total Cost) is best visualised as a vertical tower, where each block is built on top of the previous one. This is the diagram KTU examiners expect you to draw when a 7-mark question asks you to "explain the elements of cost." ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Sales Revenue] L2[Less: Profit] L3[Total Cost] L4[Add: Selling and Distribution Overheads] L5[Cost of Production] L6[Add: Administrative Overheads] L7[Add: Opening WIP, Less: Closing WIP] L8[Works Cost / Factory Cost] L9[Add: Manufacturing Overheads] L10[Prime Cost] L11[Direct Materials] L12[Direct Labour] L13[Direct Expenses] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> L7 L7 --> L8 L8 --> L9 L9 --> L10 L10 --> L11 L10 --> L12 L10 --> L13 classDef cost fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef top fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 class L1,L2 top class L3,L4,L5,L6,L7,L8,L9,L10,L11,L12,L13 cost ``` ## 4.3 Document Flow Diagram — Material Movement in a Factory The following diagram traces a material from the moment it is **purchased** until it becomes **part of the finished product cost**, with all intermediate records indicated. ```mermaid flowchart LR D1[Purchase Requisition] --> D2[Purchase Order] D2 --> D3[Goods Received Note GRN] D3 --> D4[Stores Ledger Entry] D4 --> D5[Bin Card Update] D5 --> D6[Material Requisition Note MRN] D6 --> D7[Issue to Production] D7 --> D8[Job Card and Work-in-Progress Ledger] D8 --> D9[Finished Goods Register] D9 --> D10[Cost Sheet Aggregation] classDef doc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000 class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5,D6,D7,D8,D9,D10 doc ``` > **Engineering Interpretation:** The chain `GRN → Stores Ledger → MRN → Job Card → Finished Goods → Cost Sheet` is the **physical evidence trail** that a statutory cost auditor will examine. The presence (or absence) of each document determines whether a cost claim is *verifiable* in a cost audit. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!NOTE] > All questions below are styled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. The Part A questions are 3-mark short-answer type, and Part B questions are 14-mark (with internal choice) carrying two 7-mark sub-parts each. --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Cost Accounting Records. List any four essential characteristics of good cost accounting records.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Cost Accounting Records are the organised set of books, vouchers, registers, and reports that systematically capture, classify, and summarise all transactions relating to the consumption of materials, labour, and overheads for ascertaining the cost of products, services, jobs, or processes. **Four Essential Characteristics (2 Marks — ½ mark each):** 1. **Accuracy** — Records must be free from errors and supported by source documents. 2. **Simplicity** — The recording system should be easily understood by shop-floor and office staff. 3. **Timeliness** — Information should be available promptly to enable timely managerial decisions. 4. **Comparability** — Uniform classification of items across accounting periods to allow meaningful comparisons. > **Examiner's Note:** Students often write "relevance" and "completeness" — both are acceptable, but ensure you pair each characteristic with a one-line explanation to score full marks. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Distinguish between Cost Accounting Records and Cost Audit. Why is the maintenance of cost records considered mandatory for certain companies under the Companies Act, 2013?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | Cost Accounting Records (1 Mark) | Cost Audit (1 Mark) | |---|---|---| | Meaning | Books and documents that capture cost data | Examination and verification of those books | | Conducted by | Maintained by the company's own staff | Conducted by a Cost Accountant in Practice (CMA) | | Purpose | To record, classify, and ascertain cost | To verify the accuracy and ensure compliance | | Frequency | Continuous process | Periodic (usually annual) | **Mandatory Maintenance Reason (1 Mark):** Under **Section 148 of the Companies Act, 2013** and the associated Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules, companies engaged in production, manufacturing, mining, or generation of electricity, and whose turnover exceeds the prescribed threshold, are required to maintain cost records and get them audited. The objective is to ensure **transparency, prevent cost manipulation, and protect the interests of stakeholders and the public at large.** --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU ESE Part B in management courses carries 14 marks. As per the 2024 scheme, you have an **internal choice** between two questions from the same module. Each sub-part is 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the **classification of cost accounting records** maintained in a manufacturing organisation. List at least two specific records under each category. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A factory provides the following data for the month of April 2025: | Item | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Opening Stock of Raw Materials | 40,000 | | Closing Stock of Raw Materials | 30,000 | | Purchases of Raw Materials | 2,50,000 | | Direct Wages | 95,000 | | Direct Expenses | 15,000 | | Manufacturing Overheads | 55,000 | | Administrative Overheads | 25,000 | | Selling & Distribution Overheads | 30,000 | | Opening WIP | 8,000 | | Closing WIP | 10,000 | | Opening Finished Goods | 20,000 | | Closing Finished Goods | 18,000 | | Sales | 5,00,000 | Prepare a **Cost Sheet** showing Prime Cost, Works Cost, Cost of Production, Cost of Goods Sold, Total Cost, and Net Profit. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Classification of Cost Accounting Records (7 Marks)** **Introduction (1 Mark):** Cost accounting records in a manufacturing firm are classified into logical groups based on the type of cost they capture. **Block Diagram (3 Marks — drawn by the student):** The student is expected to reproduce a classification chart similar to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1, listing: 1. **Material Records (1 Mark):** - Stores Ledger — records quantity and value of materials. - Bin Card — records only quantity of each material in stores. - Material Requisition Note (MRN) — authorises issue to production. - Goods Received Note (GRN) — records materials received from suppliers. 2. **Labour Records (1 Mark):** - Job Card — records time and operations for each worker on each job. - Attendance Register — provides basis for payroll. - Overtime Register — records extra hours worked. - Piece-work Card — records output-based wages. 3. **Overhead Records (1 Mark):** - Overhead Allocation Sheet — distributes indirect costs to cost centres. - Machine-hour Log — records actual machine usage. - Depreciation Schedule — non-cash charge on fixed assets. **Conclusion (½ Mark):** Proper integration of these records ensures accurate product costing and effective cost control. --- **Part (b) — Cost Sheet Preparation (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Direct Materials Consumed (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Direct Materials} &= 40{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 - 30{,}000 \\ &= 2{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Prime Cost (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Prime Cost} &= 2{,}60{,}000 + 95{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\ &= 3{,}70{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Works Cost (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Works Cost} &= 3{,}70{,}000 + 55{,}000 \\ &= 4{,}25{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Cost of Production (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost of Production} &= 4{,}25{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 8{,}000 - 10{,}000 \\ &= 4{,}48{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Cost of Goods Sold (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COGS} &= 4{,}48{,}000 + 20{,}000 - 18{,}000 \\ &= 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Total Cost & Profit (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Cost} &= 4{,}50{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 4{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\ \text{Profit} &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}80{,}000 = 20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\ \text{Profit \% on Cost} &= \dfrac{20{,}000}{4{,}80{,}000} \times 100 \approx 4.17\% \end{aligned} $$ **Summary Cost Sheet (½ Mark for Presentation):** | Element | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Direct Materials Consumed | 2,60,000 | | Direct Wages | 95,000 | | Direct Expenses | 15,000 | | **Prime Cost** | **3,70,000** | | Manufacturing Overheads | 55,000 | | **Works Cost** | **4,25,000** | | Administrative Overheads | 25,000 | | Add: Opening WIP | 8,000 | | Less: Closing WIP | (10,000) | | **Cost of Production** | **4,48,000** | | Add: Opening FG | 20,000 | | Less: Closing FG | (18,000) | | **Cost of Goods Sold** | **4,50,000** | | Selling & Distribution Overheads | 30,000 | | **Total Cost** | **4,80,000** | | **Profit** | **20,000** | --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Discuss the **statutory provisions relating to maintenance of cost accounting records** under the Companies Act, 2013. What are the objectives of issuing Cost Accounting Standards (CAS)? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** From the following data of a manufacturing company, prepare a **Cost Sheet** and calculate the **profit per unit**, assuming 1,000 units were produced and sold during the period. **(7 Marks)** | Item | Amount (₹) | |---|---| | Raw Materials Consumed | 1,60,000 | | Direct Wages | 80,000 | | Direct Expenses | 10,000 | | Factory Overheads (60% fixed) | 70,000 | | Office Overheads | 30,000 | | Selling Overheads (per unit ₹15) | 15,000 | | Sales | 4,50,000 | --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Statutory Provisions & CAS (7 Marks)** **Statutory Framework (4 Marks):** 1. **Section 148 of the Companies Act, 2013 (1 Mark):** Empowers the Central Government to direct classes of companies to maintain cost records and get them audited. Applies to companies in production, manufacturing, mining, or electricity generation exceeding the prescribed turnover thresholds. 2. **Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules, 2014 (1 Mark):** Prescribe the specific **Table of Industries and Sectors** (Tables A, B, C, D) that come under the purview. Thresholds are periodically revised by notification. 3. **Cost Audit (1 Mark):** Audit is conducted by a **Cost Accountant in Practice (CMA)** who is not in the employment of the company. The audit report is filed with the Central Government in the prescribed form (CRA-3). 4. **Penalty for Non-Compliance (1 Mark):** Section 148(8) prescribes a fine of ₹25,000 to ₹5,00,000 on the company and every defaulting officer for failure to maintain cost records or file the audit report. **Objectives of Cost Accounting Standards (3 Marks — 1 each):** 1. **Uniformity** — To bring consistency in cost accounting principles and treatment across different companies and industries. 2. **Comparability** — To enable stakeholders to compare cost data of different organisations for benchmarking and decision-making. 3. **Reliability & Transparency** — To enhance the credibility of cost information disclosed to investors, regulators, and tax authorities. 4. **Statutory Compliance** — To support the framework laid down under Section 148 and the associated Rules. --- **Part (b) — Cost Sheet with Profit per Unit (7 Marks)** **Given:** Units produced and sold = 1,000; Selling Overheads per unit = ₹15 → Total Selling Overheads = ₹15,000 ✓ **Step 1 — Prime Cost (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Prime Cost} &= 1{,}60{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 10{,}000 \\ &= 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Works Cost (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Works Cost} &= 2{,}50{,}000 + 70{,}000 \\ &= 3{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Cost of Production (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost of Production} &= 3{,}20{,}000 + 30{,}000 \\ &= 3{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ > *Note: No WIP adjustment is given in the problem.* **Step 4 — Total Cost (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Cost} &= 3{,}50{,}000 + 15{,}000 \\ &= 3{,}65{,}000 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Profit and Profit per Unit (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Profit} &= 4{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}65{,}000 = 85{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\ \text{Profit per Unit} &= \dfrac{85{,}000}{1{,}000} = 85 \text{ ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Verification via Cost per Unit (1 Mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost per Unit} &= \dfrac{3{,}65{,}000}{1{,}000} = 365 \text{ ₹} \\ \text{Selling Price per Unit} &= \dfrac{4{,}50{,}000}{1{,}000} = 450 \text{ ₹} \\ \text{Profit per Unit} &= 450 - 365 = 85 \text{ ₹} \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Wrong WIP sign convention** — Opening WIP is *added*, Closing WIP is *deducted*. Reversing this costs 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing "Administrative Overheads" with "Selling Overheads"** — Office salaries, rent of head office, audit fees = *Administrative*. Sales commission, freight outwards, advertising = *Selling & Distribution*. > 3. **Ignoring Opening/Closing Finished Goods stock** — Many students jump from Cost of Production directly to Total Cost. Always pass through the FG adjustment for the **Cost of Goods Sold** subtotal. > 4. **Profit % denominator** — When the question says "profit on cost," use Cost as denominator; for "profit on sales," use Sales. Mixing this up loses ½ mark. > 5. **Forgetting to show formulas** — KTU follows step-marking. A correct final answer without intermediate steps may fetch only 5 out of 7. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Cost Accounting Records** = the documentary infrastructure (vouchers, registers, ledgers, statements) that captures all cost-relevant transactions. - They serve **three masters**: statutory compliance (Companies Act 2013), management information, and cost audit. - The **cost sheet** is built as a **logical pyramid**: Direct Materials + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses = Prime Cost → add Manufacturing Overheads = Works Cost → add Administrative Overheads ± WIP = Cost of Production → add Selling & Distribution Overheads = Total Cost. - WIP and FG adjustments are **non-negotiable** in any cost sheet that covers a time period. - **Cost Accounting Standards (CAS)** issued by ICAI-CMA bring uniformity in classification and treatment of costs. - For KTU exams, **always show formulas first, then substitute values, then state the result in words** (e.g., "Hence, Prime Cost is ₹3,70,000"). - **Profit per unit** is computed only after deducting the *full* Total Cost (including selling overheads), not just the Cost of Production. - The **statutory audit** under Section 148 is conducted by an *external* Cost Accountant in Practice (CMA), not by the company's own staff. - **Good cost records are:** Accurate, Simple, Timely, Complete, Comparable, and Verifiable. - The **"Cost Tower"** (Diagram in Section 4.2) is the visual representation of cost build-up — a high-yield drawing for 7-mark theory questions.

topics.title: Ledgers and Cost Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ledgers and Cost Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ledgers in Cost Accounting — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ledger (as per KTU 2024 Scheme terminology):** A **Ledger** is a principal book of account in which all the transactions recorded in the journal are classified and posted under appropriate account heads, in a summarized form, on a chronological or analytical basis. In cost accounting, the ledger is divided into multiple specialized sub-ledgers to maintain segregation of cost elements.

In a cost accounting system, instead of maintaining a single combined ledger (as in financial accounting), the business maintains an **Integrated Accounting System** consisting of:
- **Cost Ledger** (for cost accounts)
- **Financial Ledger** (for financial accounts)
- **Cost Control Accounts** (to reconcile the two)

### Types of Specialized Cost Ledgers

| S.No. | Ledger | Function |
|-------|--------|----------|
| 1 | **Stores Ledger** | Records receipt, issue, and balance of materials |
| 2 | **Wages/Salaries Ledger** | Records labour cost allocation to jobs/processes |
| 3 | **Manufacturing/Production Overhead Ledger** | Captures indirect factory expenses |
| 4 | **Administration Overhead Ledger** | Records office and administrative expenses |
| 5 | **Selling & Distribution Overhead Ledger** | Records marketing, selling, and delivery costs |
| 6 | **Cost Ledger (Control)** | Master ledger that holds control accounts of all cost elements |

## 1.2 Cost Statement — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Statement (Cost Sheet):** A **Cost Statement** is a document that presents a systematic, classified, and analytical summary of the various elements of cost incurred in producing a product or providing a service, arranged in a logical sequence from the most direct to the most comprehensive cost figure, ultimately arriving at the cost per unit and profit.

It is prepared at three levels:
- **Cost Sheet for a Job / Batch / Contract**
- **Cost Sheet for a Process**
- **Cost Sheet for a Period (Operating Costing)**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **bakery preparing a wedding cake**:
> - The **Stores Ledger** is like the **pantry register** — every kg of flour, sugar, eggs going in and out is noted.
> - The **Wages Ledger** is the **baker's timesheet** — how many hours the chef worked on the cake and what he was paid.
> - The **Manufacturing Overhead Ledger** is the **electricity bill for the oven** and the rent for the kitchen — costs that helped bake the cake but aren't directly traceable to one cake.
> - The **Selling Ledger** is the **delivery van expense and advertising cost** for the wedding.
> - The **Cost Statement** is the **final invoice breakup** given to the customer showing: ingredients (materials) + chef's time (labour) + oven fuel (overheads) + delivery (selling) + bakery's margin (profit).

> The ledger **records**, the cost statement **summarizes and presents** — together they form the heart of cost management.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

- **Prime Cost** — the most direct cost of manufacture
- **Works Cost / Factory Cost** — cost up to factory gate
- **Cost of Production** — total cost of producing goods ready for sale
- **Cost of Goods Sold** — cost of goods actually sold (after stock adjustment)
- **Cost of Sales** — total cost incurred to effect sales
- **Profit** — Sales minus Cost of Sales
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Flow of Cost Build-up (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cost statement is built in a **pyramidal, layer-by-layer** fashion. Each layer **absorbs** the previous layer's total and **adds a new category of cost**. This is the most critical conceptual flow tested in KTU examinations.

**Layer 1 — Direct / Prime Inputs (the most traceable costs):**
- **Direct Material** → materials that can be identified and measured per unit (e.g., steel in a car, flour in bread)
- **Direct Labour / Wages** → wages paid to workers who physically convert raw material into finished product
- **Direct / Chargeable Expenses** → expenses directly attributable to manufacture (e.g., hire of a special machine for one job)

**Layer 2 — Factory / Works Cost (adds factory overheads):**
- Add: **Manufacturing / Factory Overheads** (indirect material, indirect labour, factory rent, depreciation, factory power, supervisor salary)

**Layer 3 — Cost of Production (adds office overheads):**
- Add: **Administration Overheads** (office salaries, director fees, postage, stationery, legal expenses, audit fees)

**Layer 4 — Cost of Goods Sold (adds stock adjustment):**
- Add: **Opening Stock of Finished Goods**
- Less: **Closing Stock of Finished Goods**

**Layer 5 — Cost of Sales (adds selling expenses):**
- Add: **Selling & Distribution Overheads** (advertising, salesmen commission, packing, carriage outward, bad debts)
- Add: **Non-Operating Expenses / Add:** (interest paid, discount allowed)

**Layer 6 — Profit (final layer):**
- Add: **Net Profit** (target / actual)

**The Reverse gives:** **Sales / Total Revenue**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving every cost sheet problem in KTU examinations. Memorize the structure, not just the formula.

| # | Cost Element | Formula | Unit / Output |
|---|--------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Prime Cost (PC)** | $PC = DM + DL + DExp$ | ₹ / total batch |
| 2 | **Works / Factory Cost (WC)** | $WC = PC + FOH$ | ₹ / total batch |
| 3 | **Cost of Production (CP)** | $CP = WC + AOH$ | ₹ / total batch |
| 4 | **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** | $COGS = CP + OS_{FG} - CS_{FG}$ | ₹ / total batch |
| 5 | **Cost of Sales (CS)** | $CS = COGS + S\&DOH + NOExp$ | ₹ / total batch |
| 6 | **Sales (S)** | $S = CS + Profit$ | ₹ / total batch |
| 7 | **Cost per Unit** | $Cost/Unit = Total\ Cost / Total\ Units$ | ₹ / unit |
| 8 | **Profit as % of Sales** | $P\% = (Profit / Sales) \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 9 | **Profit as % of Cost** | $P\% = (Profit / CS) \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 10 | **Sales from % Profit on Cost** | $Sales = CS \times (1 + P\%/100)$ | ₹ |
| 11 | **Sales from % Profit on Sales** | $Sales = CS / (1 - P\%/100)$ | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend:** DM = Direct Material, DL = Direct Labour, DExp = Direct Expenses, FOH = Factory Overheads, AOH = Administration Overheads, OS_FG = Opening Stock of Finished Goods, CS_FG = Closing Stock of Finished Goods, S&DO H = Selling & Distribution Overheads, NOExp = Non-Operating Expenses.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industries** (automobile, electronics, textiles): Use cost sheets to determine break-even pricing and to compare actual vs. standard cost.
- **Construction / Civil Engineering:** Cost sheet for a contract helps in tendering and progress billing.
- **Process Industries** (chemicals, cement): Cost sheets by process help in process improvement and waste reduction.
- **Service Sector** (software firms, airlines): Use unit costing to determine service cost per delivery hour or per ticket.
- **Decision-Making:** Forms the basis for **make-or-buy decisions**, **price determination**, **cost control**, and **budgeting**.

## 2.4 Key Concept: Relationship between Cost Sheet and Ledgers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Each **element of cost** in the cost sheet is the **summary of a corresponding ledger**.
> - Example: The "Direct Material" figure in the cost sheet is the **total of all direct material postings** in the Stores Ledger for the period.
> - Ledgers = **Detailed Recording** | Cost Statement = **Final Aggregated Presentation**.
- This is why KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 places both topics together — they are **two sides of the same coin**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of the Cost Build-up Equation

Let us derive the **master cost equation** by sequentially adding layers.

**Step 1: Prime Cost Equation**

The most direct cost involves only those items that can be **physically and economically identified** with the product. These are direct material, direct labour, and direct expenses.

$$
PC = DM + DL + DExp
$$

**Step 2: Works Cost (Factory Cost) Equation**

Factory overheads (also called manufacturing or works overheads) are the **indirect costs** incurred inside the factory boundary — from receiving stores to finished goods godown — that cannot be directly traced to a unit but are essential for production.

$$
WC = PC + FOH
$$

**Step 3: Cost of Production Equation**

Administration overheads are the costs of running the **business office** — they do not relate to manufacturing but are necessary for the business to exist. They are added to reach the full cost of producing the goods (whether sold or held in stock).

$$
CP = WC + AOH
$$

**Step 4: Cost of Goods Sold Equation**

Not all goods produced are sold in the same period. The opening stock of finished goods (produced last period, sold this period) is **added**, and the closing stock of finished goods (produced this period, unsold) is **deducted**.

$$
COGS = CP + OS_{FG} - CS_{FG}
$$

**Step 5: Cost of Sales Equation**

Selling and distribution overheads (advertising, sales commission, delivery, after-sales service) plus non-operating expenses (discount allowed, interest paid) bring us to the final cost of making the sale.

$$
CS = COGS + S\&DOH + NOExp
$$

**Step 6: Master Cost-Plus-Profit Equation**

Sales revenue must cover the cost of sales and leave a profit margin.

$$
Sales = CS + Profit
$$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Profit Conversion Formulas

**Case A: Given Profit as % of Sales, find Sales from Cost**

Let $P$ be the profit and $S$ be the sales. We are given:

$$
\frac{P}{S} = \frac{p}{100}
$$

From the cost equation, $P = S - CS$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{S - CS}{S} = \frac{p}{100}
$$

$$
1 - \frac{CS}{S} = \frac{p}{100}
$$

$$
\frac{CS}{S} = 1 - \frac{p}{100} = \frac{100 - p}{100}
$$

$$
S = CS \times \frac{100}{100 - p}
$$

**Case B: Given Profit as % of Cost, find Sales from Cost**

Given $\dfrac{P}{CS} = \dfrac{p}{100}$, so $P = CS \times \dfrac{p}{100}$.

$$
S = CS + P = CS + \frac{p}{100} CS = CS \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)
$$

$$
S = CS \times \frac{100 + p}{100}
$$

## 3.3 KTU-Style Analytical Matrix — Where Each Item Goes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects students to **classify items correctly** into the appropriate cost element. This table is the most common place where students lose marks.

| Item of Cost | Classification | Where in Cost Sheet |
|--------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Steel used in car body | Direct Material | Direct Material |
| Wages to assembly line worker | Direct Labour | Direct Labour |
| Salary of factory supervisor | Indirect Labour | Factory Overhead |
| Factory rent | Indirect | Factory Overhead |
| Director's fees | Indirect | Administration Overhead |
| Office stationery | Indirect | Administration Overhead |
| Carriage outward (delivery to customer) | Selling | Selling & Distribution Overhead |
| Carriage inward (raw material to factory) | Direct/Indirect Material handling | Direct Material / Factory OH |
| Advertisement | Selling | Selling & Distribution Overhead |
| Discount allowed | Non-operating | Below COGS (Cost of Sales) |
| Discount received | Non-operating income | Deducted from cost (negative expense) |
| Salesmen commission | Selling | Selling & Distribution Overhead |
| Bad debts | Selling | Selling & Distribution Overhead |
| Depreciation of plant & machinery | Indirect | Factory Overhead |
| Depreciation of office building | Indirect | Administration Overhead |
| Interest on loan taken for factory | Indirect | Factory Overhead |
| Interest on loan for general business | Non-operating | Cost of Sales |
| Packing — primary (for product) | Direct Material | Direct Material |
| Packing — secondary (for transit) | Selling | Selling & Distribution Overhead |
| Customs duty on raw material | Direct | Direct Material |

## 3.4 Numerical Example — KTU Standard Style

**Problem:** A manufacturing company provides the following data for the year ended 31-03-2025:
- Raw material consumed: ₹ 5,00,000
- Direct wages: ₹ 3,00,000
- Direct expenses: ₹ 1,00,000
- Factory overheads: ₹ 2,00,000
- Administration overheads: ₹ 1,50,000
- Selling & distribution overheads: ₹ 1,20,000
- Opening stock of finished goods: ₹ 80,000
- Closing stock of finished goods: ₹ 70,000
- Profit: 25% on sales

**Required:** Prepare a Cost Statement and calculate (a) Cost per unit if 1,000 units were produced, (b) Sales value, (c) Profit.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Prime Cost**

$$
PC = 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Works Cost**

$$
WC = 9{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Cost of Production**

$$
CP = 11{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 12{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Cost of Goods Sold**

$$
COGS = 12{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 - 70{,}000 = 12{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Cost of Sales**

$$
CS = 12{,}60{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 = 13{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Sales (using formula S = CS × 100/(100-p))**

$$
Sales = 13{,}80{,}000 \times \frac{100}{100 - 25} = 13{,}80{,}000 \times \frac{100}{75}
$$

$$
Sales = 18{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 7: Profit**

$$
Profit = Sales - CS = 18{,}40{,}000 - 13{,}80{,}000 = 4{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 8: Cost per Unit**

$$
Cost/Unit = \frac{13{,}80{,}000}{1{,}000} = ₹\ 1{,}380\ per\ unit
$$

$$
Sales/Unit = \frac{18{,}40{,}000}{1{,}000} = ₹\ 1{,}840\ per\ unit
$$

$$
Profit/Unit = ₹\ 460\ per\ unit
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Verification: Profit % on sales = (4,60,000 / 18,40,000) × 100 = **25%** ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of Cost Ledgers (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SourceA[Source Documents: Materials, Wages, Invoices, GRNs, Time Cards]
    SourceB[Source Documents: Utility Bills, Rent Receipts, Depreciation Schedules]
    SourceC[Source Documents: Sales Invoices, Delivery Notes, Ad Bills]
    SourceD[Source Documents: Office Expenses, Salary Slips, Audit Bills]
    SL1[Stores Ledger - Material Account]
    WL1[Wages Ledger - Labour Cost Account]
    FOHL[Factory Overhead Ledger]
    AOHL[Administration Overhead Ledger]
    SDOHL[Selling and Distribution Overhead Ledger]
    CCA[Cost Ledger Control Account]
    FLA[Financial Ledger Adjustment Account]
    FS[Financial Accounts: Profit and Loss Account]
    CS[Cost Statement / Cost Sheet]
    RP[Reports to Management]
    
    SourceA --> SL1
    SourceA --> WL1
    SourceB --> FOHL
    SourceD --> AOHL
    SourceC --> SDOHL
    SL1 --> CCA
    WL1 --> CCA
    FOHL --> CCA
    AOHL --> CCA
    SDOHL --> CCA
    CCA --> CS
    CCA --> FLA
    FLA --> FS
    CS --> RP
    FLA --> RP
```

## 4.2 Cost Statement — Pyramidal Layered Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Sales / Total Revenue]
    L1[Layer 6: Profit]
    L2[Layer 5: Cost of Sales]
    L3[Layer 4: Cost of Goods Sold]
    L4[Layer 3: Cost of Production]
    L5[Layer 2: Works Cost or Factory Cost]
    L6[Layer 1: Prime Cost]
    L7[Direct Material + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses]
    
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
```

## 4.3 Inter-Ledger Posting Flow (Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Purchases of Material] --> B[Stores Ledger - Debit Entry]
    C[Issue of Material] --> D[Stores Ledger - Credit Entry]
    D --> E[Work in Progress Account - Debit]
    F[Labour Engaged] --> G[Wages Ledger - Credit]
    G --> E
    H[Factory Overheads Incurred] --> I[Factory OH Ledger - Debit]
    I --> E
    E --> J[Finished Goods - Debit]
    K[Sold Units] --> J
    J --> L[Cost of Sales - Debit]
    L --> M[Profit and Loss Summary]
```

## 4.4 Reconciliation Bridge — Cost Ledger to Financial Ledger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cost Ledger Totals] --> B[Cost Control Account]
    C[Financial Ledger Totals] --> D[Financial Control Account]
    B --> E[Reconciliation Statement]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Adjusted Profit per Financial Books]
    E --> G[Profit per Cost Accounts]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** The reconciliation step is a statutory requirement for companies maintaining cost records (under the Companies (Cost Records and Audit) Rules). The difference between the two profits is called the **Reconciliation Difference** and arises mainly due to items of pure financial nature (e.g., income from investments) and items of pure cost nature (e.g., notional rent of owned premises).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define a Ledger and list the various subsidiary ledgers maintained under a Cost Accounting system.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Ledger** is a book of final entry where all journal transactions are classified and posted under appropriate account heads in a summarized form. In cost accounting, the following subsidiary ledgers are maintained:
1. **Stores Ledger** — for materials
2. **Wages Ledger** — for labour
3. **Manufacturing/Factory Overhead Ledger**
4. **Administration Overhead Ledger**
5. **Selling & Distribution Overhead Ledger**
6. **Cost Ledger Control** — master summary

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Distinguish between Prime Cost and Works Cost.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Prime Cost | Works Cost |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Formula** | DM + DL + DExp | Prime Cost + Factory Overheads |
| **Scope** | Direct/identifiable costs only | Direct + Indirect factory costs |
| **Stage** | First stage of cost buildup | Second stage |
| **Includes overheads?** | No | Yes (Factory only) |
| **Useful for** | Quoting direct cost | Pricing at factory gate |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never confuse** the two profit percentages. *"25% on cost"* means $P = 0.25 \times CS$, but *"25% on sales"* means $P = 0.25 \times S$. The numerical answer changes drastically.
> - **Do NOT add** opening and closing stock at the same level. Opening stock is **added** to Cost of Production, closing stock is **deducted**.
> - **Classify carefully**: Carriage inward goes to **Direct Material / Factory OH**, but Carriage outward (to customer) goes to **Selling OH**.
> - **Depreciation on office building** = Administration OH, **NOT** Factory OH.
> - Always **show the formula used** for sales/profit conversion — examiners award step marks for this.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**The following data relate to M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd. for the year ended 31-03-2024:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Raw materials purchased | 6,00,000 |
| Opening stock of raw materials | 80,000 |
| Closing stock of raw materials | 60,000 |
| Wages (60% direct, 40% indirect) | 5,00,000 |
| Factory rent & taxes | 1,20,000 |
| Depreciation on plant & machinery | 80,000 |
| Office salaries | 90,000 |
| Printing & stationery (office) | 10,000 |
| Advertisement expenses | 70,000 |
| Salesmen commission | 50,000 |
| Carriage outward | 30,000 |
| Opening stock of finished goods | 1,20,000 |
| Closing stock of finished goods | 90,000 |
| Units produced | 5,000 |
| Profit | 20% on sales |

**Required:**
(a) Prepare a **Cost Statement** showing Prime Cost, Works Cost, Cost of Production, Cost of Goods Sold, and Cost of Sales. (7 marks)
(b) Calculate **Cost per unit, Sales per unit, and Profit per unit**. Also find total Sales value. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Direct Material Consumed**
$$
DM = Opening\ Stock + Purchases - Closing\ Stock
$$
$$
DM = 80{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 6{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Direct Wages**
$$
DL = 60\% \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Prime Cost** (no direct expenses mentioned, so DExp = 0)
$$
PC = 6{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 0 = 9{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Factory Overheads**
- Indirect wages = 40% × 5,00,000 = ₹ 2,00,000
- Factory rent = ₹ 1,20,000
- Depreciation on plant = ₹ 80,000
- Total FOH = ₹ 4,00,000

**Step 5: Works Cost**
$$
WC = 9{,}20{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 13{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Administration Overheads**
- Office salaries = ₹ 90,000
- Printing & stationery = ₹ 10,000
- Total AOH = ₹ 1,00,000

**Step 7: Cost of Production**
$$
CP = 13{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 8: Cost of Goods Sold**
$$
COGS = 14{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 - 90{,}000 = 14{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 9: Selling & Distribution Overheads**
- Advertisement = ₹ 70,000
- Salesmen commission = ₹ 50,000
- Carriage outward = ₹ 30,000
- Total SDOH = ₹ 1,50,000

**Step 10: Cost of Sales**
$$
CS = 14{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 11: Sales Value** (20% profit on sales means $S = CS \times 100 / (100 - 20)$)
$$
S = 16{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{100}{80} = 20{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 12: Profit**
$$
Profit = 20{,}00{,}000 - 16{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 13: Per Unit**
$$
Cost/Unit = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = ₹\ 320
$$
$$
Sales/Unit = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = ₹\ 400
$$
$$
Profit/Unit = ₹\ 80
$$

**Final Cost Statement Format (Expected in Answer Sheet):**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) | Total (₹) |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| Opening Stock of Raw Material | 80,000 | |
| Add: Purchases | 6,00,000 | |
| Less: Closing Stock of Raw Material | (60,000) | |
| **Raw Material Consumed** | | 6,20,000 |
| Direct Wages | | 3,00,000 |
| **PRIME COST** | | **9,20,000** |
| Add: Factory Overheads | | 4,00,000 |
| **WORKS COST** | | **13,20,000** |
| Add: Administration Overheads | | 1,00,000 |
| **COST OF PRODUCTION** | | **14,20,000** |
| Add: Opening Stock of Finished Goods | | 1,20,000 |
| Less: Closing Stock of Finished Goods | | (90,000) |
| **COST OF GOODS SOLD** | | **14,50,000** |
| Add: Selling & Distribution OH | | 1,50,000 |
| **COST OF SALES** | | **16,00,000** |
| Add: Profit (20% on Sales) | | 4,00,000 |
| **SALES** | | **20,00,000** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Question]

**From the following particulars of a manufacturing unit, prepare a Cost Sheet for the year ended 31-03-2025:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Direct Material | 4,00,000 |
| Direct Labour | 2,50,000 |
| Direct Expenses | 50,000 |
| Factory Overheads (50% of Prime Cost) | ? |
| Administration Overheads (25% of Works Cost) | ? |
| Selling & Distribution Overheads | 1,00,000 |
| Opening Stock of Finished Goods | 60,000 |
| Closing Stock of Finished Goods | 40,000 |
| Profit | 20% on Cost |
| Units produced | 2,000 |

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the missing Factory Overheads and Administration Overheads, then prepare the **Cost Statement**. (7 marks)
(b) Compute **Total Sales, Cost per unit, Sales per unit, and Profit per unit**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Prime Cost**
$$
PC = 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Factory Overheads (50% of Prime Cost)**
$$
FOH = 50\% \times 7{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Works Cost**
$$
WC = 7{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 = 10{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Administration Overheads (25% of Works Cost)**
$$
AOH = 25\% \times 10{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}62{,}500
$$

**Step 5: Cost of Production**
$$
CP = 10{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}62{,}500 = 13{,}12{,}500
$$

**Step 6: Cost of Goods Sold**
$$
COGS = 13{,}12{,}500 + 60{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 13{,}32{,}500
$$

**Step 7: Cost of Sales**
$$
CS = 13{,}32{,}500 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}32{,}500
$$

**Step 8: Sales (20% on cost means $S = CS \times (1 + 0.20)$)**
$$
S = 14{,}32{,}500 \times 1.20 = 17{,}19{,}000
$$

**Step 9: Profit**
$$
Profit = 17{,}19{,}000 - 14{,}32{,}500 = 2{,}86{,}500
$$

**Step 10: Per Unit (2,000 units)**
$$
Cost/Unit = \frac{14{,}32{,}500}{2{,}000} = ₹\ 716.25
$$
$$
Sales/Unit = \frac{17{,}19{,}000}{2{,}000} = ₹\ 859.50
$$
$$
Profit/Unit = ₹\ 143.25
$$

**Verification:** Profit % on cost = (2,86,500 / 14,32,500) × 100 = **20%** ✓

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Board Exam Ready):**

- **Definition:** A **Ledger** records classified transactions; a **Cost Statement** aggregates them into a structured cost presentation.
- **Subsidiary ledgers** in cost accounting: Stores, Wages, Factory OH, Admin OH, Selling & Dist OH, and Cost Ledger Control.
- **The cost buildup pyramid (must memorize in order):** Prime Cost → Works Cost → Cost of Production → COGS → Cost of Sales → Sales.
- **Prime Cost = DM + DL + DExp** (most direct, no overheads).
- **Works Cost = PC + FOH** (cost up to factory gate).
- **Cost of Production = WC + AOH** (cost of all goods made, sold or not).
- **COGS = CP + Opening Stock FG − Closing Stock FG**.
- **Cost of Sales = COGS + Selling & Dist OH ± Non-operating items**.
- **Sales = CS + Profit** — and the profit percentage changes the formula used.
- **Profit on Sales formula:** $S = CS \times 100/(100-p)$.
- **Profit on Cost formula:** $S = CS \times (1 + p/100)$.
- **Classification traps:** Carriage inward ≠ Carriage outward; depreciation of office building ≠ plant; packing for transit ≠ packing for product; interest on factory loan ≠ general loan.
- **Stock treatment:** Opening stock is **added**, closing stock is **deducted** (for finished goods); for raw materials, opening stock is added and closing stock is deducted within the material consumed block itself.
