
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the **hierarchical taxonomy** of all ratio types as per KTU Module 2 syllabus.
> - The second diagram depicts the **input-output flow**: Raw financial statements → Computation Engine → Categorized Ratios → Managerial Decisions.
> - The third diagram serves as a **diagnostic decision tree** used by analysts to interpret what a computed ratio *actually means* in business terms.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. (a) [3 Marks] Define Ratio Analysis. List any four limitations of Ratio Analysis.**

**Model Answer:**
Ratio Analysis is a technique of financial statement analysis that establishes quantitative relationships between two related accounting variables to evaluate the financial health and operational performance of a business.

**Limitations:**
1. **Lack of Standardized Benchmarks:** Ideal ratios vary across industries; a single benchmark cannot be universally applied.
2. **Ignores Price-Level Changes:** Ratios are based on historical costs and do not adjust for inflation.
3. **Window Dressing:** Companies can manipulate Balance Sheet items (e.g., advance collection of receivables) to inflate ratios before reporting dates.
4. **Qualitative Blindness:** Ratios ignore non-financial factors such as management quality, brand reputation, and labor relations.

---

**Q1. (b) [3 Marks] Distinguish between Current Ratio and Quick Ratio.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Current Ratio | Quick Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| **Numerator** | All Current Assets | Quick Assets (excluding Inventory & Prepaid Expenses) |
| **Strictness** | Less strict | More strict (Acid Test) |
| **Ideal Standard** | **2 : 1** | **1 : 1** |
| **Purpose** | Tests general short-term solvency | Tests *immediate* liquidity excluding inventory |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**From the following Balance Sheet of XYZ Engineering Ltd. as on 31-03-2024, calculate: (a) Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Debt-Equity Ratio, and Proprietary Ratio; (b) Inventory Turnover Ratio, Debtors Turnover Ratio, and Collection Period.**

| **Liabilities** | ₹ | **Assets** | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 5,00,000 | Land & Building | 3,00,000 |
| 12% Debentures | 2,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 4,00,000 |
| Creditors | 1,50,000 | Inventory | 2,00,000 |
| Bills Payable | 50,000 | Debtors | 1,50,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 1,00,000 | Cash in Hand | 50,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 1,00,000 |
| | **10,00,000** | | **10,00,000** |

**Additional Information:**
- Net Credit Sales for the year = ₹12,00,000
- Cost of Goods Sold = ₹8,00,000

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — PART (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Identify Current Assets and Current Liabilities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Current\ Assets &= Inventory + Debtors + Cash\ in\ Hand + Cash\ at\ Bank \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Current\ Liabilities &= Creditors + Bills\ Payable + Outstanding\ Expenses \\
&= 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Current Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Current\ Ratio &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 1.667 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Quick Ratio** (Assuming Inventory is the only excluded item)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Quick\ Ratio &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 1 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Long-Term Debt and Equity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Long\text{-}Term\ Debt &= 12\%\ Debentures = 2{,}00{,}000 \\
Shareholders'\ Funds &= Equity\ Share\ Capital = 5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Debt-Equity Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Debt\text{-}Equity &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.4 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Proprietary Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Proprietary\ Ratio &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 0.5\ or\ 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — PART (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 7: Inventory Turnover Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Inventory\ Turnover &= \frac{COGS}{Inventory} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 4\ times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Debtors Turnover Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Debtors\ Turnover &= \frac{Net\ Credit\ Sales}{Debtors} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = 8\ times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Collection Period**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Collection\ Period &= \frac{365}{8} = 45.625\ days \approx 46\ days
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** XYZ Engineering Ltd. has a comfortable liquidity position (Current Ratio 1.67, Quick Ratio 1:1), a conservative capital structure (Debt-Equity 0.4:1), and collects receivables within ~46 days. The lower inventory turnover of 4 times indicates slower stock movement which needs managerial attention.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]

**From the following data relating to PQR Industries, calculate: (a) Gross Profit Ratio, Net Profit Ratio, Operating Profit Ratio, and Return on Investment; (b) Interest Coverage Ratio and Comment on the solvency position.**

| **Particulars** | ₹ |
|---|---|
| Net Sales | 20,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 14,00,000 |
| Operating Expenses | 2,00,000 |
| Interest Expense | 1,00,000 |
| Net Profit After Tax | 1,50,000 |
| Total Capital Employed | 10,00,000 |

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — PART (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Gross Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Gross\ Profit &= Net\ Sales - COGS = 20{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Gross Profit Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GP\ Ratio &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Net Profit Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NP\ Ratio &= \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 7.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Operating Profit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Operating\ Profit &= Net\ Sales - COGS - Operating\ Expenses \\
&= 20{,}00{,}000 - 14{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Operating Profit Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OP\ Ratio &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Earnings Before Interest & Tax (EBIT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EBIT &= Operating\ Profit + Interest\ Expense = 4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Return on Investment (ROI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \frac{EBIT}{Capital\ Employed} \times 100 = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — PART (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 8: Interest Coverage Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Interest\ Coverage &= \frac{EBIT}{Interest\ Expense} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 5\ times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Solvency Position:**
The Interest Coverage Ratio of **5 times** substantially exceeds the safety benchmark of **3 times**, indicating that PQR Industries comfortably meets its interest obligations from operating earnings — strong **long-term solvency position**. The firm is not over-leveraged and can absorb a moderate downturn in earnings without defaulting on debt servicing.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warnings:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract Operating Expenses** when computing Operating Profit — many students treat Operating Profit as merely Gross Profit. This is a **3-mark deduction trap**.
> 2. **Using Net Profit After Tax instead of EBIT** for ROI computation. Always check whether the formula uses *Net Profit Before Interest and Tax* (DuPont style) or *Net Profit After Tax* (Shareholder style).
> 3. **Misclassifying Bills Payable and Outstanding Expenses** — both are *Current Liabilities*, not long-term. Incorrect classification breaks the entire Current Ratio computation.
> 4. **Unit consistency:** Always express percentages with the **× 100** multiplier; otherwise, you will be marked down.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Ratio Analysis** transforms raw financial statements into standardized, comparable performance metrics.
- **Four categories:** Liquidity, Solvency, Activity, Profitability.
- **Current Ratio ideal = 2:1;** Quick Ratio ideal = **1:1**; Cash Ratio ideal = **0.5:1**.
- **Debt-Equity ideal ≤ 1:1** for most non-financial firms.
- **Interest Coverage ≥ 3 times** is the safety benchmark.
- **Inventory Turnover = COGS ÷ Average Inventory** (not Closing Inventory, unless specifically stated).
- **Debtors Turnover** uses **Net Credit Sales** (not Total Sales).
- **Collection Period = 365 ÷ Debtors Turnover.**
- **Gross Profit Ratio excludes Operating Expenses**; **Operating Profit Ratio** includes them.
- **ROI uses EBIT and Capital Employed;** **ROE uses Net Profit After Tax and Shareholders' Equity.**
- **DuPont Identity:** ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier.
- **Cross-Verification:** Always cross-check answers using the DuPont framework if both ROI and ROE are computed.
- **Limitations to mention** in theory answers: Window dressing, inflation effects, qualitative blindness, industry variability.
- **Examination Tip:** Always *show the formula first, then plug in values* — KTU examiners reward methodology clarity with step marks even if the final answer has a minor arithmetic slip.
- **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to convert ratios to **percentages** where required — Gross Profit, Net Profit, ROI, ROE all use **× 100**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computation and Interpretation of Financial Ratios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computation and Interpretation of Financial Ratios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Financial Ratios** are quantitative metrics derived from financial statements (Balance Sheet and Profit & Loss Account) used to evaluate the overall financial health, operational efficiency, liquidity position, solvency, and profitability of a business enterprise. They express the relationship between two accounting variables and are the cornerstone of **Financial Statement Analysis** under KTU 2024 scheme.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ratio analysis is a tool of financial statement analysis that simplifies complex financial data into meaningful comparable indicators, enabling stakeholders (management, investors, creditors, employees) to make informed economic decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine you are a doctor checking a patient's health. You don't just look at one number — you measure Blood Pressure, Heart Rate, Sugar Level, etc. Each measurement tells a small story, and together they reveal overall health.

In the same way, a single figure like "Sales = ₹50 Lakhs" tells us very little. But when we compare it to "Net Profit = ₹5 Lakhs", we get a **ratio (10%)** that tells a story — *"Out of every rupee of sales, the company keeps 10 paise as profit."*

**Financial Ratios are the "vital signs" of a company.**

### 1.3 Why Are Ratios Computed?

| Purpose | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Simplification** | Converts huge numbers (Crores) into manageable percentages |
| **Comparability** | Allows comparison across time (Intra-firm) and across companies (Inter-firm) |
| **Trend Analysis** | Identifies whether performance is improving, static, or deteriorating |
| **Decision Making** | Helps banks sanction loans, investors buy shares, and management strategize |
| **Identifying Weakness** | Pinpoints problem areas like over-stocking, slow collection, or excessive debt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standards and Benchmarks (KTU 2024 Context):** Although there are no universal "ideal" ratios, generally accepted benchmarks (industry standards) under Indian context are:
> - Current Ratio ideal range: **1.5 to 2 : 1**
> - Quick Ratio ideal range: **1 : 1**
> - Debt-Equity Ratio ideal: **2 : 1** (varies by industry)

### 1.4 Classification of Financial Ratios (KTU 2024 Module 2 Framework)

Financial ratios are broadly classified into **four major categories**:

1. **Liquidity Ratios** — Short-term solvency (ability to pay current liabilities)
2. **Solvency/Leverage Ratios** — Long-term solvency (ability to pay long-term liabilities)
3. **Activity/Turnover Ratios** — Efficiency of asset utilization
4. **Profitability Ratios** — Overall performance and earnings capability
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Liquidity Ratios (Short-Term Solvency)

Liquidity means the ability of the firm to meet its **short-term obligations** (due within 1 year) using its current assets. Three ratios are important:

#### (a) Current Ratio
- **Why:** To know whether the firm has enough current assets to cover current liabilities.
- **Logic:** Current Assets are converted to cash within 1 year; Current Liabilities are due within 1 year. So we compare them.

#### (b) Quick (Acid Test) Ratio
- **Why:** Inventory is the *least liquid* current asset. To find *instant* payment capacity excluding inventory.
- **Logic:** Quick Assets = Current Assets − Inventory − Prepaid Expenses

#### (c) Absolute Liquid Ratio (Cash Ratio)
- **Why:** To find the most conservative liquidity measure using only cash and cash equivalents.
- **Logic:** Most stringent test; ignores even receivables.

### 2.2 Solvency Ratios (Long-Term Solvency / Leverage)

Solvency means the ability of the firm to meet its **long-term obligations**.

#### (a) Debt-Equity Ratio
- **Why:** Measures the relative contribution of creditors vs. owners in financing assets.
- **Logic:** A ratio of 2:1 means for every ₹1 of equity, there is ₹2 of debt.

#### (b) Debt-Total Assets Ratio
- **Why:** Shows what proportion of total assets is financed by debt.
- **Logic:** Useful for banks to assess risk.

#### (c) Proprietary Ratio
- **Why:** Shows the proportion of total assets financed by shareholders' funds.
- **Logic:** Higher proprietary ratio = lower risk.

#### (d) Interest Coverage Ratio
- **Why:** Measures the firm's ability to pay interest expenses from operating profit.
- **Logic:** Higher the ratio, safer it is for lenders.

### 2.3 Activity (Turnover) Ratios

These ratios measure how efficiently the firm uses its assets.

#### (a) Inventory Turnover Ratio
- **Why:** Shows how many times inventory is sold and replaced.
- **Logic:** Higher = faster movement, less storage cost.

#### (b) Debtors / Receivables Turnover Ratio
- **Why:** Measures the speed of collecting cash from credit customers.
- **Logic:** Lower = slow collection = bad for liquidity.

#### (c) Creditors / Payables Turnover Ratio
- **Why:** Measures the speed of paying off suppliers.
- **Logic:** Too high = may damage supplier relationships.

#### (d) Fixed Assets Turnover Ratio
- **Why:** Measures the efficiency of fixed asset utilization.

#### (e) Working Capital Turnover Ratio
- **Why:** Measures efficiency of working capital usage.

### 2.4 Profitability Ratios

These measure the firm's ability to generate profits.

#### (a) Gross Profit Ratio
- **Why:** Indicates efficiency of production and pricing policy.

#### (b) Net Profit Ratio
- **Why:** Indicates overall profitability after all expenses.

#### (c) Operating Profit Ratio
- **Why:** Shows profit from core operations (excludes non-operating income).

#### (d) Return on Investment (ROI) / Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)
- **Why:** Measures return generated on total capital employed.

#### (e) Return on Equity (ROE)
- **Why:** Measures return generated for shareholders.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the following table, all absolute value bars are replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| **Category** | **Ratio Name** | **Formula** | **Ideal Benchmark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liquidity** | Current Ratio | $\frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | 2:1 |
| | Quick Ratio | $\frac{\text{Quick Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ or $\frac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid}}{\text{CL}}$ | 1:1 |
| | Absolute Liquid Ratio | $\frac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | 0.5:1 |
| **Solvency** | Debt-Equity Ratio | $\frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}$ | 2:1 |
| | Debt-Total Assets | $\frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | $\lt$ 0.5 |
| | Proprietary Ratio | $\frac{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Higher better |
| | Interest Coverage | $\frac{\text{EBIT (Operating Profit)}}{\text{Interest Charges}}$ | 3 to 4 times |
| **Activity** | Inventory Turnover | $\frac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Higher better |
| | Debtors Turnover | $\frac{\text{Net Credit Sales}}{\text{Average Debtors}}$ | Higher better |
| | Creditors Turnover | $\frac{\text{Net Credit Purchases}}{\text{Average Creditors}}$ | Moderate |
| | Fixed Assets Turnover | $\frac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Net Fixed Assets}}$ | Higher better |
| | Working Capital Turnover | $\frac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Net Working Capital}}$ | Higher better |
| **Profitability** | Gross Profit Ratio | $\frac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry specific |
| | Net Profit Ratio | $\frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Higher better |
| | Operating Profit Ratio | $\frac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Higher better |
| | ROCE | $\frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100$ | Higher better |
| | ROE | $\frac{\text{Net Profit after Tax}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100$ | Higher better |
| | EPS | $\frac{\text{Net Profit after Tax}}{\text{Number of Equity Shares}}$ | Higher better |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Application
In an **engineering firm**, ratios are vital:
- An **EPC contractor** uses Current Ratio to assess working capital before bidding a project.
- A **manufacturing unit** uses Inventory Turnover to optimize production cycles.
- A **consulting firm** uses ROCE to decide on new equipment investments.
- Banks use **Debt-Equity Ratio** before sanctioning term loans for plant setup.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Solved Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Solved Example 1: Computation of All Major Ratios

**Problem Statement:**

From the following Balance Sheet and Profit & Loss Account of M/s Apex Engineering Ltd., compute the following ratios for the year ending 31-03-2024:
(a) Current Ratio
(b) Quick Ratio
(c) Debt-Equity Ratio
(d) Inventory Turnover Ratio
(e) Gross Profit Ratio
(f) Net Profit Ratio
(g) ROCE
(h) Interest Coverage Ratio

**Balance Sheet as on 31-03-2024:**

| **Liabilities** | **₹** | **Assets** | **₹** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 5,00,000 | Land & Building | 4,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 2,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 3,00,000 |
| Creditors | 1,50,000 | Stock (Inventory) | 1,50,000 |
| Bills Payable | 50,000 | Debtors | 1,00,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 30,000 | Cash in Hand | 20,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 60,000 |
| | | Prepaid Expenses | 20,000 |
| | | Patents | 50,000 |
| | | Goodwill | 1,00,000 |
| **Total** | **9,30,000** | **Total** | **12,00,000** |

> [!NOTE]
> Discrepancy in question: Totals do not match. We will proceed assuming the Balance Sheet total on Asset side is **₹12,00,000** and adjust Equity Share Capital to **₹8,00,000** for academic solution.

**Adjusted Balance Sheet:**

| **Liabilities** | **₹** | **Assets** | **₹** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 8,00,000 | Land & Building | 4,00,000 |
| 10% Debentures | 2,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 3,00,000 |
| Creditors | 1,50,000 | Stock (Inventory) | 1,50,000 |
| Bills Payable | 50,000 | Debtors | 1,00,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 30,000 | Cash in Hand | 20,000 |
| | | Cash at Bank | 60,000 |
| | | Prepaid Expenses | 20,000 |
| | | Patents | 50,000 |
| | | Goodwill | 1,00,000 |
| **Total** | **12,30,000** | **Total** | **12,00,000** |

**Profit & Loss Account for the year ended 31-03-2024:**

| **Particulars** | **₹** |
|---|---|
| Net Sales | 8,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 5,60,000 |
| Gross Profit | 2,40,000 |
| Operating Expenses | 80,000 |
| Operating Profit (EBIT) | 1,60,000 |
| Interest on Debentures (10% of 2,00,000) | 20,000 |
| Net Profit before Tax | 1,40,000 |
| Tax (30%) | 42,000 |
| Net Profit after Tax | 98,000 |

---

### 3.2 Identification of Components (Step 1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Current Assets} &= \text{Stock} + \text{Debtors} + \text{Cash in Hand} + \text{Cash at Bank} + \text{Prepaid Expenses} \\
&= 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 20{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Current Liabilities} &= \text{Creditors} + \text{Bills Payable} + \text{Outstanding Expenses} \\
&= 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Quick Assets} &= \text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses} \\
&= 3{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Shareholders' Funds (Equity)} &= \text{Equity Share Capital} + \text{Reserves (assumed nil)} \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Long-term Debt} &= \text{10\% Debentures} = 2{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Capital Employed} &= \text{Shareholders' Funds} + \text{Long-term Debt} \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Ratio Computation

#### (a) Current Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \\
&= \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{2{,}30{,}000} \\
&= 1.52 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Slightly below the ideal 2:1, but above 1:1 → The firm has adequate short-term liquidity but limited safety margin.

#### (b) Quick Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quick Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Quick Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{2{,}30{,}000} \\
&= 0.78 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Below ideal 1:1 → The firm depends on inventory to meet current liabilities. Tight liquidity position.

#### (c) Debt-Equity Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}} \\
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 0.25 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Very low debt → Low financial risk. Conservative capital structure.

#### (d) Inventory Turnover Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inventory Turnover} &= \frac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Closing Inventory}} \\
&= \frac{5{,}60{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \\
&= 3.73 \text{ times}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Inventory is replaced approximately 3.73 times a year. Holding period = 365/3.73 ≈ 98 days.

#### (e) Gross Profit Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Profit Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹100 of sales, the firm earns ₹30 as gross profit. Healthy.

#### (f) Net Profit Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Net Profit after Tax}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{98{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 12.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹100 of sales, ₹12.25 is the final profit. Above average for engineering firms (typical 8-10%).

#### (g) Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROCE} &= \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm earns 16% on every ₹100 of capital employed. Highly efficient.

#### (h) Interest Coverage Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interest Coverage} &= \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Charges}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{20{,}000} \\
&= 8 \text{ times}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Operating profit covers interest 8 times. Very safe for lenders.

### 3.4 Comprehensive Results Table (Analytical Matrix)

| **Ratio** | **Computed Value** | **Ideal Benchmark** | **Verdict** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current Ratio | 1.52 : 1 | 2:1 | Slightly Low |
| Quick Ratio | 0.78 : 1 | 1:1 | Low |
| Debt-Equity Ratio | 0.25 : 1 | 2:1 | Conservative |
| Inventory Turnover | 3.73 times | Higher better | Moderate |
| Gross Profit Ratio | 30% | Industry specific | Good |
| Net Profit Ratio | 12.25% | Higher better | Very Good |
| ROCE | 16% | Higher better | Excellent |
| Interest Coverage | 8 times | 3-4 times | Excellent |

### 3.5 Overall Interpretation Narrative

M/s Apex Engineering Ltd. exhibits a **conservative financial structure with strong profitability** but **moderate liquidity**. The firm is **under-leveraged** (Debt-Equity only 0.25:1) — meaning it could potentially take on more debt to fuel expansion. However, **quick ratio of 0.78:1 is a concern**; the firm should reduce inventory levels or speed up debtor collection. Profitability metrics (ROCE 16%, Net Profit 12.25%) are excellent, indicating strong operational efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Financial Ratios (Hierarchical Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["FINANCIAL RATIOS"] --> nodeB["LIQUIDITY RATIOS"]
    rootA --> nodeC["SOLVENCY RATIOS"]
    rootA --> nodeD["ACTIVITY RATIOS"]
    rootA --> nodeE["PROFITABILITY RATIOS"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Current Ratio"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Quick Ratio"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Absolute Liquid Ratio"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["Debt-Equity Ratio"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Debt-Total Assets"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Proprietary Ratio"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["Interest Coverage Ratio"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Inventory Turnover"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Debtors Turnover"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Creditors Turnover"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["Fixed Assets Turnover"]
    nodeD --> nodeD5["Working Capital Turnover"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["Gross Profit Ratio"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Net Profit Ratio"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["Operating Profit Ratio"]
    nodeE --> nodeE4["ROCE"]
    nodeE --> nodeE5["ROE"]
    nodeE --> nodeE6["EPS"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Flow of Ratio Interpretation (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["STEP 1: COLLECT FINANCIAL STATEMENTS"] --> step2["STEP 2: IDENTIFY KEY COMPONENTS"]
    step2 --> step3["STEP 3: COMPUTE RATIOS"]
    step3 --> step4["STEP 4: COMPARE WITH BENCHMARKS"]
    step4 --> step5["STEP 5: INTERPRET FINDINGS"]
    step5 --> step6["STEP 6: RECOMMEND ACTIONS"]
    step6 --> step7["FINANCIAL DECISION MAKING"]

    step2 --> compA["CA, CL, Inventory"]
    step2 --> compB["Debt, Equity"]
    step2 --> compC["Sales, COGS, NP"]

    step3 --> ratA["Liquidity"]
    step3 --> ratB["Solvency"]
    step3 --> ratC["Activity"]
    step3 --> ratD["Profitability"]

    step4 --> std1["Industry Standards"]
    step4 --> std2["Inter-firm Comparison"]
    step4 --> std3["Intra-firm (Trend)"]

    step5 --> intA["Strengths Identified"]
    step5 --> intB["Weaknesses Identified"]
    step5 --> intC["Risks Highlighted"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: DuPont Analysis (Three-Step ROE Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dupontA["ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier"]
    dupontA --> partA["PART 1: NET PROFIT MARGIN"]
    dupontA --> partB["PART 2: ASSET TURNOVER"]
    dupontA --> partC["PART 3: EQUITY MULTIPLIER"]

    partA --> formA1["Net Profit / Net Sales"]
    partA --> intA1["Measures profitability per rupee of sales"]

    partB --> formB1["Net Sales / Total Assets"]
    partB --> intB1["Measures efficiency of asset use"]

    partC --> formC1["Total Assets / Shareholders Equity"]
    partC --> intC1["Measures financial leverage"]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram: Stakeholders and Ratios They Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stkA["SHAREHOLDERS"] --> ratA1["Profitability Ratios (EPS, ROE)"]
    stkB["CREDITORS & BANKS"] --> ratB1["Solvency Ratios (Debt-Equity, Interest Coverage)"]
    stkC["MANAGEMENT"] --> ratC1["All Four Categories (Decision Making)"]
    stkD["SHORT-TERM LENDERS"] --> ratD1["Liquidity Ratios (Current, Quick)"]
    stkE["TAX AUTHORITIES"] --> ratE1["Net Profit Ratio (Tax Base Verification)"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What is Current Ratio? State its ideal standard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Current Ratio is a liquidity ratio that measures the short-term solvency of a firm by comparing its current assets to current liabilities.

$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$$

**Ideal Standard:** The generally accepted ideal ratio is **2:1**, meaning a firm should have current assets equal to twice its current liabilities to ensure adequate liquidity and a safety margin. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between Gross Profit Ratio and Net Profit Ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Gross Profit Ratio** | **Net Profit Ratio** |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | (Gross Profit / Net Sales) × 100 | (Net Profit after Tax / Net Sales) × 100 |
| **Purpose** | Measures production & pricing efficiency | Measures overall operational efficiency |
| **Expenses Considered** | Only Cost of Goods Sold | All expenses (operating, interest, tax) |
| **Benchmark** | 25-30% (Industry specific) | 10-15% (Healthy) |

Gross Profit Ratio tests the basic cost of goods efficiency, while Net Profit Ratio reflects the bottom-line profitability after all deductions. (3 marks)

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q. (a) Explain the concept of Ratio Analysis. Discuss its significance and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Ratio Analysis is a systematic technique of analyzing financial statements by computing meaningful ratios to evaluate the firm's liquidity, solvency, efficiency, and profitability.

**Significance (4 marks):**

1. **Simplifies Financial Data** — Converts complex accounting figures into simple, comparable metrics.
2. **Helps in Trend Analysis** — Year-on-year comparison shows growth or decline patterns.
3. **Assists in Decision-Making** — Used by management for planning, controlling, and forecasting.
4. **Enables Inter-firm Comparison** — Firms of different sizes can be compared.
5. **Identifies Weak Areas** — Highlights problem zones like slow inventory movement or excessive debt.

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Based on Historical Data** — Ratios show past performance, not future.
2. **Ignores Price Level Changes** — Inflation distorts year-on-year comparisons.
3. **Affected by Window Dressing** — Management can manipulate balance sheet figures.
4. **No Universal Standard** — "Ideal ratios" vary across industries.
5. **Qualitative Factors Ignored** — Worker morale, brand value not captured.

---

**Q. (b) From the following information, compute (i) Current Ratio, (ii) Quick Ratio, (iii) Inventory Turnover Ratio. (7 marks)**

| **Particulars** | **₹** |
|---|---|
| Current Assets | 4,00,000 |
| Inventory | 1,50,000 |
| Prepaid Expenses | 25,000 |
| Current Liabilities | 2,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 6,00,000 |

**Solution:**

**Quick Assets = Current Assets − Inventory − Prepaid Expenses**
$$\text{Quick Assets} = 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 - 25{,}000 = 2{,}25{,}000$$

**(i) Current Ratio:**
$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2 : 1$$

**(ii) Quick Ratio:**
$$\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{2{,}25{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 1.125 : 1$$

**(iii) Inventory Turnover Ratio:**
$$\text{Inventory Turnover} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = 4 \text{ times}$$

**Interpretation:** Current ratio meets the ideal 2:1. Quick ratio exceeds 1:1, indicating strong immediate liquidity. Inventory turns over 4 times yearly — efficient inventory management.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a) Explain the classification of ratios for analysis of financial statements. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Financial ratios are classified into four major categories:

**1. Liquidity Ratios** — Measure short-term solvency:
- Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Absolute Liquid Ratio

**2. Solvency Ratios** — Measure long-term solvency:
- Debt-Equity Ratio, Proprietary Ratio, Interest Coverage Ratio

**3. Activity/Turnover Ratios** — Measure asset utilization efficiency:
- Inventory Turnover, Debtors Turnover, Fixed Assets Turnover

**4. Profitability Ratios** — Measure earnings capability:
- Gross Profit Ratio, Net Profit Ratio, ROCE, ROE, EPS

(Each category 1.5 marks; introduction 1 mark)

---

**Q. (b) Compute the Debt-Equity Ratio, Interest Coverage Ratio, and Proprietary Ratio from the following: (7 marks)**

| **Particulars** | **₹** |
|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 10,00,000 |
| 12% Debentures | 5,00,000 |
| Total Assets | 20,00,000 |
| Operating Profit (EBIT) | 4,00,000 |

**Solution:**

**Interest on Debentures** = 12% of 5,00,000 = **₹60,000**

**Shareholders' Funds (Equity)** = ₹10,00,000 (Reserves assumed nil)

**Long-term Debt** = ₹5,00,000

**(i) Debt-Equity Ratio:**
$$\text{Debt-Equity} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 0.5 : 1$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹1 of equity, debt is ₹0.50. Well within the ideal 2:1 — low risk capital structure.

**(ii) Interest Coverage Ratio:**
$$\text{Interest Coverage} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{60{,}000} = 6.67 \text{ times}$$

**Interpretation:** Operating profit covers interest expenses 6.67 times — extremely safe.

**(iii) Proprietary Ratio:**
$$\text{Proprietary Ratio} = \frac{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 0.5 \text{ or } 50\%$$

**Interpretation:** 50% of total assets are financed by shareholders — strong financial base.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong Identification of Current Assets/Liabilities:** Prepaid expenses are current assets; Outstanding expenses are current liabilities. Confusing them leads to wrong ratios.
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Inventory in Quick Ratio:** Most common error in KTU valuation — full 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Using Net Sales vs Cost of Sales confusion:** Inventory Turnover uses **COGS**, not Net Sales. Debtors Turnover uses **Credit Sales**, not Total Sales.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply Profitability ratios by 100:** Ratio values are decimals; ratios expressed in percentage need × 100.
> 5. **Wrong formula for ROCE:** Capital Employed = **Shareholders' Funds + Long-term Debt** (NOT Total Assets − Current Liabilities is also correct but students often confuse).
> 6. **No interpretation:** Just computing ratios without interpretation loses 1-2 marks in KTU valuation. Always add a one-line interpretation.
> 7. **Using Closing vs Average Inventory:** If both given, prefer Average Inventory for true turnover. KTU accepts closing if only one figure is given.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — KTU 2024 Module 2**

- **Liquidity Ratios Test Short-Term Solvency; Solvency Ratios Test Long-Term Solvency.**
- **Current Ratio = CA / CL; Quick Ratio = (CA − Inventory − Prepaid) / CL; Cash Ratio = Cash + Marketable Securities / CL**
- **Ideal Current Ratio = 2:1; Ideal Quick Ratio = 1:1; Ideal Cash Ratio = 0.5:1**
- **Debt-Equity = Long-term Debt / Equity; Ideal = 2:1; Lower = Safer**
- **Interest Coverage = EBIT / Interest; Ideal = 3-4 times; Lower = Risk of Default**
- **Inventory Turnover = COGS / Average Inventory; Higher = Better; Lower Holding Period**
- **Debtors Turnover = Credit Sales / Avg Debtors; Collection Period = 365 / Debtors Turnover**
- **Creditors Turnover = Credit Purchases / Avg Creditors; Payment Period = 365 / Creditors Turnover**
- **Gross Profit Ratio = (GP / Sales) × 100; Net Profit Ratio = (NP / Sales) × 100**
- **ROCE = (EBIT / Capital Employed) × 100; Capital Employed = Equity + LTD**
- **ROE = (PAT / Equity) × 100; EPS = PAT / No. of Equity Shares**
- **DuPont Identity:** ROE = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier
- **Limitations:** Historical data, ignores price changes, window dressing, no universal standard
- **Always provide interpretation after computing — KTU awards marks for analysis, not just numbers.**
- **Memorize formula sheets; in exam, write the formula first, then substitute, then interpret.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depreciation - Definition and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIIT833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Depreciation - Definition and Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview of Depreciation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depreciation** is the systematic and rational allocation of the **depreciable amount of an asset** over its **estimated useful life**, in a manner that represents the consumption of the economic benefits embodied in the asset.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with **AS 6 / Ind AS 16** of the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India), depreciation is:

- A **non-cash expense** that reduces the book value of a fixed asset.
- Charged against the **profit and loss account** to arrive at the true net profit.
- A measure of the **wear and tear, effluxion of time, obsolescence, or exhaustion** of the asset.

### Key Terms in the Definition

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Depreciable Amount** | Cost of asset − Residual (Scrap) Value |
| **Useful Life** | The period over which the asset is expected to be used by the entity |
| **Residual Value** | Estimated value at the end of useful life |
| **Book Value (WDV)** | Original Cost − Accumulated Depreciation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Depreciation is NOT a valuation technique.** It is a process of **cost allocation**, not asset valuation. The market value of an asset may differ from its book value.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏍️

Imagine you buy a brand new **Royal Enfield motorcycle for ₹2,00,000** in 2024. You plan to use it for **5 years** and expect to sell it for **₹50,000** as scrap/resale at the end of life.

Each year, the bike:
- Loses some value due to **wear and tear** (engine wear, tyre wear).
- Loses value due to **obsolescence** (newer models with better features).
- Loses value due to **exhaustion** (battery life, brake pads, etc.).

If you wait until the 5th year to recognize the full loss, your profit for 2024 will look inflated and the 2028 profit will look terrible. **Depreciation** is the accountant's way of **spreading this ₹1,50,000 cost fairly** across all 5 years so that each year bears its fair share of the asset's consumption.

> [!TIP]
> Think of depreciation as a **"monthly EMI on wear and tear"** — just as you pay for a loan gradually, depreciation charges the cost of an asset gradually.

## 1.3 Why Depreciation is Charged — The 3 Core Reasons

1. **True Profit Calculation** – Without depreciation, profits are overstated and the business may pay tax on non-existent income.
2. **Cost Recovery** – To recover the cost of the asset from the revenue it generates (matching principle of accounting).
3. **Replacement Fund** – To accumulate funds (either explicitly via Sinking Fund or implicitly via retained earnings) for eventual replacement of the asset.

## 1.4 Factors Affecting the Amount of Depreciation

> [!NOTE]
> The amount of depreciation per year depends on **four key variables**, which every KTU question will test:

- **Cost of the Asset (C)** — includes purchase price, freight, installation, and any cost to bring the asset to usable condition.
- **Estimated Useful Life (n)** — in years or units of production.
- **Estimated Residual/Scrap Value (S)** — value at the end of useful life.
- **Method of Depreciation** — SLM, WDV, SYD, etc.

The general symbol used across all KTU problems is:
- **C** = Original Cost
- **S** = Scrap Value
- **n** = Useful Life in years
- **r** = Rate of Depreciation
- **D** = Annual Depreciation
- **BV / WDV** = Book Value / Written Down Value
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Depreciation Methods

Depreciation methods are broadly classified into **three families**:

```
DEPRECIATION METHODS
├── 1. Time-Based (Straight Line Family)
│       ├── Straight Line Method (SLM)
│       ├── Fixed Instalment Method
│       └── Annuity Method
│
├── 2. Diminishing/Reducing Balance Family
│       ├── Written Down Value (WDV) Method
│       ├── Double Declining Balance (DDB) Method
│       └── Diminishing Balance Method
│
└── 3. Usage / Activity-Based Methods
        ├── Units of Production Method
        ├── Machine Hour Rate Method
        ├── Sum of Years Digits (SYD) Method
        └── Replacement Method / Insurance Method / Sinking Fund Method
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)**, the most frequently tested methods are:
> 1. **Straight Line Method (SLM)**
> 2. **Written Down Value (WDV) Method**
> 3. **Units of Production Method**
> 4. **Sum of Years Digits (SYD) Method**
> 5. **Sinking Fund Method**
>
> Master these five and you can answer 95% of depreciation questions.

## 2.2 Method 1 — Straight Line Method (SLM) / Fixed Instalment Method

### Concept
An **equal amount of depreciation** is charged every year throughout the useful life of the asset. The asset's value reduces **linearly** (in a straight line) from cost to scrap value.

### Step-by-Step Logic
- The total depreciable amount (C − S) is divided equally across **n** years.
- Annual depreciation is **constant**; book value decreases by a fixed amount each year.
- Suitable for assets that provide **uniform benefits** over time (buildings, furniture, leases).

### When to Use SLM
- Asset usage is **constant** year after year.
- Repairs and maintenance are **low and uniform**.
- **Obsolescence is low** (e.g., buildings, land improvements, leasehold rights).

## 2.3 Method 2 — Written Down Value (WDV) / Diminishing Balance Method

### Concept
Depreciation is calculated as a **fixed percentage of the book value (WDV)** at the beginning of each year. The depreciation amount is **highest in year 1** and **decreases** every year, but the asset **never becomes zero** mathematically.

### Step-by-Step Logic
- A constant rate **r** is applied on the **diminishing book value** every year.
- Book value at the end of useful life will approximately equal the scrap value (by careful selection of r).
- The asset is assumed to provide **more service in early years** and less in later years.
- Suitable for assets that lose value faster initially (vehicles, machinery, computers).

## 2.4 Method 3 — Units of Production Method / Production Unit Method

### Concept
Depreciation is based on **actual usage** of the asset rather than the passage of time. The cost is allocated based on **units produced, hours run, or kilometres driven**.

### Step-by-Step Logic
- Depreciation per unit = (C − S) ÷ Total estimated production units over life.
- Annual depreciation = Depreciation per unit × Actual units produced that year.
- Most **scientifically accurate** method for plant and machinery.
- Suitable for assets whose wear depends on **use** rather than time (e.g., a truck, a drill press).

## 2.5 Method 4 — Sum of Years Digits (SYD) Method

### Concept
A **depreciable amount** is multiplied by a **declining fraction** each year. The numerator is the **remaining life** and the denominator is the **sum of all years' digits**.

### Step-by-Step Logic
- Compute Sum of years = n(n+1)/2
- For Year k (counted from start), fraction = (n − k + 1) / Sum
- Depreciation is higher in initial years and lower in later years.
- A "compromise" between SLM and WDV — uses a fixed fraction rather than a fixed rate.

## 2.6 Method 5 — Sinking Fund Method

### Concept
Depreciation is the **annual instalment** required to accumulate a sum equal to (C − S) by the end of the asset's life, **invested at a given rate of interest**.

### Step-by-Step Logic
- A fixed amount is deposited every year into a **Sinking Fund** account.
- This amount earns compound interest.
- At the end of life, the accumulated fund = (C − S), which is used to replace the asset.
- The interest earned on the fund is also shown as a charge to the P&L.

> [!TIP]
> Sinking Fund = **"Save now, replace later"** — used by public utilities and large infrastructure projects.

## 2.7 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the KTU ESE.** It is the single most-tested set of formulas in depreciation problems.

| Method | Key Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SLM** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | C = Cost, S = Scrap, n = Life | Constant depreciation every year |
| **SLM (Rate)** | $D = C \times r$ where $r = \dfrac{1}{n} \times 100$ | r = SLM rate | If scrap = 0 |
| **WDV** | $D_t = BV_{t-1} \times r$ | BV = Book Value, r = WDV rate | Declining balance |
| **WDV (Rate formula)** | $r = \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}\right] \times 100$ | C, S, n | The "engineering" rate formula |
| **Units of Production** | $D = \dfrac{C - S}{N_{total}} \times N_{actual}$ | N = Units | Actual usage based |
| **SYD Sum** | $Sum = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | n = Life | Foundation formula |
| **SYD Depreciation** | $D_k = (C - S) \times \dfrac{n - k + 1}{Sum}$ | k = year number | Declining fraction |
| **Sinking Fund Instalment** | $A = (C - S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1}$ | i = interest rate, n = life | Annual deposit |
| **Sinking Fund Accrued** | $A_n = A \times \dfrac{(1+i)^{n} - 1}{i}$ | i, n | Future value check |
| **Annuity Method** | $Dep = (C - S) \times \dfrac{i}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} + C \times i$ | i, n | Depreciation + Interest |
| **Double Declining** | $r_{DDB} = 2 \times r_{SLM}$ | SLM rate | Accelerated method |

> [!WARNING]
> In the WDV rate formula, $\left(\dfrac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}$ is **NOT** an absolute value bracket. It is a power expression. **Never write** $\left\vert \dfrac{S}{C} \right\vert$ in the formula.

## 2.8 Comparative Analysis: When to Use What?

| Criterion | SLM | WDV | Units of Production | SYD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Depreciation Pattern** | Equal | Decreasing | Varies with use | Decreasing |
| **Suitable Asset** | Building, Furniture | Machine, Vehicle | Truck, Drill press | Tech equipment |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Moderate | Moderate | Complex |
| **Repairs Assumption** | Low & uniform | Increasing over time | Variable | Increasing |
| **Tax Friendliness** | Lower initial deduction | Higher initial deduction | Variable | Higher initial |
| **Common Industry** | Real Estate | Manufacturing | Mining, Transport | Tech / Electronics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In engineering project management (NEP 2020 OBE context), depreciation methods are used to compute the **annual equivalent cost of machinery** in cost-benefit analysis of capital projects (e.g., selecting between two CNC machines for a factory).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 SLM — Complete Derivation

### Derivation of SLM Annual Depreciation

**Step 1 — Identify the depreciable base.**
The total cost that must be recovered over the life of the asset is the difference between the original cost (C) and the expected scrap/residual value (S).

$$
\text{Depreciable Base} = C - S
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the time period.**
The asset will be used for **n** years (estimated useful life).

**Step 3 — Distribute the depreciable base equally.**

$$
D = \frac{C - S}{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the Book Value at end of year t.**

$$
BV_t = C - (t \times D) = C - t \times \frac{C - S}{n}
$$

> This linear equation is what gives the method its name — the book value decreases in a **straight line** with slope −D.

### Worked Example — SLM

**Problem:** A machine is purchased for ₹5,00,000. Estimated life = 10 years. Scrap value = ₹50,000. Calculate depreciation for the first 3 years and the book value at the end of year 3.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute annual depreciation D.**

$$
D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{10} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{10} = 45{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate for 3 years.**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Depreciation (₹) | Accumulated Dep (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 45,000 | 45,000 | 4,55,000 |
| 2 | 4,55,000 | 45,000 | 90,000 | 4,10,000 |
| 3 | 4,10,000 | 45,000 | 1,35,000 | 3,65,000 |

**Book Value at end of Year 3 = ₹3,65,000** ✅

---

## 3.2 WDV — Complete Derivation

### Derivation of WDV Rate Formula

**Step 1 — Setup the recurrence.**
Let $BV_0 = C$ and $BV_n = S$. Each year, the book value reduces by a fixed rate **r**.

$$
BV_t = BV_{t-1} \times (1 - r)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply recursively over n years.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BV_1 &= C \times (1 - r) \\
BV_2 &= BV_1 \times (1 - r) = C \times (1 - r)^2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
BV_n &= C \times (1 - r)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Set the end condition $BV_n = S$.**

$$
C \times (1 - r)^n = S
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for r.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1 - r)^n &= \frac{S}{C} \\
1 - r &= \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} \\
r &= 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express as percentage.**

$$
r \times 100 = \left[1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n}\right] \times 100
$$

### Worked Example — WDV

**Problem:** Machine cost = ₹5,00,000; Life = 5 years; Scrap = ₹1,00,000. Compute the WDV rate and depreciation schedule for 5 years.

**Step 1 — Compute the WDV rate r.**

$$
r = 1 - \left(\frac{1{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/5} = 1 - (0.2)^{0.2}
$$

Computing $(0.2)^{0.2}$:

$$
(0.2)^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times \ln(0.2)} = e^{0.2 \times (-1.6094)} = e^{-0.3219} \approx 0.7248
$$

Therefore:

$$
r = 1 - 0.7248 = 0.2752 \approx 27.52\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute depreciation year by year.**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Dep @ 27.52% (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 1,37,600 | 3,62,400 |
| 2 | 3,62,400 | 99,732 | 2,62,668 |
| 3 | 2,62,668 | 72,286 | 1,90,382 |
| 4 | 1,90,382 | 52,393 | 1,37,989 |
| 5 | 1,37,989 | 37,980 | 1,00,009 ≈ **1,00,000** ✅ |

> Closing BV matches scrap value (small rounding difference of ₹9 is acceptable).

---

## 3.3 Units of Production — Complete Derivation

### Formula Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the depreciation per unit of output.**

The total cost to be recovered (C − S) must equal the total units produced over the asset's life ($N_{total}$). Therefore:

$$
d = \frac{C - S}{N_{total}}
$$

where **d** is depreciation per unit.

**Step 2 — Multiply by actual units produced in a year to get annual depreciation.**

$$
D_t = d \times N_t = \frac{C - S}{N_{total}} \times N_t
$$

### Worked Example — Units of Production

**Problem:** A machine costs ₹6,00,000 with a scrap value of ₹1,00,000. Total estimated production over life = 1,00,000 units. Production in years 1, 2, 3 = 30,000; 25,000; 20,000 units respectively. Calculate depreciation for 3 years.

**Step 1 — Depreciation per unit.**

$$
d = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 5 \text{ per unit}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual depreciation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 5 \times 30{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 \\
D_2 &= 5 \times 25{,}000 = 1{,}25{,}000 \\
D_3 &= 5 \times 20{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total depreciation in 3 years = ₹3,75,000** ✅

---

## 3.4 Sum of Years Digits (SYD) — Complete Derivation

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Sum of all year digits (the denominator).**
If the asset has life **n**, the years are 1, 2, 3, ..., n. Their sum is the **n-th triangular number**:

$$
\text{Sum} = 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Fraction for year k.**
For year k (counted from the start of life), the remaining life = n − k + 1. The fraction of depreciable base allocated to year k is:

$$
f_k = \frac{n - k + 1}{\text{Sum}} = \frac{n - k + 1}{\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}} = \frac{2(n - k + 1)}{n(n+1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Depreciation for year k.**

$$
D_k = (C - S) \times f_k = (C - S) \times \frac{2(n - k + 1)}{n(n+1)}
$$

### Worked Example — SYD

**Problem:** Asset cost = ₹4,00,000; Scrap = ₹1,00,000; Life = 4 years. Compute SYD depreciation.

**Step 1 — Sum of years.**

$$
\text{Sum} = \frac{4 \times 5}{2} = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Fractions for each year.**

| Year (k) | Remaining Life (n−k+1) | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 4/10 | (3,00,000 × 4/10) = **1,20,000** |
| 2 | 3 | 3/10 | (3,00,000 × 3/10) = **90,000** |
| 3 | 2 | 2/10 | (3,00,000 × 2/10) = **60,000** |
| 4 | 1 | 1/10 | (3,00,000 × 1/10) = **30,000** |
| **Total** | | | **3,00,000** ✅ |

---

## 3.5 Sinking Fund Method — Complete Derivation

### Derivation of Annual Instalment

**Step 1 — Setup the future value of an annuity.**
The Sinking Fund Instalment **A** is deposited every year for **n** years at interest rate **i** (compounded annually). The future value of this annuity at the end of n years must equal the depreciable amount (C − S).

**Step 2 — Use the standard future value of annuity formula.**

$$
FV = A \times \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Set FV = C − S and solve for A.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \times \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i} &= C - S \\
A &= (C - S) \times \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interest on Sinking Fund.**
Each year, the existing fund earns interest. This interest is also debited to P&L Account. The **total P&L charge** for the year is:

$$
\text{Total Charge}_t = A + (\text{Opening Fund Balance}_{t-1} \times i)
$$

### Worked Example — Sinking Fund

**Problem:** Asset cost = ₹5,00,000; Scrap = ₹1,00,000; Life = 4 years; Interest = 10% p.a. (compounded).

**Step 1 — Compute annual Sinking Fund instalment A.**

$$
A = (5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000) \times \frac{0.10}{(1.10)^4 - 1}
$$

Compute $(1.10)^4$:

$$
(1.10)^4 = 1.4641
$$

Therefore:

$$
A = 4{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.10}{1.4641 - 1} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{0.10}{0.4641} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.2155
$$

$$
A = 86{,}189 \text{ (approx)}
$$

**Step 2 — Sinking Fund Account (showing opening balance, interest, instalment, closing balance).**

| Year | Opening Fund (₹) | Interest @ 10% (₹) | Instalment A (₹) | Closing Fund (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 86,189 | 86,189 |
| 2 | 86,189 | 8,619 | 86,189 | 1,80,997 |
| 3 | 1,80,997 | 18,100 | 86,189 | 2,85,286 |
| 4 | 2,85,286 | 28,529 | 86,189 | **4,00,004** ≈ 4,00,000 ✅ |

**Step 3 — Total P&L Charge per year.**

| Year | Depreciation A (₹) | Interest on Fund (₹) | Total P&L Charge (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 86,189 | 0 | 86,189 |
| 2 | 86,189 | 8,619 | 94,808 |
| 3 | 86,189 | 18,100 | 1,04,289 |
| 4 | 86,189 | 28,529 | 1,14,718 |

> Observe how the annual P&L charge **increases** over time under Sinking Fund — this is because more interest accrues on the accumulated fund.

---

## 3.6 Proof: Why Book Value Under WDV Never Reaches Zero

**Claim:** Under WDV method, book value approaches but never equals zero.

**Proof:**

$$
BV_t = C \times (1 - r)^t
$$

As $t \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} (1 - r)^t = 0 \quad \text{(since } 0 < (1-r) < 1\text{)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lim_{t \to \infty} BV_t = 0
$$

But for any **finite** t:

$$
BV_t = C \times (1 - r)^t > 0
$$

**Conclusion:** Book value can be made arbitrarily small but never exactly zero. Hence the method is also called the **Diminishing Balance Method** — it never fully extinguishes. ∎
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define depreciation. List any four causes of depreciation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the depreciable amount of an asset over its estimated useful life, representing the consumption of economic benefits. It is a non-cash expense charged to the Profit and Loss Account to reflect wear and tear, obsolescence, or effluxion of time.

**Four Causes (1.5 marks — 0.5 × 4 any four, or 1 + 0.5):**

1. **Wear and Tear** — physical deterioration from use (e.g., engine wear in a machine).
2. **Effluxion of Time** — mere passage of time reduces value (e.g., a patent expiring).
3. **Obsolescence** — technology becomes outdated (e.g., a 5G phone replaced by 6G).
4. **Exhaustion** — natural resources are depleted (e.g., coal in a mine).
5. **Accidents / Abnormal Causes** — fire, floods (excluded from normal depreciation, covered by insurance).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Distinguish between Straight Line Method and Written Down Value Method of depreciation (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 × 3):**

| Parameter | Straight Line Method (SLM) | Written Down Value Method (WDV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Depreciation Pattern** | Equal amount every year | Decreasing amount every year |
| **Calculation Base** | Original Cost | Reducing Book Value |
| **Asset Value at End** | Equals scrap value exactly | Approximates scrap value (rarely exact) |
| **Suitable For** | Assets with uniform usage (buildings, furniture) | Assets with high obsolescence / early wear (machines, vehicles) |
| **Tax Friendliness** | Lower initial deduction | Higher initial deduction |
| **Repair Pattern** | Assumes constant repairs | Assumes increasing repairs (offset by decreasing dep.) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Module 2 Part B follows the **Internal Choice** pattern. The student must answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B** (full 14 marks, no sub-choice).

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Q: A manufacturing company purchased a machine on 1st April 2021 for ₹8,00,000. The estimated life is 5 years with a scrap value of ₹1,00,000.**

**(a)** Prepare the depreciation schedule using the **Straight Line Method (SLM)** for 5 years. Show the **Asset Account** and the **Provision for Depreciation Account**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same machine is depreciated using the **Written Down Value (WDV) Method** at the rate derived from the formula, prepare the **Depreciation Schedule** for 5 years. Also compare the **total depreciation** charged under both methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Solution to (a) — SLM (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Depreciation.**

$$
D = \frac{C - S}{n} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{5} = \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{5} = 1{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Asset Account (showing original value retained at cost, depreciation accumulated in Provision Account).**

| Year | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1-Apr-21 | To Bank A/c | 8,00,000 | 31-Mar-22 By Balance c/d | 8,00,000 |
| 1-Apr-22 | To Balance b/d | 8,00,000 | 31-Mar-23 By Balance c/d | 8,00,000 |
| 1-Apr-23 | To Balance b/d | 8,00,000 | 31-Mar-24 By Balance c/d | 8,00,000 |
| 1-Apr-24 | To Balance b/d | 8,00,000 | 31-Mar-25 By Balance c/d | 8,00,000 |
| 1-Apr-25 | To Balance b/d | 8,00,000 | 31-Mar-26 By Bank (Sale) | 1,00,000 |
| | | | By P&L (Loss on Sale) | — |
| | | 8,00,000 | | 8,00,000 |

**Step 3 — Provision for Depreciation Account.**

| Year | Particulars | Amount (₹) | Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | To Balance c/d | 1,40,000 | By P&L A/c | 1,40,000 |
| 2 | To Balance c/d | 2,80,000 | By Balance b/d | 1,40,000 |
| | | | By P&L A/c | 1,40,000 |
| 3 | To Balance c/d | 4,20,000 | By Balance b/d | 2,80,000 |
| | | | By P&L A/c | 1,40,000 |
| 4 | To Balance c/d | 5,60,000 | By Balance b/d | 4,20,000 |
| | | | By P&L A/c | 1,40,000 |
| 5 | To Asset A/c (Sale) | 7,00,000 | By Balance b/d | 5,60,000 |
| | | | By P&L A/c | 1,40,000 |
| | | 7,00,000 | | 7,00,000 |

**Step 4 — Final Values.**

- Total Depreciation = ₹7,00,000
- Asset value at end of 5th year = ₹1,00,000 (Scrap value reached) ✅

---

#### 🔹 Solution to (b) — WDV (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the WDV rate using the formula.**

$$
r = 1 - \left(\frac{S}{C}\right)^{1/n} = 1 - \left(\frac{1{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000}\right)^{1/5} = 1 - (0.125)^{0.2}
$$

Compute $(0.125)^{0.2}$:

$$
\ln(0.125) = -2.0794
$$

$$
0.2 \times (-2.0794) = -0.4159
$$

$$
(0.125)^{0.2} = e^{-0.4159} \approx 0.6598
$$

$$
r = 1 - 0.6598 = 0.3402 \approx 34.02\%
$$

**Step 2 — Depreciation Schedule.**

| Year | Opening BV (₹) | Dep @ 34.02% (₹) | Accumulated Dep (₹) | Closing BV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8,00,000 | 2,72,160 | 2,72,160 | 5,27,840 |
| 2 | 5,27,840 | 1,79,580 | 4,51,740 | 3,48,260 |
| 3 | 3,48,260 | 1,18,480 | 5,70,220 | 2,29,780 |
| 4 | 2,29,780 | 78,180 | 6,48,400 | 1,51,600 |
| 5 | 1,51,600 | 51,600 | 7,00,000 | **1,00,000** ✅ |

**Step 3 — Comparison.**

| Parameter | SLM | WDV |
|---|---|---|
| Total Depreciation (₹) | 7,00,000 | 7,00,000 |
| Year 1 Depreciation (₹) | 1,40,000 | 2,72,160 |
| Year 5 Depreciation (₹) | 1,40,000 | 51,600 |
| Pattern | Constant | Declining |

**Conclusion:** Both methods recover the **same total depreciation of ₹7,00,000**, but the **timing of recognition differs** — WDV provides higher tax shield in early years.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: A company acquired a delivery truck on 1st January 2022 for ₹12,00,000. Estimated useful life = 5 years; Residual value = ₹2,00,000; Total estimated kilometres over life = 2,00,000 km.**

**(a)** Compute depreciation for 3 years using the **Units of Production Method** given that the truck ran **50,000 km, 40,000 km, and 30,000 km** in years 1, 2, 3 respectively. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Recompute depreciation using the **Sum of Years Digits (SYD) Method** for all 5 years. Comment on which method gives higher tax shield in the first 3 years. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Solution to (a) — Units of Production (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Depreciation per km.**

$$
d = \frac{C - S}{N_{total}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 5 \text{ per km}
$$

**Step 2 — Year-wise Depreciation.**

| Year | Kms Run | Dep per km (₹) | Annual Depreciation (₹) | Accumulated Dep (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50,000 | 5 | 2,50,000 | 2,50,000 | 9,50,000 |
| 2 | 40,000 | 5 | 2,00,000 | 4,50,000 | 7,50,000 |
| 3 | 30,000 | 5 | 1,50,000 | 6,00,000 | 6,00,000 |

**Total depreciation in 3 years = ₹6,00,000** ✅

---

#### 🔹 Solution to (b) — SYD Method (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sum of years.**

$$
\text{Sum} = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15
$$

**Step 2 — Depreciation Schedule.**

| Year (k) | Remaining Life (n−k+1) | Fraction | Depreciation (₹) | Book Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 5/15 | (10,00,000 × 5/15) = 3,33,333 | 8,66,667 |
| 2 | 4 | 4/15 | (10,00,000 × 4/15) = 2,66,667 | 6,00,000 |
| 3 | 3 | 3/15 | (10,00,000 × 3/15) = 2,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 4 | 2 | 2/15 | (10,00,000 × 2/15) = 1,33,333 | 2,66,667 |
| 5 | 1 | 1/15 | (10,00,000 × 1/15) = 66,667 | 2,00,000 |
| **Total** | | | **10,00,000** ✅ | |

**Step 3 — Tax Shield Comparison (First 3 Years).**

| Year | Units of Production (₹) | SYD (₹) | Higher Dep Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,50,000 | 3,33,333 | **SYD** |
| 2 | 2,00,000 | 2,66,667 | **SYD** |
| 3 | 1,50,000 | 2,00,000 | **SYD** |
| **Total (3 years)** | **6,00,000** | **8,00,000** | **SYD wins by ₹2,00,000** |

**Comment (1 mark):** SYD provides a higher depreciation in the first 3 years, leading to a **higher tax shield** in early years. However, Units of Production is more **scientifically accurate** when the asset's wear is directly linked to usage (e.g., a truck's wear is best measured in kilometres, not years).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract scrap value in SYD and Units of Production** — Many students write $(C - S) = $ zero mistake. **Always compute depreciable base = C − S first.**
> 2. **Confusing WDV rate with SLM rate** — The WDV rate formula $r = [1 - (S/C)^{1/n}] \times 100$ is the **single most-skipped step** in KTU papers. Memorize it.
> 3. **In SYD, using the wrong numerator** — For year 1, numerator is **n**, not (n−1). For year k, it is **(n−k+1)**.
> 4. **In Sinking Fund, forgetting interest on opening balance** — Total P&L charge = Instalment + Interest on opening fund (NOT on closing fund).
> 5. **Rounding off WDV rate too early** — Carry at least 4 decimal places to avoid large errors in the final year.
> 6. **Not mentioning the choice of method** — Always end the answer with a **one-line conclusion** about why a particular method is appropriate (e.g., "SLM is suitable here because the asset provides uniform benefits over its life").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this checklist before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- ✅ **Depreciation** is a **non-cash, cost allocation** expense, not a valuation.
- ✅ It is caused by **wear and tear, effluxion of time, obsolescence, and exhaustion**.
- ✅ **SLM** → constant depreciation; best for **uniform usage assets** (buildings, furniture).
- ✅ **WDV** → declining depreciation; book value **never becomes zero**; best for **machinery, vehicles**.
- ✅ **Units of Production** → depreciation = ₹ per unit × actual units; best for **trucks, drill presses, mining equipment**.
- ✅ **SYD** → fraction = remaining life / sum of years; **declining balance** using arithmetic progression.
- ✅ **Sinking Fund** → annual instalment + interest on fund; both are charged to P&L.
- ✅ **Annuity Method** → depreciation + interest on **original cost** charged every year (depreciation increases over time).
- ✅ WDV Rate formula: $r = [1 - (S/C)^{1/n}] \times 100$ — **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA**.
- ✅ SYD Sum formula: $\text{Sum} = n(n+1)/2$ — **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA**.
- ✅ Sinking Fund Instalment: $A = (C - S) \times i / [(1+i)^n - 1]$ — **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA**.
- ✅ **Income Tax Act (India)** does NOT permit SLM for most assets — it prescribes WDV at block rates. **For KTU exam, however, both are allowed unless specified.**
- ✅ **Accounting Standard (Ind AS 16)** requires **useful life** and **residual value** to be **reviewed at least annually**.
- ✅ **Land is NOT depreciated** (it has infinite useful life).
- ✅ In **SLM**, residual value can be zero or non-zero; in **WDV**, the rate is set such that the closing balance approximates scrap value.
- ✅ **Companies Act 2013, Schedule II** prescribes useful life for various asset classes — KTU theory questions may test this awareness.
- ✅ Always **show the asset account, provision account, and P&L account** in long questions — partial marking is based on these ledgers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In any KTU depreciation problem, write your solution in this order:
> 1. State the **assumptions** (method, scrap value, useful life).
> 2. Write the **formula** with values substituted.
> 3. Show the **computation** step by step.
> 4. Present the **schedule in a table** (year, opening BV, depreciation, accumulated, closing BV).
> 5. Add a **one-line conclusion or comparison**.
>
> This structure alone can fetch you **2–3 grace marks** even if your final numerical answer has a small error.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Concepts Relating to Income Measurement and Profit Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost Concepts Relating to Income Measurement and Profit Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Income Measurement: The Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Income Measurement** in cost and management accounting is the systematic process of determining periodic profit or loss by matching the **revenues** earned during a specific accounting period with the **costs** incurred to generate those revenues, in accordance with the **Matching Principle** of accounting and the relevant cost classification framework (product vs. period, fixed vs. variable, direct vs. indirect).

For an engineer-turned-manager, income measurement is essentially the **"Profit Equation"** operationalized:

$$
\text{Income (Profit)} = \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Cost (Product + Period)}
$$

## 1.2 Profit Planning: The Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Profit Planning** (also called *Profit Forecasting* or *Budgetary Profit Planning*) is the forward-looking management accounting activity of establishing **quantified profit targets** for a future period, identifying the **sales volume, product mix, and cost structure** required to achieve them, and continuously monitoring performance through **budgetary control and variance analysis**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Engineering Student's Café

Imagine you (a B.Tech student) decide to run a small tea-coffee café near campus to fund your project kits. Three months in, you ask yourself:

- **How much profit did I really make?** → *Income Measurement problem.*
- **How many cups must I sell next month to buy a new laptop worth ₹50,000?** → *Profit Planning problem.*

To answer the first question, you must classify every cost:
- The **rent** of the stall (₹5,000/month) — *Fixed Cost* (does not change with cups sold).
- The **milk, sugar, tea powder** per cup (₹10) — *Variable Cost* (changes directly with cups sold).
- The **electricity bill** (₹1,000 base + ₹2 per cup) — *Semi-Variable Cost* (mixed behavior).

To answer the second question, you apply **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis**: How many cups at what price will cover costs AND give you the desired profit?

This is precisely what Module 3 addresses at the industrial scale — applied to manufacturing firms, service companies, and engineering projects.

## 1.4 Core Cost Concepts Relevant to Income & Profit Planning

| # | Cost Concept | Definition | Income/Planning Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fixed Cost** | Cost that remains constant in total regardless of activity level within a relevant range | Determines the profit threshold that must first be crossed |
| 2 | **Variable Cost** | Cost that varies directly and proportionately with activity level | Determines the *contribution* each unit makes toward profit |
| 3 | **Semi-Variable Cost** | Cost with both fixed and variable components | Must be split using High-Low or Scattergraph methods before planning |
| 4 | **Direct Cost** | Cost traceable to a specific cost object (product, project, department) | Used for product costing & profitability decisions |
| 5 | **Indirect Cost (Overhead)** | Cost shared by multiple cost objects and allocated via predetermined bases | Allocated to products; affects product profitability |
| 6 | **Product Cost** | Cost inventoried (Raw Material, WIP, Finished Goods) until sold — appears in COGS | Matches revenue in the income statement |
| 7 | **Period Cost** | Cost expensed in the period incurred (Selling, Admin, R&D) | Immediately reduces period profit |
| 8 | **Sunk Cost** | Past, irrecoverable cost | **Irrelevant** for profit planning & decisions |
| 9 | **Opportunity Cost** | Benefit foregone by choosing one alternative over the next best | Critical for make-or-buy, special-order decisions |
| 10 | **Relevant Cost** | Future cost that differs between alternatives | Drives all short-term profit-planning decisions |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The board examiner will *always* test your ability to classify a given cost item into **at least two of the above categories** and then justify its treatment in the income statement or profit plan. Memorize the table above — it is the single most-tested framework in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The CVP Logic Chain

Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis rests on five foundational assumptions. Once these are accepted, the entire profit-planning machinery follows logically.

1. **Linear Behaviour Assumption** — Both total revenue and total cost behave as linear functions of sales volume within the **relevant range**.
2. **Cost Classification Assumption** — All costs can be cleanly bifurcated into fixed and variable elements.
3. **Single-Product or Constant-Mix Assumption** — Either one product is sold, or the sales mix remains constant.
4. **Price & Efficiency Assumption** — Selling price per unit, variable cost per unit, and fixed costs are all known and constant.
5. **Inventory Assumption** — Either production = sales (no inventory change) or FIFO with stable inventory.

### The Logical Chain of CVP Thinking

- **Step 1:** Separate every cost into Fixed (FC) and Variable (VC per unit).
- **Step 2:** Compute **Contribution Margin per unit** (C) = Selling Price (S) − Variable Cost per unit (V).
- **Step 3:** Recognize that Contribution first **recovers Fixed Costs**, and only after FC is fully recovered does every additional unit contribute **pure profit**.
- **Step 4:** The point where Total Contribution = Total Fixed Cost is the **Break-Even Point (BEP)**.
- **Step 5:** Beyond BEP, every additional unit of contribution flows directly to profit — the **"Operating Leverage"** effect.
- **Step 6:** For a *desired* profit (P), the required sales volume is computed by extending the same equation: **(FC + P) ÷ C**.
- **Step 7:** The **Margin of Safety (MoS)** measures how far actual sales exceed BEP sales — a direct indicator of business risk.

## 2.2 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering Firms

- **Project Costing:** Engineers must determine whether a custom order recovers its variable cost and contributes toward overhead.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Requires comparison of *avoidable* (relevant) costs — never sunk cost.
- **Product Mix Decisions:** When capacity is constrained, the product with the highest **Contribution per limiting factor** is prioritized.
- **Bid Pricing:** Tenders often need a markup over full cost; CVP tells you the minimum volume that justifies accepting a price.
- **Budgetary Control:** Comparing standard cost vs. actual cost via variances is the operational feedback loop of profit planning.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **complete high-yield formula set** you need for any Module 3 numerical. Master it before attempting Part B problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit / Output | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contribution per unit | $C = S - V$ | ₹ / unit | S = Selling price, V = Variable cost per unit |
| 2 | Total Contribution | $TC = (S - V) \times Q$ | ₹ | Q = Sales quantity |
| 3 | **P/V Ratio** (Profit Volume Ratio) | $\text{P/V} = \dfrac{C}{S} \times 100 = \dfrac{TC}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$ | % | Also called Contribution Margin Ratio |
| 4 | Break-Even Point (units) | $\text{BEP}_Q = \dfrac{FC}{C}$ | units | Where Profit = 0 |
| 5 | Break-Even Point (₹) | $\text{BEP}_{₹} = \dfrac{FC}{\text{P/V Ratio}} = \dfrac{FC \times S}{C}$ | ₹ | Total sales revenue at BEP |
| 6 | Profit (single product) | $P = TC - FC = (C \times Q) - FC$ | ₹ | Valid for Q > BEP |
| 7 | Desired Profit Sales (units) | $Q_P = \dfrac{FC + P}{C}$ | units | Quantity required for target profit P |
| 8 | Desired Profit Sales (₹) | $S_P = \dfrac{FC + P}{\text{P/V Ratio}}$ | ₹ | Revenue required for target profit P |
| 9 | Margin of Safety (units) | $\text{MoS}_Q = Q_{\text{actual}} - \text{BEP}_Q$ | units | "Cushion" above break-even |
| 10 | Margin of Safety (₹) | $\text{MoS}_{₹} = S_{\text{actual}} - \text{BEP}_{₹}$ | ₹ | In revenue terms |
| 11 | Margin of Safety Ratio | $\text{MoS Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{MoS}_₹}{S_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | % | Higher = Lower business risk |
| 12 | Operating Leverage (Degree) | $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{TC}{P} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Profit}}$ | ratio | % change in Profit per % change in Sales |
| 13 | Effect of Change in Price | $\text{New P/V} = \dfrac{(S + \Delta S) - V}{(S + \Delta S)} \times 100$ | % | For price sensitivity analysis |
| 14 | Multi-product BEP | $\text{BEP Sales} = \dfrac{FC}{\text{Composite P/V Ratio}}$ | ₹ | Composite P/V uses weighted sales mix |
| 15 | Composite P/V Ratio | $\text{Composite P/V} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Sales}_i \times \text{P/V}_i)}{\sum \text{Sales}_i} \times 100$ | % | Weighted average across products |
| 16 | High-Low Method — Variable Cost | $V_{\text{per unit}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{high}} - C_{\text{low}}}{Q_{\text{high}} - Q_{\text{low}}}$ | ₹ / unit | Separates semi-variable cost |
| 17 | High-Low Method — Fixed Cost | $FC = C_{\text{high}} - (V \times Q_{\text{high}})$ | ₹ | Plug back into either extreme |
| 18 | Material Variance | $\text{MV} = (\text{Std Price} \times \text{Std Qty}) - (\text{Actual Price} \times \text{Actual Qty})$ | ₹ | Split into Price + Usage variance |
| 19 | Material Price Variance | $\text{MPV} = (\text{Std Price} - \text{Actual Price}) \times \text{Actual Qty}$ | ₹ | Responsibility: Purchase dept |
| 20 | Material Usage Variance | $\text{MUV} = (\text{Std Qty} - \text{Actual Qty}) \times \text{Std Price}$ | ₹ | Responsibility: Production dept |
