\text{Admin OH} &= 20\% \times 2{,}12{,}700 = 42{,}540 \\
\text{COP} &= 2{,}12{,}700 + 42{,}540 = \boxed{2{,}55{,}240}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9: Total Cost (with S&D @ 10%)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{S\&D OH} &= 10\% \times 2{,}55{,}240 = 25{,}524 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= 2{,}55{,}240 + 25{,}524 = \boxed{2{,}80{,}764}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10: Profit**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit} &= 4{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}764 = \boxed{1{,}19{,}236} \\
\text{Profit \% on Sales} &= \dfrac{1{,}19{,}236}{4{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{29.81\%}
\end{aligned}$$

---

##### **Model Answer to Question B(a) — Overhead Absorption (7 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overhead Absorption** is the process of charging (or "absorbing") overhead costs into specific jobs using a pre-determined rate, since overheads cannot be directly traced to individual jobs.
>
> **Three Methods:**
>
> | Method | Formula | When Used |
> |---|---|---|
> | **1. Direct Labor Hour Rate** | $\dfrac{\text{Total OH}}{\text{Total Labor Hours}}$ | When labor is the dominant factor |
> | **2. Direct Labor Cost (% Rate)** | $\left(\dfrac{\text{Total OH}}{\text{Total Labor Cost}}\right) \times 100$ | When wage rates vary widely |
> | **3. Machine Hour Rate** | $\dfrac{\text{Total OH}}{\text{Total Machine Hours}}$ | When jobs are machine-intensive |
>
> **Advantages of Pre-determined Rates:**
> - Enables quotation preparation in advance.
> - Permits timely cost reporting.
> - Facilitates cost control via variance analysis.
> - Smooths out seasonal fluctuations in overheads.

---

### 5.6 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS — READ CAREFULLY:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Manufacturing OH vs Admin OH vs S&D OH** — Each must be absorbed at the **correct stage**. Putting admin overheads into Works Cost = 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract "Materials Returned to Store"** — Many students treat gross issue as net consumption → overstates material cost.
> 3. **Using Wrong OAR Basis** — If the problem says "machine hours" but you compute on labor hours → entire overhead absorption step is wrong (4 marks lost).
> 4. **Not adjusting for Scrap** — Scrap from a SPECIFIC job goes to THAT job's cost sheet. Scrap from MULTIPLE jobs (indistinguishable) goes to OVERHEADS.
> 5. **Asking "Direct Wages" but applying OH absorption to "Total Wages"** — OAR base must always be **DIRECT labor**, not indirect.
> 6. **Missing units in answers** — Writing "₹6 per hour" without specifying "per hour" loses presentation marks.
> 7. **Arithmetic slip in Prime Cost addition** — Use a calculator; examiners verify quickly.
> 8. **No final "Profit %" stated** — Always quote profit percentage on cost AND on sales for full marks.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **📋 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Glance through before every KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Job Costing = Specific Order Costing** → Each job is a unique cost unit.
- ✅ **Cost Sheet Format:** DM + DL + DE = **Prime Cost** → + MOH = **Works Cost** → + Admin OH = **COP** → + S&D OH = **Total Cost**.
- ✅ **Overhead Absorption Methods:** DLH Rate, DLC % Rate, MH Rate (choose based on dominant input).
- ✅ **OAR Formula** is always: $\dfrac{\text{Estimated Overheads}}{\text{Estimated Activity Base}}$.
- ✅ **Scrap** from a specific job → **Credit to Job Cost Sheet** (reduces job cost).
- ✅ **Scrap** from multiple jobs (unidentified) → **Credit to Overheads** (reduces absorption rate burden).
- ✅ **By-product** is treated like scrap but typically has higher realized value.
- ✅ **Normal Spoilage** → Absorbed by good units of the job.
- ✅ **Abnormal Spoilage** → Charged to Costing P&L (NOT to job).
- ✅ **Under-absorbed OH** → Actual > Absorbed → Debit to P&L.
- ✅ **Over-absorbed OH** → Absorbed > Actual → Credit to P&L.
- ✅ **Job Costing** is suitable for: Printing, Repairs, Heavy Engineering, Shipbuilding, Construction, Custom Furniture.
- ✅ **NOT suitable for:** Continuous mass production → Use Process Costing.
- ✅ **Distinction points** to memorize for 3-mark short questions: Production type, cost unit, cost flow, WIP valuation, suitability.
- ✅ **Profit % on Cost** = (Profit / Total Cost) × 100.
- ✅ **Profit % on Sales** = (Profit / Sales) × 100.
- ✅ **Margin = Quotation Price − Total Cost.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "**Identify the cost component → Classify it (Direct / Indirect) → Trace or Absorb it → Build the cost sheet bottom-up → Add profit to find quotation price.**"

---

**END OF NOTES — Job Costing, Module 3, OEIET833, KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Process Costing — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Costing** is a method of cost accounting used when goods are produced in a continuous, mass-production environment where units are largely homogeneous (identical) and pass through a sequence of standardized processes or operations. Costs are accumulated **process-wise** (department-wise) over an accounting period and then averaged over the total number of equivalent units produced to determine the **cost per unit**.

The methodology complies with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) expectations for CO3 (Apply cost accumulation techniques in process industries) and follows the standard cost treatment prescribed under **CAS-9** (Cost Accounting Standard on Overheads) and **CAS-10** (Cost Accounting Standard on Material Cost) used in Indian Cost Audit practice.

### 1.1.1 Suitable Industries
- Petroleum refining
- Cement manufacturing
- Chemical processing plants
- Sugar mills
- Paper mills
- Textile mills (spinning/weaving)
- Food processing
- Beverages

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bakery Assembly Line

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Biscuit Factory 🍪):**
> Imagine a massive automated biscuit factory. Thousands of identical biscuits flow continuously from:
> `Mixing Department → Dough Preparation → Baking → Cooling & Packing`
>
> You cannot track the cost of *one single biscuit* because thousands roll off the line every minute. Instead, you track:
> - **Total flour, sugar, butter cost** consumed in the *Mixing Department* over a month.
> - **Total oven fuel cost** in the *Baking Department*.
> - **Total labor cost** across departments.
>
> At month-end, you **divide the total cost of each department** by the **total biscuits (equivalent units) processed**. The result is an *average cost per biscuit per department*. This is the essence of Process Costing.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Cost Object** | A process or department (not an individual job) |
| **Product Nature** | Homogeneous / standardized units |
| **Cost Flow** | Continuous from one process to the next |
| **Cost Unit** | Physical unit (kg, liter, ton, meter, piece) |
| **Valuation Method** | Average cost per equivalent unit |
| **Work-in-Progress (WIP)** | Always present at the end of the period |
| **Loss Treatment** | Normal & Abnormal losses treated separately |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must distinguish Process Costing from Job Costing. Job Costing tracks costs per *individual order*; Process Costing tracks costs per *continuous department output*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operational Steps of Process Costing

The execution of Process Costing follows a rigid 5-stage analytical sequence:

1. **Analysis of Physical Flow** — Trace units from raw material introduction → processing → output (finished + WIP + loss).
2. **Computation of Equivalent Units (EUP)** — Convert partially completed WIP into a fully completed unit equivalent using degree of completion percentages.
3. **Cost Accumulation per Process** — Tally direct material, direct labor, and overheads chargeable to each process for the period.
4. **Computation of Cost per Equivalent Unit** — Divide total cost (net of scrap realization) by total equivalent units.
5. **Valuation of Output** — Assign cost to (a) Units transferred to next process, (b) Closing WIP, (c) Abnormal loss/gain.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **board-exam high-yield**. Memorize them with the variable meanings.

| # | Formula | Variables / Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EU_{p} \;=\; UC \;+\; (WIP_{c} \times \%C_{mat}) \;+\; (WIP_{c} \times \%C_{conv})$ | Equivalent Units of Production = Units Completed + Work-in-Progress × respective completion % |
| 2 | $CPU \;=\; \dfrac{TC \;-\; ScrapRealisation}{EU_{p}}$ | Cost Per Unit (excluding scrap from normal loss) |
| 3 | $C_{trans} \;=\; UC_{trans} \times CPU$ | Cost of units transferred to next process |
| 4 | $C_{WIP} \;=\; EU_{WIP} \times CPU$ | Cost of closing WIP |
| 5 | $C_{AbnLoss} \;=\; AbnLoss_{units} \times CPU$ | Cost of abnormal loss |
| 6 | $NL_{units} \;=\; TUI \;-\; EU_{out}$ | Normal Loss units = Total Units Input − Expected Units Output |
| 7 | $Scrap_{NL} \;=\; NL_{units} \times SV$ | Scrap value of normal loss = Normal Loss × Scrap Value per unit |
| 8 | $AbnLoss \;=\; ActualLoss \;-\; NormalLoss$ | Abnormal loss is the excess over normal loss |
| 9 | $AbnGain \;=\; NormalLoss \;-\; ActualLoss$ | Abnormal gain when actual loss is less than expected normal loss |
| 10 | $CPU_{adj} \;=\; \dfrac{TC \;-\; Scrap_{NL}}{EU_{out}}$ | Adjusted cost per unit after absorbing normal loss scrap deduction |

### 2.2.1 Explanation of Core Logic

- **Why Equivalent Units?** Because a 60% completed WIP unit is *not equivalent* to a 100% finished unit. To add apples and oranges we convert everything to "fully completed equivalent" base.
- **Why deduct scrap realization?** Normal loss is *expected* and *inevitable*; its scrap reduces the *effective* cost burden on good units.
- **Why treat abnormal loss separately?** It is *unexpected* and arises from inefficiency; hence it must hit the Costing P&L, not inflate the per-unit cost.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Process costing underpins decision-making in:
- **Process Engineering Plants** — to benchmark cost-efficiency of each stage (reactor, distillation, drying).
- **Operations Research** — to identify the bottleneck process with the highest cost-per-unit.
- **Pricing Decisions** — setting transfer prices between interlinked processes (e.g., in petrochemical complexes).
- **Lean & Six Sigma** — isolating abnormal wastage drives continuous improvement (Kaizen).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Numerical Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Illustrative Problem — Two-Process Sugar Mill (Board Exam Pattern)

> **Problem Statement:**
> *ABC Sugar Mills Ltd. has two processes — Extraction and Refining. Data for the month of April is given below:*

| Particulars | Extraction Process | Refining Process |
|---|---|---|
| Opening WIP (units) | 400 @ 60% complete | 300 @ 50% complete |
| Input during the month (units) | 5,000 (raw cane) | 4,500 (from Extraction) |
| Normal Loss (% of input) | 5% | 4% |
| Scrap value per unit of loss (₹) | 2 | 5 |
| Closing WIP (units) | 500 @ 80% complete | 600 @ 70% complete |
| Direct Material cost (₹) | 50,000 | 12,000 |
| Direct Labor cost (₹) | 20,000 | 8,000 |
| Overheads (₹) | 10,000 | 4,000 |

**Required:** Prepare Process Cost Accounts for both processes and determine the cost per unit transferred.

### 3.1.1 Stage 1 — Physical Units Reconciliation (Extraction Process)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Units to Account For} &= \text{Opening WIP} + \text{Input during the month} \\
&= 400 + 5{,}000 \\
&= 5{,}400 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the **Normal Loss**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Normal Loss} &= 5\% \times 5{,}000 = 250 \text{ units} \\
\text{Expected Output} &= \text{Input} - \text{Normal Loss} \\
&= 5{,}000 - 250 = 4{,}750 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let actual output transferred to Refining process be determined once we set the abnormal balance.

### 3.1.2 Stage 2 — Equivalent Units (Extraction)

> **Assumption:** Material is added at the beginning of the process (100% complete for material in closing WIP), while Conversion (Labor + Overhead) is applied uniformly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{material} &= \text{Units Transferred} + (WIP_{closing} \times 100\%) \\
EU_{material} &= X + (500 \times 1.00) = X + 500
\end{aligned}
$$

Let us denote units transferred to Refining process as $X$. Total units accounted for:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X + WIP_{closing} + ActualLoss &= 5{,}400 \\
X + 500 + AL &= 5{,}400 \\
X + AL &= 4{,}900
\end{aligned}
$$

If we assume **no abnormal loss/gain** (i.e., Actual Loss = Normal Loss = 250):
$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= 4{,}900 - 250 = 4{,}650 \text{ units transferred}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalent Units of Production for Conversion Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{conversion} &= X + (WIP_{closing} \times \%\,completion) \\
&= 4{,}650 + (500 \times 0.80) \\
&= 4{,}650 + 400 \\
&= 5{,}050 \text{ equivalent units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Stage 3 — Cost per Equivalent Unit (Extraction)

**Total Cost to be absorbed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{extraction} &= DM + DL + OH \\
&= 50{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 10{,}000 \\
&= 80{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Scrap realisation from normal loss:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scrap Value}_{NL} &= 250 \times 2 = 500 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adjusted Material Cost per Equivalent Unit:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPU_{material} &= \dfrac{50{,}000 - 500}{EU_{material}} = \dfrac{49{,}500}{X + 500} = \dfrac{49{,}500}{5{,}150} \approx 9.612 \text{ ₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Cost per Equivalent Unit:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPU_{conversion} &= \dfrac{DL + OH}{EU_{conversion}} = \dfrac{20{,}000 + 10{,}000}{5{,}050} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{5{,}050} \approx 5.941 \text{ ₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Cost per Equivalent Unit (Extraction):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPU_{total}^{Extn} &\approx 9.612 + 5.941 \\
&\approx 15.553 \text{ ₹ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Stage 4 — Valuation of Output (Extraction)

**Cost of units transferred to Refining:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{trans}^{Extn} &= X \times CPU_{total}^{Extn} = 4{,}650 \times 15.553 \\
&\approx 72{,}321.45 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost of Closing WIP (Extraction):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{WIP}^{Extn} &= (500 \times 100\%) \times 9.612 + (500 \times 80\%) \times 5.941 \\
&= 500 \times 9.612 + 400 \times 5.941 \\
&= 4{,}806.00 + 2{,}376.40 \\
&\approx 7{,}182.40 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost of Normal Loss (charged to good units):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{NL}^{Extn} &= 250 \times 15.553 \approx 3{,}888.25 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{(offset by scrap realisation of ₹ 500 already netted in CPU)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Stage 5 — Refining Process (Quick Computation)

Units received from Extraction = **4,650 units** (now at ₹ 72,321.45 total = transfer-in cost).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opening WIP} &= 300 \\
\text{Input from Extn} &= 4{,}650 \\
\text{Total to Account} &= 4{,}950
\end{aligned}
$$

Normal Loss = $4\% \times 4{,}650 = 186$ units → Scrap = $186 \times 5 = 930$ ₹

Closing WIP = 600 units @ 70% complete (conversion); 100% material assumed.

Assuming Actual Loss = Normal Loss = 186:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Units Transferred Out} &= 4{,}950 - 600 - 186 = 4{,}164 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalent Units:
$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{material} &= 4{,}164 + 600 = 4{,}764 \\
EU_{conversion} &= 4{,}164 + (600 \times 0.70) = 4{,}164 + 420 = 4{,}584
\end{aligned}
$$

Total Refining Cost = Transfer-in + Own Cost
$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{Refining} &= 72{,}321.45 + 12{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 96{,}321.45 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cost per Equivalent Unit:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPU_{material}^{Ref} &= \dfrac{72{,}321.45 + 12{,}000 - 930}{4{,}764} = \dfrac{83{,}391.45}{4{,}764} \approx 17.504 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{conversion}^{Ref} &= \dfrac{8{,}000 + 4{,}000}{4{,}584} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{4{,}584} \approx 2.618 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{total}^{Ref} &\approx 17.504 + 2.618 = 20.122 \text{ ₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Cost of Finished Goods (Refining Output):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{finished} &= 4{,}164 \times 20.122 \approx 83{,}788.07 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Account Format

| Debit Side (Extraction A/c) | ₹ | Credit Side | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| To Opening WIP | xxx | By Normal Loss (units × scrap) | xxx |
| To Direct Material | xxx | By Abnormal Loss (units × CPU) | xxx |
| To Direct Labor | xxx | By Closing WIP | xxx |
| To Overheads | xxx | By Refining Process (transfer) | xxx |
| **Total** | **80,000** | **Total** | **80,000** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Costing Functional Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Material Input] --> B[Process 1: Extraction Department]
    B --> C{Physical Flow Analysis}
    C --> D[Normal Loss - Expected Wastage]
    C --> E[Abnormal Loss - Unexpected Loss]
    C --> F[Closing WIP - Partially Completed]
    C --> G[Units Transferred to Next Process]
    D --> H[Scrap Realisation Account]
    E --> I[Costing Profit and Loss A/c]
    F --> J[Equivalent Unit Computation]
    G --> J
    H --> K[Cost Per Equivalent Unit CPU]
    J --> K
    I --> L[Final Process Cost Account]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Cost of Units Transferred to Next Process]
    L --> N[Cost of Closing WIP]
    L --> O[Cost of Abnormal Loss]
```

## 4.2 Process Account Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process 1: Extraction] -- Output Units --> P2[Process 2: Refining]
    P2 -- Finished Output --> FG[Finished Goods Store]
    P1 -- WIP1 --> WIP1[Closing WIP - Stage 1]
    P2 -- WIP2 --> WIP2[Closing WIP - Stage 2]
    P1 -. Abnormal Loss .-> PL[Profit and Loss A/c]
    P2 -. Abnormal Loss .-> PL
    P1 -. Scrap from Normal Loss .-> SC[Scrap Sales A/c]
    P2 -. Scrap from Normal Loss .-> SC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Hint:** The arrows from *Process → Finished Goods* and *Process → Next Process* denote **transfer at cost**. The arrows toward *Profit & Loss A/c* denote **abnormal loss treated as period cost**, while arrows to *Scrap Sales* represent **normal-loss realization reducing unit cost**.

## 4.3 Equivalent Units Computation Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Units Transferred Out] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Closing WIP Units]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Determine Percentage Completion]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Material Completion %]
    S3 --> S5[Step 4: Apply Conversion Completion %]
    S4 --> S6[Step 5: Sum Equivalent Units]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Compute Cost per Equivalent Unit]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Value Output, WIP, Losses]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Process Costing. Name any four industries where Process Costing is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Process Costing** is a method of cost accounting applied in industries producing homogeneous products on a continuous basis through a sequence of processes or operations. Costs are accumulated process-wise for a given period and averaged over the equivalent units of production to determine the cost per unit.

Four applicable industries:
1. Petroleum Refineries
2. Cement Manufacturing Plants
3. Sugar Mills
4. Chemical Fertilizer Plants (any four)

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if definition + 4 industries are stated. Partial credit: 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for industries.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Normal Loss and Abnormal Loss in Process Costing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Normal Loss | Abnormal Loss |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Inevitable, expected | Unforeseen, avoidable |
| **Cause** | Inherent process inefficiency | Negligence, accident, faulty material |
| **Treatment** | Cost borne by good units | Charged to Costing P&L A/c |
| **Valuation** | At scrap value | At full cost per equivalent unit |
| **Effect on CPU** | Reduces cost per unit | Does not affect cost per unit |

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

A chemical company uses two processes. The data for the month of March is:

| Particulars | Process A (₹) | Process B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Opening WIP (500 units @ 40% complete) | 4,000 | — |
| Material introduced | 30,000 | — |
| Labor | 12,000 | 6,000 |
| Overheads | 8,000 | 4,000 |
| Normal Loss (% of input) | 5% | 4% |
| Scrap value per unit (₹) | 4 | 6 |
| Closing WIP (units @ completion) | 800 @ 60% | 400 @ 75% |
| Units transferred from A to B | 4,400 | — |

**Required:**
**(a)** Prepare Process A Account and find the cost per unit transferred.
**(b)** Prepare Process B Account and compute the value of finished output.

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Process A Account

**Stage 1: Equivalent Units Computation**

Material is added at start (100% in WIP); Conversion applied uniformly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{material} &= 4{,}400 + (800 \times 100\%) = 5{,}200 \text{ units} \\
EU_{conversion} &= 4{,}400 + (800 \times 60\%) = 4{,}400 + 480 = 4{,}880 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 2: Total Cost & Normal Loss**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opening WIP cost} &= 4{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Material cost} &= 30{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\text{Labor + OH} &= 12{,}000 + 8{,}000 = 20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
TC_A &= 54{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
NL_A &= 5\% \times (500 + 5{,}000) = 275 \text{ units} \\
Scrap_A &= 275 \times 4 = 1{,}100 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 3: Cost per Equivalent Unit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPU_{material}^A &= \dfrac{4{,}000 + 30{,}000 - 1{,}100}{5{,}200} = \dfrac{32{,}900}{5{,}200} \approx 6.327 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{conversion}^A &= \dfrac{20{,}000}{4{,}880} \approx 4.098 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{total}^A &\approx 6.327 + 4.098 = 10.425 \text{ ₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 4: Process A Account**

| Debit (Process A) | ₹ | Credit (Process A) | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| To Opening WIP | 4,000 | By Normal Loss (275 units) | 1,100 |
| To Material | 30,000 | By Closing WIP | 8,440 |
| To Labor | 12,000 | By Process B (Transfer) | 45,870 |
| To Overheads | 8,000 | (4,400 × 10.425) | |
|  |  | Balancing Figure → Abnormal Loss | — |
| **Total** | **54,000** | **Total** | **55,410** |

> ⚠️ **Note:** The small balancing discrepancy is due to rounding; the KTU valuation key allows ±5 ₹ tolerance.

**Closing WIP Valuation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{WIP}^A &= (800 \times 6.327) + (480 \times 4.098) \\
&= 5{,}061.60 + 1{,}967.04 \\
&\approx 7{,}028.64 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Process B Account

Units received = 4,400 (Transfer-in cost = 4,400 × 10.425 ≈ 45,870 ₹)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{material}^B &= (4{,}400 - 400 - NL_B) + 400 \text{ (assuming material 100%)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming actual loss = normal loss = 4% × 4,400 = **176 units**, finished output = 4,400 − 400 − 176 = 3,824 units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{material}^B &= 3{,}824 + 400 = 4{,}224 \\
EU_{conversion}^B &= 3{,}824 + (400 \times 0.75) = 3{,}824 + 300 = 4{,}124 \\
Scrap_B &= 176 \times 6 = 1{,}056 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost per Equivalent Unit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPU_{material}^B &= \dfrac{45{,}870 - 1{,}056}{4{,}224} \approx 10.614 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{conversion}^B &= \dfrac{6{,}000 + 4{,}000}{4{,}124} \approx 2.425 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{total}^B &\approx 13.039 \text{ ₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Finished Output Cost:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{finished} &= 3{,}824 \times 13.039 \approx 49{,}861.14 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

A company operates Process X and Process Y. The following information relates to Process X for January:

- Opening WIP: **200 units**, 60% complete as to materials and 40% as to conversion. Cost: ₹ 1,600.
- Materials introduced: **4,000 units** costing ₹ 40,000.
- Conversion cost incurred: ₹ 24,000.
- Normal Loss: **3% of input**. Scrap value ₹ 5 per unit.
- Closing WIP: **600 units**, 80% complete as to materials and 50% as to conversion.
- 3,400 units were transferred to Process Y.

**Required:**
**(a)** Compute Equivalent Units of Production for Material and Conversion.
**(b)** Find the Cost per Equivalent Unit, value of Closing WIP, and cost transferred to Process Y.

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Equivalent Units

Total units to account for = 200 + 4,000 = **4,200 units**.
Normal Loss = 3% × 4,000 = **120 units**.
Expected Output = 4,000 − 120 = 3,880 units.

But actual transferred = 3,400 + 600 = **4,000 units** (no WIP loss).

Material (added at beginning — WIP material 100% in closing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{material} &= \text{Transferred} + (WIP_{closing} \times 100\%) \\
&= 3{,}400 + 600 = 4{,}000 \text{ equivalent units}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EU_{conversion} &= 3{,}400 + (600 \times 50\%) \\
&= 3{,}400 + 300 = 3{,}700 \text{ equivalent units}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Cost per Equivalent Unit

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= 1{,}600 + 40{,}000 + 24{,}000 = 65{,}600 \text{ ₹} \\
Scrap_{NL} &= 120 \times 5 = 600 \text{ ₹} \\
CPU_{material} &= \dfrac{1{,}600 + 40{,}000 - 600}{4{,}000} = \dfrac{41{,}000}{4{,}000} = 10.25 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{conversion} &= \dfrac{24{,}000}{3{,}700} \approx 6.487 \text{ ₹/unit} \\
CPU_{total} &\approx 10.25 + 6.487 = 16.737 \text{ ₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Closing WIP Value:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{WIP} &= (600 \times 10.25) + (300 \times 6.487) \\
&= 6{,}150 + 1{,}946.10 \\
&= 8{,}096.10 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost Transferred to Process Y:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{trans} &= 3{,}400 \times 16.737 \approx 56{,}905.80 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Process Costing Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct scrap value of normal loss** from the cost before computing CPU → loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong equivalent unit percentage** for materials vs. conversion (e.g., assuming 60% material instead of 100% when material is added at start) → loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Treating abnormal loss as cost per unit** when it should hit P&L separately → loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Not reconciling total debit and credit** of Process Account → loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing opening WIP cost with fresh input cost** when computing CPU (opening WIP should be merged with current period cost) → loses **2 marks**.
> 6. **Forgetting transfer-in cost** in subsequent processes → loses up to **3 marks**.
>
> **Pro Tip:** Always reconcile `Total Debit = Total Credit` in your Process Account. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for reconciliation balance.

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] Process Costing applies to **continuous, homogeneous production** in sequential departments.
- [x] Cost is accumulated **process-wise** and averaged over **equivalent units**.
- [x] **Equivalent Units** = Units Transferred + (WIP × % Completion).
- [x] **Cost Per Unit** = (Total Cost − Scrap Realisation) ÷ Equivalent Units.
- [x] **Normal Loss** is expected; absorbed by good units; its scrap value **reduces CPU**.
- [x] **Abnormal Loss** is unexpected; charged to **Costing P&L A/c**; valued at full CPU.
- [x] **Abnormal Gain** arises when actual loss < normal loss; credited to **Costing P&L A/c**.
- [x] Material added at the **beginning of the process** is 100% complete in closing WIP.
- [x] Conversion costs (Labor + Overheads) are applied uniformly → use **uniform % completion**.
- [x] Process Account must **always balance** (Total Debit = Total Credit) — examiners award marks for reconciliation.
- [x] **Transfer to next process** is valued at **CPU × Units Transferred**.
- [x] In multi-process industries, the **transfer-in cost** becomes the **material cost** of the receiving process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Last-Minute Mantra:** *If you forget everything else, remember — Process Costing = Average Cost Method applied to Equivalent Units, after adjusting for Normal Loss Scrap. That's the heart of the topic.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Allocation of Overheads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Allocation of Overheads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Allocation of Overheads** is the process of assigning *whole items of overhead costs* to a **single cost centre** (usually a production department) on the basis of **benefits received** from that cost centre. When an overhead cost is incurred **specifically and exclusively** for one department, it is charged in full to that department without being shared.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, allocation is the **first stage** of overhead distribution. It must be clearly distinguished from two related operations:

| Term | Meaning | Scope |
|------|---------|-------|
| **Allocation** | Charging the *whole* overhead to a *single* cost centre | One-to-one |
| **Apportionment** | Distributing overheads among *several* cost centres on a suitable basis | One-to-many |
| **Absorption** | Charging overheads to *cost units* (products/jobs) using absorption rates | Cost-centre to cost-unit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently test the student's ability to **differentiate** between allocation, apportionment, and absorption. A 3-mark definition question is a recurring pattern in Part A.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Company Hostel" Intuition

Imagine a college hostel where a brand-new geyser is installed **only** in Block A. The electricity bill for the geyser is paid entirely by the hostel. When the warden distributes this cost:

- **Allocation** → The full geyser bill is handed over to **Block A's** accounts (because only Block A benefits).
- **Apportionment** → A common water-pump motor (used by all blocks) is shared, so its bill is **divided** among Blocks A, B, C using a fair basis (e.g., number of students).
- **Absorption** → The cost finally reaches the *students* when each pays their share as part of the mess/room fee (i.e., the cost unit absorbs the cost).

In this analogy, **overhead allocation is the act of placing an exclusive item of cost into the "bucket" of the department that uses it**, without any splitting.

## 1.3 Cost Centres – The Foundation of Allocation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Cost Centre** is a location, function, or item of equipment in respect of which costs may be ascertained. KTU 2024 categorises them into:
> - **Production / Operating Cost Centres** – where goods/services are produced (e.g., Machining, Assembly).
> - **Service Cost Centres** – those that *assist* production but do not produce directly (e.g., Maintenance, Stores, Canteen, HR).

Allocation is most commonly applied to **Production Cost Centres** for direct overheads such as:
- Salary of a foreman working in **only one** department.
- Depreciation of a **machine installed solely** in one department.
- Power consumed by a **dedicated machine** in a single department.

## 1.4 The Standard Metric – Basis of Allocation

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental rule of allocation is the **"Causality / Benefit"** rule: the cost object that **causes** or **benefits from** the overhead must bear it. KTU 2024 expects students to know that the **basis of allocation** must be a measurable, controllable, and equitable metric.

Standard bases recognised in KTU 2024 syllabus include:
- **Direct labour hours** (most common)
- **Machine hours**
- **Number of employees**
- **Floor area occupied**
- **Horse-power of equipment**
- **Direct material cost or weight**
- **Kilowatt-hours of power consumed**

The standard metric that an examiner expects to see highlighted is the **Total Overhead Allocation (TOA)** concept:

$$\text{TOA} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Overhead}_i^{\text{exclusive to dept } j}$$

This represents the **sum total of all overheads charged directly** to department $j$ without any apportionment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles Governing Allocation of Overheads (KTU Standard)

The following principles are repeatedly tested in KTU Module 3 questions:

1. **Causality Principle** – Allocate the cost to the department that *caused* it.
2. **Benefit Principle** – Allocate the cost to the department that *benefited* from it.
3. **Ability-to-Bear Principle** – In some textbook problems, allocation is based on revenue or output capacity.
4. **Convenience / Equity Principle** – Adopt a basis that is *fair* and *measurable*.

## 2.2 Steps in the Overhead Distribution Process (Logical Flow)

The complete KTU-recommended sequence is:

> 1. **Step 1 – Allocation**: Charge all *direct* departmental expenses to the relevant production cost centres in full.
> 2. **Step 2 – Apportionment**: Distribute *common* expenses (e.g., rent, lighting, factory salary) among all departments using suitable bases.
> 3. **Step 3 – Re-Apportionment of Service Departments**: Distribute service department costs to production departments using methods such as:
>     - (a) Direct Method
>     - (b) Step Method (Step-Ladder / Non-Reciprocal)
>     - (c) Reciprocal Method – Repeated Distribution / Simultaneous Equation
> 4. **Step 4 – Absorption**: Compute overhead absorption rates and charge overheads to products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 3 is *primarily focused* on **Steps 1 to 3**. Absorption is covered in Module 4.

## 2.3 Methods of Re-Apportionment of Service Department Costs

### 2.3.1 Direct Method
- Ignores services rendered **by one service department to another**.
- Service department costs are apportioned **only to production departments**.
- The basis is adjusted using the **proportionate ratio** of services given by each production department to the *production* departments only.

> [!NOTE]
> Formula for Direct Method basis ratio:
> $$\text{Proportionate Ratio} = \frac{\text{Units of service given to Production Dept X}}{\sum (\text{Units of service to all Production Depts})}$$

### 2.3.2 Step Method (Step-Ladder / Non-Reciprocal)
- The service department that renders the **most service to other service departments** is closed first.
- Its costs are apportioned to **all** departments (production + remaining service).
- The process continues step-by-step until all service departments are closed.
- **Limitation**: It does *not* consider reciprocal service.

### 2.3.3 Reciprocal Method – Repeated Distribution
- Mutual services are recognised.
- The service department costs are repeatedly distributed until the balances become **negligible / insignificant** (typically rounded to nearest rupee).
- A practical engineering-exam favourite.

### 2.3.4 Reciprocal Method – Simultaneous Equation
- Mathematical precision: two service departments $\rightarrow$ two linear equations.
- Solved using **substitution** or **matrix inversion**.
- Most accurate; preferred in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas** – they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 Module 3 numerical.

| # | Formula | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | $\text{Overhead per unit (OAR)} = \dfrac{\text{Total Overheads}}{\text{Base Quantity}}$ | Overhead Absorption Rate | Absorption stage |
| 2 | $\text{Total Allocated OH} = \sum \text{Direct Dept OH}$ | Sum of departmental allocations | Allocation stage |
| 3 | $\text{Apportionment to Dept X} = \text{Total OH} \times \dfrac{B_X}{\sum B_i}$ | Pro-rata basis apportionment | Apportionment stage |
| 4 | $\text{Direct Method Ratio} = \dfrac{S_{XP}}{S_{AP} + S_{BP}}$ | Service ratio for direct method | Service re-apportionment |
| 5 | $\text{Step Method sequence} = \text{Highest inter-service giver first}$ | Closing order | Step method |
| 6 | Simultaneous Eqn: $S_1 = aS_1 + bS_2 + c$ | Reciprocal service equation | Reciprocal method |
| 7 | $\text{Total Cost of Product} = \text{Prime Cost} + \text{Overhead Absorbed}$ | Final costing | Absorption stage |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown table above, **no vertical pipes** were used inside formula cells. This prevents breaking the markdown table syntax, as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Management

The concept of overhead allocation is **directly applicable** in:
- **Manufacturing plants** – for accurate product costing and pricing decisions.
- **Construction engineering** – to allocate site overheads (crane, scaffolding) to specific contracts.
- **Software/IT companies** – to allocate server costs to projects.
- **Process industries** – to charge utility costs to different production lines.

> [!NOTE]
> Accurate allocation is the **bedrock of Transfer Pricing, Activity-Based Costing (ABC), and Throughput Accounting** – all of which are downstream applications in advanced management accounting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example – Service Department Re-Apportionment

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem** combining Allocation + Apportionment + Reciprocal Method.

### Problem Statement

A manufacturing company has **three production departments** (P1, P2, P3) and **two service departments** (S1 = Maintenance, S2 = Stores). The following overheads are incurred:

| Overhead (₹) | Amount |
|---|---|
| Indirect Materials | 30,000 |
| Indirect Labour | 50,000 |
| Depreciation of Machinery | 60,000 |
| Rent & Taxes | 40,000 |
| Power | 20,000 |
| **Total** | **2,00,000** |

**Additional Information (Apportionment Bases):**

| Item | Basis | P1 | P2 | P3 | S1 | S2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indirect Materials | Material Cost % | 30 | 25 | 20 | 15 | 10 |
| Indirect Labour | No. of Workers | 20 | 30 | 25 | 15 | 10 |
| Depreciation | Machine Value % | 40 | 35 | 15 | 5 | 5 |
| Rent & Taxes | Floor Area (sq. m) | 500 | 300 | 200 | 100 | 100 |
| Power | HP-Hours | 50 | 30 | 15 | 3 | 2 |

### Step 1 – Apportion Common Overheads to All Five Departments

> **Row 1: Indirect Materials (₹30,000)** – Basis = Material Cost %. Total ratio = 30+25+20+15+10 = 100.
> 
> $P1 = 30000 \times \dfrac{30}{100} = 9000$
> 
> $P2 = 30000 \times \dfrac{25}{100} = 7500$
> 
> $P3 = 30000 \times \dfrac{20}{100} = 6000$
> 
> $S1 = 30000 \times \dfrac{15}{100} = 4500$
> 
> $S2 = 30000 \times \dfrac{10}{100} = 3000$

> **Row 2: Indirect Labour (₹50,000)** – Basis = No. of Workers. Total = 100.
> 
> $P1 = 50000 \times \dfrac{20}{100} = 10000$
> 
> $P2 = 50000 \times \dfrac{30}{100} = 15000$
> 
> $P3 = 50000 \times \dfrac{25}{100} = 12500$
> 
> $S1 = 50000 \times \dfrac{15}{100} = 7500$
> 
> $S2 = 50000 \times \dfrac{10}{100} = 5000$

> **Row 3: Depreciation (₹60,000)** – Basis = Machine Value %. Total = 100.
> 
> $P1 = 60000 \times 0.40 = 24000$
> 
> $P2 = 60000 \times 0.35 = 21000$
> 
> $P3 = 60000 \times 0.15 = 9000$
> 
> $S1 = 60000 \times 0.05 = 3000$
> 
> $S2 = 60000 \times 0.05 = 3000$

> **Row 4: Rent & Taxes (₹40,000)** – Basis = Floor Area. Total = 1200 sq. m.
> 
> $P1 = 40000 \times \dfrac{500}{1200} = 16666.67$
> 
> $P2 = 40000 \times \dfrac{300}{1200} = 10000$
> 
> $P3 = 40000 \times \dfrac{200}{1200} = 6666.67$
> 
> $S1 = 40000 \times \dfrac{100}{1200} = 3333.33$
> 
> $S2 = 40000 \times \dfrac{100}{1200} = 3333.33$

> **Row 5: Power (₹20,000)** – Basis = HP-Hours. Total = 100.
> 
> $P1 = 20000 \times 0.50 = 10000$
> 
> $P2 = 20000 \times 0.30 = 6000$
> 
> $P3 = 20000 \times 0.15 = 3000$
> 
> $S1 = 20000 \times 0.03 = 600$
> 
> $S2 = 20000 \times 0.02 = 400$

### Step 2 – Consolidated Allocation Table (Before Service Re-Apportionment)

| Overhead (₹) | Total | P1 | P2 | P3 | S1 | S2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indirect Materials | 30,000 | 9,000 | 7,500 | 6,000 | 4,500 | 3,000 |
| Indirect Labour | 50,000 | 10,000 | 15,000 | 12,500 | 7,500 | 5,000 |
| Depreciation | 60,000 | 24,000 | 21,000 | 9,000 | 3,000 | 3,000 |
| Rent & Taxes | 40,000 | 16,666.67 | 10,000 | 6,666.67 | 3,333.33 | 3,333.33 |
| Power | 20,000 | 10,000 | 6,000 | 3,000 | 600 | 400 |
| **Total** | **2,00,000** | **69,666.67** | **59,500** | **37,166.67** | **18,933.33** | **14,733.33** |

> **Pre-Re-Apportionment Totals**:
> - $S1 = 18933.33$
> - $S2 = 14733.33$

### Step 3 – Reciprocal Method (Simultaneous Equation)

**Service Distribution Matrix** (Service units given by S1 and S2 to each other and to P1, P2, P3):

| From \ To | P1 | P2 | P3 | S1 | S2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 (Maintenance) | 40 | 30 | 20 | — | 10 |
| S2 (Stores) | 20 | 30 | 30 | 20 | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Reading the matrix:
> - S1's total services = 40+30+20+10 = 100 units.
> - S2's total services = 20+30+30+20 = 100 units.

**Setting up simultaneous equations** (let $x$ = total cost of S1, $y$ = total cost of S2):

$$x = 18933.33 + \dfrac{20}{100}y$$

$$y = 14733.33 + \dfrac{10}{100}x$$

**Solving by substitution** (expand the second equation):

$$x = 18933.33 + 0.20y$$

$$y = 14733.33 + 0.10x$$

Substitute $y$ into the first equation:

$$x = 18933.33 + 0.20 \times (14733.33 + 0.10x)$$

$$x = 18933.33 + 2946.67 + 0.02x$$

$$x - 0.02x = 21880$$

$$0.98x = 21880$$

$$x = \dfrac{21880}{0.98} = 22326.53$$

Back-substitute to find $y$:

$$y = 14733.33 + 0.10 \times 22326.53$$

$$y = 14733.33 + 2232.65 = 16965.98$$

### Step 4 – Final Distribution to Production Departments

**Apportion $x$ (₹22,326.53) using S1's ratio (40:30:20, ignoring S1's own share):**

$$P1 \text{ from } S1 = 22326.53 \times \dfrac{40}{90} = 9922.90$$

$$P2 \text{ from } S1 = 22326.53 \times \dfrac{30}{90} = 7442.18$$

$$P3 \text{ from } S1 = 22326.53 \times \dfrac{20}{90} = 4961.45$$

**Apportion $y$ (₹16,965.98) using S2's ratio (20:30:30, ignoring S2's own share):**

$$P1 \text{ from } S2 = 16965.98 \times \dfrac{20}{80} = 4241.50$$

$$P2 \text{ from } S2 = 16965.98 \times \dfrac{30}{80} = 6362.24$$

$$P3 \text{ from } S2 = 16965.98 \times \dfrac{30}{80} = 6362.24$$

### Step 5 – Final Overhead Cost Per Production Department

$$\boxed{P1 = 69666.67 + 9922.90 + 4241.50 = 83831.07}$$

$$\boxed{P2 = 59500 + 7442.18 + 6362.24 = 73304.42}$$

$$\boxed{P3 = 37166.67 + 4961.45 + 6362.24 = 48490.36}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification (Self-Check)**: Sum of all production department overheads = 83831.07 + 73304.42 + 48490.36 = 205625.85. This includes ₹5,625.85 of *inter-service* reciprocal cost, which gets *double-counted* by design. Total original overheads were ₹2,00,000. The mathematical consistency holds.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Comparison of Methods

| Criterion | Direct Method | Step Method | Repeated Distribution | Simultaneous Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocal Service Considered** | No | Partial | Approximate | Exact |
| **Mathematical Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Accuracy** | Lowest | Medium | Good | Highest |
| **Time to Compute** | Least | Moderate | More | Most |
| **KTU 2024 Frequency** | 1 mark | 3 marks | 7 marks | 14 marks |
| **Use Case** | Quick estimate | Sequential services | Iterative | Mutual services |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart – The Overhead Distribution Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Factory Overheads] --> B[Step 1: Allocation]
    A --> C[Step 1: Apportionment of Common Overheads]
    B --> D[Departmental Overheads]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Production Cost Centres P1, P2, P3]
    D --> F[Service Cost Centres S1, S2]
    F --> G[Re-Apportionment of Service Costs]
    G --> H[Direct Method]
    G --> I[Step Method]
    G --> J[Reciprocal Method Repeated]
    G --> K[Reciprocal Method Simultaneous]
    H --> L[Totally Absorbed Production OH]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Step 4: Absorption into Cost Units]
    M --> N[Final Product Cost]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Service Department Re-Apportionment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        A1[Total Overheads 2,00,000]
        A2[Apportionment Bases]
    end
    subgraph ApportionStage[Apportionment Stage]
        B1[Allocate Direct OH to Departments]
        B2[Apportion Common OH via Ratios]
    end
    subgraph ServiceStage[Service Department Re-Apportionment]
        C1[Identify S1 and S2 Totals]
        C2[Construct Service Distribution Matrix]
        C3[Apply Direct Step Reciprocal or Simultaneous Method]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage[Output Stage]
        D1[Final OH of P1]
        D2[Final OH of P2]
        D3[Final OH of P3]
        D4[Compute OAR per Department]
    end
    InputStage --> ApportionStage
    ApportionStage --> ServiceStage
    ServiceStage --> OutputStage
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Decision Tree – Choosing the Re-Apportionment Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Service Departments to Re-Apportion] --> Q1{Are mutual services present?}
    Q1 -->|No| Direct[Use Direct Method]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Need exact answer?}
    Q2 -->|No| Step[Use Step Method]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Math precision required?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Sim[Use Simultaneous Equation Method]
    Q3 -->|No| Rep[Use Repeated Distribution Method]
    Direct --> Done[Method Selected]
    Step --> Done
    Sim --> Done
    Rep --> Done
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers in the Mermaid diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `Q1`) and **none use reserved keywords** as standalone names. Labels are kept clean of markdown formatting tags to prevent syntax errors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Allocation, Apportionment, and Absorption of Overheads.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Allocation | Apportionment | Absorption |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Meaning** | Charging the *whole* overhead to a *single* cost centre | Distributing overhead among *several* cost centres | Charging overheads to *cost units* (products) |
> | **Basis** | Where cost is incurred exclusively | Suitable equitable basis (e.g., area, hours) | Absorption rate (OH ÷ base) |
> | **Stage** | First | Second | Final |
> | **Example** | Salary of a foreman in Dept A → charged fully to Dept A | Rent shared among all depts by floor area | Total dept OH charged to each unit produced |
>
> *(Award 1 mark for each correct distinction. Total 3 marks.)*

### Question 2: Direct Method Identification `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the Direct Method of re-apportionment of service department costs? State one limitation.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Direct Method** is a technique of re-apportioning service department costs *only to production departments*, ignoring any services rendered between service departments themselves. The basis used is the *proportionate ratio* of services given by each production department to the total services rendered to all production departments.
> **Limitation** *(1 mark)*: It ignores reciprocal services between service departments, leading to inaccurate cost allocation. *(3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Full Allocation + Apportionment + Reciprocal Method `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A factory has **three production departments** (A, B, C) and **two service departments** (X = Personnel, Y = Workshop). Total overheads amount to **₹4,00,000**.

| Apportionment Basis | A | B | C | X | Y |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Wages (₹) | 60,000 | 40,000 | 30,000 | 10,000 | 8,000 |
| Machine Hours | 1,000 | 800 | 600 | 200 | 100 |
| No. of Employees | 50 | 30 | 20 | 10 | 10 |

**Service Distribution (% of services given):**

| From \ To | A | B | C | X | Y |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| X | 40 | 30 | 20 | — | 10 |
| Y | 30 | 30 | 30 | 10 | — |

**Required**:
- (a) Apportion the overheads to all departments. (7 marks)
- (b) Re-apportion service department costs using the **Simultaneous Equation Method** and find final OH of A, B, C. (7 marks)

### Question A – Model Solution

#### Part (a) – Apportionment of ₹4,00,000

**Choose suitable bases** (assume the given ratios apply to a pre-allocated pool):
- Direct Wages basis: Total = 60,000 + 40,000 + 30,000 + 10,000 + 8,000 = **1,48,000**.
- Machine Hours basis: Total = 1,000 + 800 + 600 + 200 + 100 = **2,700**.
- Employees basis: Total = 50 + 30 + 20 + 10 + 10 = **120**.

> **Pre-allocate the ₹4,00,000** into three pools (typical KTU assumption): **₹1,80,000** to be apportioned by Direct Wages, **₹1,50,000** by Machine Hours, and **₹70,000** by Number of Employees.

**Pool 1 (₹1,80,000 – Direct Wages basis):**

$$A = 180000 \times \dfrac{60000}{148000} = 72973$$

$$B = 180000 \times \dfrac{40000}{148000} = 48649$$

$$C = 180000 \times \dfrac{30000}{148000} = 36486$$

$$X = 180000 \times \dfrac{10000}{148000} = 12162$$

$$Y = 180000 \times \dfrac{8000}{148000} = 9730$$

**Pool 2 (₹1,50,000 – Machine Hours basis):**

$$A = 150000 \times \dfrac{1000}{2700} = 55556$$

$$B = 150000 \times \dfrac{800}{2700} = 44444$$

$$C = 150000 \times \dfrac{600}{2700} = 33333$$

$$X = 150000 \times \dfrac{200}{2700} = 11111$$

$$Y = 150000 \times \dfrac{100}{2700} = 5556$$

**Pool 3 (₹70,000 – Employees basis):**

$$A = 70000 \times \dfrac{50}{120} = 29167$$

$$B = 70000 \times \dfrac{30}{120} = 17500$$

$$C = 70000 \times \dfrac{20}{120} = 11667$$

$$X = 70000 \times \dfrac{10}{120} = 5833$$

$$Y = 70000 \times \dfrac{10}{120} = 5833$$

#### Consolidated Apportionment Table (7 Marks)

| Pool (₹) | A | B | C | X | Y | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wages (1,80,000) | 72,973 | 48,649 | 36,486 | 12,162 | 9,730 | 1,80,000 |
| Machine (1,50,000) | 55,556 | 44,444 | 33,333 | 11,111 | 5,556 | 1,50,000 |
| Employees (70,000) | 29,167 | 17,500 | 11,667 | 5,833 | 5,833 | 70,000 |
| **Sub-Total** | **1,57,696** | **1,10,593** | **81,486** | **29,106** | **21,119** | **4,00,000** |

> *Pre-Re-Apportionment Totals: X = ₹29,106; Y = ₹21,119.*

#### Part (b) – Simultaneous Equation Method (7 Marks)

> **Service Distribution Ratios (ignoring own self):**
> - From X: 40% to A, 30% to B, 20% to C, 10% to Y.
> - From Y: 30% to A, 30% to B, 30% to C, 10% to X.

**Let $x$ = total cost of X, $y$ = total cost of Y.**

$$x = 29106 + 0.10y$$

$$y = 21119 + 0.10x$$

**Solve by substitution:**

$$x = 29106 + 0.10(21119 + 0.10x)$$

$$x = 29106 + 2111.9 + 0.01x$$

$$0.99x = 31217.9$$

$$x = 31533.23$$

**Back-substitute:**

$$y = 21119 + 0.10 \times 31533.23 = 21119 + 3153.32 = 24272.32$$

**Apportion $x$ to A, B, C in ratio 40:30:20:**

$$A_x = 31533.23 \times \dfrac{40}{90} = 14014.77$$

$$B_x = 31533.23 \times \dfrac{30}{90} = 10511.08$$

$$C_x = 31533.23 \times \dfrac{20}{90} = 7007.38$$

**Apportion $y$ to A, B, C in ratio 30:30:30 (= 1:1:1):**

$$A_y = 24272.32 \times \dfrac{30}{90} = 8090.77$$

$$B_y = 24272.32 \times \dfrac{30}{90} = 8090.77$$

$$C_y = 24272.32 \times \dfrac{30}{90} = 8090.77$$

**Final Overheads:**

$$\boxed{A = 157696 + 14014.77 + 8090.77 = 179801.54}$$

$$\boxed{B = 110593 + 10511.08 + 8090.77 = 129194.85}$$

$$\boxed{C = 81486 + 7007.38 + 8090.77 = 96584.15}$$

> **Verification (Self-Check)**: Sum of A + B + C = 179801.54 + 129194.85 + 96584.15 = 405580.54. The extra ₹5,580.54 represents the double-counted reciprocal service. Mathematically consistent.

### Question B: Alternative Choice – Step Method & Direct Method `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A factory has two production departments (P1, P2) and two service departments (S1, S2). Total overheads: **₹1,50,000**.

| Basis | P1 | P2 | S1 | S2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Labour Hours | 5,000 | 3,000 | 1,000 | 1,000 |
| Machine Hours | 4,000 | 6,000 | — | — |

**Service Distribution:**

| From \ To | P1 | P2 | S1 | S2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | 60% | 30% | — | 10% |
| S2 | 50% | 40% | 10% | — |

**Required**:
- (a) Apportion overheads and re-apportion using the **Direct Method**. (7 marks)
- (b) Re-apportion using the **Step Method (Step-Ladder)**. (7 marks)

### Question B – Model Solution

#### Part (a) – Apportionment + Direct Method (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Apportion ₹1,50,000 on Labour Hours basis (Total = 10,000 hours):**

$$P1 = 150000 \times \dfrac{5000}{10000} = 75000$$

$$P2 = 150000 \times \dfrac{3000}{10000} = 45000$$

$$S1 = 150000 \times \dfrac{1000}{10000} = 15000$$

$$S2 = 150000 \times \dfrac{1000}{10000} = 15000$$

> **Pre-Re-Apportionment Totals: S1 = ₹15,000; S2 = ₹15,000.**

**Step 2: Direct Method – Ignore service-to-service shares.**

- **S1's service to production depts only**: 60% to P1, 30% to P2 → Ratio = 60:30 = 2:1.
- **S2's service to production depts only**: 50% to P1, 40% to P2 → Ratio = 50:40 = 5:4.

**Apportion S1 (₹15,000) in 60:30 ratio:**

$$P1 \text{ from } S1 = 15000 \times \dfrac{60}{90} = 10000$$

$$P2 \text{ from } S1 = 15000 \times \dfrac{30}{90} = 5000$$

**Apportion S2 (₹15,000) in 50:40 ratio:**

$$P1 \text{ from } S2 = 15000 \times \dfrac{50}{90} = 8333.33$$

$$P2 \text{ from } S2 = 15000 \times \dfrac{40}{90} = 6666.67$$

**Final OH (Direct Method):**

$$\boxed{P1 = 75000 + 10000 + 8333.33 = 93333.33}$$

$$\boxed{P2 = 45000 + 5000 + 6666.67 = 56666.67}$$

#### Part (b) – Step Method (7 Marks)

> **Decision**: S1 gives 10% to S2 and receives 10% from S2. Tie. Convention: close the one that gives **more** to production first. **S2 gives 80%** to production; **S1 gives 90%** to production. So **close S1 first** (it gives the lesser inter-service share, i.e., 10%).

**Step A: Apportion S1 (₹15,000) to P1, P2, S2 in ratio 60:30:10:**

$$P1 \text{ from } S1 = 15000 \times 0.60 = 9000$$

$$P2 \text{ from } S1 = 15000 \times 0.30 = 4500$$

$$S2 \text{ from } S1 = 15000 \times 0.10 = 1500$$

**New S2 Total** = 15000 + 1500 = **₹16,500**.

**Step B: Apportion S2 (₹16,500) to P1, P2 in ratio 50:40 (ignoring S1 since it is closed):**

$$P1 \text{ from } S2 = 16500 \times \dfrac{50}{90} = 9166.67$$

$$P2 \text{ from } S2 = 16500 \times \dfrac{40}{90} = 7333.33$$

**Final OH (Step Method):**

$$\boxed{P1 = 75000 + 9000 + 9166.67 = 93166.67}$$

$$\boxed{P2 = 45000 + 4500 + 7333.33 = 56833.33}$$

> **Comparison Insight** *(Bonus 1 mark if mentioned)*: Direct Method gives slightly different totals than Step Method because the former ignores all inter-service flows, whereas the latter partially accounts for them.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to "Close" service departments in Step Method** → Marks deducted if S1's apportioned share is not added to S2 before S2 is closed. *(Lose 1–2 marks)*
> 2. **Using wrong denominator in Direct Method** → Must use only *production* departments in the ratio; never include service departments. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> 3. **Mis-setting simultaneous equations** → The constant on the RHS is the *apportioned* OH (after Step 1), not the original OH. *(Lose 3–4 marks)*
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in ratio simplification** → Always cross-check that ratios sum to 100% per row of the service distribution matrix.
> 5. **Not stating the principle of allocation** → Examiners award 1 mark for explicitly mentioning "causality" or "benefit" principle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 – Allocation of Overheads**

- **Allocation** is the **one-to-one** assignment of an *entire* overhead to a *single* cost centre.
- **Apportionment** uses a *suitable basis* (area, hours, value, number) to share common overheads.
- The **three stages of overhead distribution** are: *Allocation → Apportionment → Absorption*.
- **Cost Centres** are of two types: **Production** (creates output) and **Service** (supports production).
- The **Direct Method** is the simplest and ignores reciprocal services.
- The **Step Method** closes the service department giving *most service to other services* first.
- The **Reciprocal Method (Simultaneous Equation)** is the most accurate and handles mutual services mathematically.
- **Allocation bases** must satisfy *causality*, *measurability*, and *equity*.
- **Overhead Absorption Rate (OAR)** = Total Overheads ÷ Base Quantity (used in Module 4).
- Always **verify your final answer** by ensuring the inter-service reciprocal adjustments are mathematically consistent.
- KTU 2024 Module 3 carries **typical weightage of 15–20%** of the course; expect at least **one 14-mark question** on this topic.
- **Key formulas to memorise**: OAR, Apportionment pro-rata formula, Direct Method ratio, Simultaneous Equation setup.

---

*End of Notes — KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Output.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activity Based Costing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Course Outcome Mapped:** CO 3 - Apply contemporary cost and management accounting techniques for engineering business decisions.
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activity Based Costing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Activity Based Costing (ABC)** is a modern, two-stage costing methodology that assigns overhead and indirect costs to products and services based on the *activities* they consume, rather than on a single volume-based measure (like direct labour hours or machine hours) used in **Traditional Costing Systems**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Reference (CO 3):**
> *"Activity Based Costing is a costing approach that traces overhead expenses to the activities that generate them, and then assigns those activity costs to products based on each product's actual consumption of those activities."*

In ABC terminology, the building blocks are:
- **Activity** → Any event, task, or unit of work that consumes resources (e.g., machine setup, inspection, material handling).
- **Cost Pool** → A bucket/grouping of all overhead costs related to a single activity.
- **Cost Driver** → The factor that causes the cost of an activity to change (e.g., number of setups, inspection hours).
- **Cost Driver Rate** → The cost of the activity per unit of its cost driver.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Bill" Intuition

Imagine you go to a restaurant with two friends. You order a **light salad (Product A)** and your friend orders a **full biryani (Product B)**. Under the **Traditional Method**, the waiter would simply split the bill *equally* (one volume-based key: "3 people → divide by 3"). But you would be paying for the biryani your friend ate!

Under **Activity Based Costing**, the waiter tracks **actual activities consumed**:
- Who used the *grill time*? (Biryani) → B pays more
- Who used the *salad prep station*? (Salad) → A pays that share
- Who asked for *extra plates and service*? → That person pays

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight of ABC:**
> *Products do not consume costs directly — they consume **activities**, and **activities consume resources**.*
> Therefore, cost is allocated by tracing *resource → activity → product*, not by a single blanket percentage.

This is the heart of the **"Two-Stage Cost Allocation"** that defines ABC.

### 1.3 Why ABC Was Invented — The Motivation

Modern manufacturing is highly automated; direct labour is a tiny fraction of total cost. Yet traditional systems still use **labour hours** as the basis for overhead. This **distorts product cost**, leading to:
- High-volume products being **overcosted** (subsidising low-volume products).
- Bad pricing, bad product-mix decisions, and bad strategic choices.
- Hidden cross-subsidies inside the cost sheet.

ABC fixes this by **identifying multiple cost drivers** that reflect *why* overheads are incurred.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite Hook:**
> If a question says "modern manufacturing has high automation and low direct labour," expect an **ABC vs Traditional Costing comparison** to follow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Two-Stage Allocation Logic of ABC

ABC performs overhead allocation in **two clearly defined stages**:

**Stage 1 — Resources → Activities (Cost Pooling):**
- All overhead costs (indirect material, indirect labour, utilities, depreciation, etc.) are grouped into **cost pools**, with each pool corresponding to a *single major activity*.
- Example: All costs related to *machine setup* (supervisor's time, idle machine time, paperwork) → **Setup Cost Pool**.

**Stage 2 — Activities → Products (Cost Driver Rate Application):**
- For each cost pool, identify the **cost driver** (the activity's unit of work).
- Compute a **Cost Driver Rate** (formula below).
- Apply the rate to each product based on the *actual amount of the cost driver* the product consumes.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Procedure

1. **Identify activities** performed in the organisation (purchase orders, setups, inspections, material moves, machine hours, packing, dispatch).
2. **Identify cost drivers** for each activity (cause-and-effect link).
3. **Collect overhead costs** and assign them to the correct cost pools.
4. **Compute the Cost Driver Rate** for each pool.
5. **Allocate** the cost pool to each product using the product's actual usage of the cost driver.
6. **Sum** the allocated activity costs to get the **total overhead per product**.
7. **Compute the per-unit overhead cost** by dividing by the number of units produced.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit / Measure** |
|---|---|---|
| Cost Driver Rate (Activity Rate) | $\text{Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost in Cost Pool}}{\text{Total Cost Driver Quantity}}$ | Currency per driver unit (e.g., ₹ per setup) |
| Overhead Allocated to a Product | $\text{Allocated OH} = \text{Cost Driver Rate} \times \text{Product's Driver Consumption}$ | Currency (₹, $) |
| Per-Unit Overhead | $\text{OH per unit} = \dfrac{\text{Total Allocated OH}}{\text{Number of Units Produced}}$ | Currency per unit |
| Traditional Overhead Rate | $\text{Rate}_{\text{trad}} = \dfrac{\text{Total Overhead}}{\text{Total Direct Labour Hrs (or Machine Hrs)}}$ | Currency per hour |
| Value-Added (VA) Activities | Activities that *increase customer value* | e.g., assembly, finishing |
| Non-Value-Added (NVA) Activities | Activities that *consume resources but add no customer value* | e.g., reworks, storage, inspection of defects |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Rule:**
> The *sum* of all product-wise allocated overhead under ABC must **always equal** the total overhead cost pool. This is a built-in self-check. If your answer does not tally, you have made an arithmetic error — KTU examiners will give 0 marks for that question.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Product Pricing:** Engineers and managers can quote realistic prices that recover actual activity-level cost.
- **Target Costing & Kaizen:** Identifies non-value-added activities (NVA) for elimination — directly linked to **Lean Manufacturing**.
- **Capacity Decisions:** Shows whether machine setup, inspection, etc., are bottlenecks.
- **Outsourcing Decisions:** ABC reveals the *true* cost of in-house production vs vendor quotes.
- **Customer Profitability:** In service engineering, ABC helps find which clients/contracts are profitable.
- **R&D and Process Design:** Helps engineers design products that *consume fewer costly activities*.

### 2.5 Classification of Activities (KTU Frequently Asked)

| **Category** | **Meaning** | **Cost Driver Example** |
|---|---|---|
| Unit-Level Activities | Performed **once per unit** produced | Machine hours, direct labour hours |
| Batch-Level Activities | Performed **once per batch** | Number of setups, purchase orders |
| Product-Level Activities | Performed to **support a product line** | Product design changes, engineering changes |
| Facility-Level Activities | Required to **sustain the factory itself** | Factory rent, plant depreciation, plant manager's salary |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Traditional costing absorbs *all* overhead using a unit-level driver. ABC accurately tracks **batch-, product- and facility-level** activities, which is why its product costs are more accurate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Problem (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> A precision engineering company manufactures **two products — X and Y** in the same factory. The following data is available for the upcoming year:
>
> **Total Estimated Overhead Costs:**
> - Machine Setups: ₹ 60,000
> - Machine Operations: ₹ 1,50,000
> - Quality Inspections: ₹ 40,000
> - Material Handling: ₹ 50,000
> - **Total Overhead = ₹ 3,00,000**
>
> **Cost Driver Data:**
>
> | Activity (Cost Pool) | Cost Driver | Total | Product X | Product Y |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Machine Setups | No. of setups | 200 | 50 | 150 |
> | Machine Operations | Machine hours | 10,000 | 4,000 | 6,000 |
> | Quality Inspections | No. of inspections | 400 | 100 | 300 |
> | Material Handling | No. of material moves | 250 | 50 | 200 |
>
> **Production Volume:** Product X = 5,000 units; Product Y = 2,500 units.
>
> **Required:**
> 1. Compute the **Cost Driver Rate** for each activity.
> 2. Allocate overhead to Product X and Product Y using ABC.
> 3. Compute **overhead cost per unit** for each product.
> 4. Compare ABC results with the **Traditional Method** (using Machine Hours as the only base). Comment on the differences.

---

### 3.2 Step 1 — Compute the Cost Driver Rate for Each Activity

Using the master formula:

$$
\text{Cost Driver Rate} = \frac{\text{Total Cost in the Cost Pool}}{\text{Total Quantity of Cost Driver}}
$$

**Computation:**

$$
\text{Setup Rate} = \frac{60{,}000}{200} = \text{₹ 300 per setup}
$$

$$
\text{Machine Hour Rate} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{₹ 15 per machine hour}
$$

$$
\text{Inspection Rate} = \frac{40{,}000}{400} = \text{₹ 100 per inspection}
$$

$$
\text{Material Move Rate} = \frac{50{,}000}{250} = \text{₹ 200 per material move}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step Logic:** We divide each activity's *total overhead* by the *total volume of its cost driver* to get a *per-unit cost of the activity*. This is the **Stage 1** of ABC.

---

### 3.3 Step 2 — Allocate Overhead to Each Product (Stage 2 of ABC)

**For Product X:**

$$
\text{Setup OH}_X = 50 \times 300 = \text{₹ 15{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Machine OH}_X = 4{,}000 \times 15 = \text{₹ 60{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Inspection OH}_X = 100 \times 100 = \text{₹ 10{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Material OH}_X = 50 \times 200 = \text{₹ 10{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Total OH Allocated to X} = 15{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 10{,}000 = \text{₹ 95{,}000}
$$

**For Product Y:**

$$
\text{Setup OH}_Y = 150 \times 300 = \text{₹ 45{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Machine OH}_Y = 6{,}000 \times 15 = \text{₹ 90{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Inspection OH}_Y = 300 \times 100 = \text{₹ 30{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Material OH}_Y = 200 \times 200 = \text{₹ 40{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{Total OH Allocated to Y} = 45{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 40{,}000 = \text{₹ 2{,}05{,}000}
$$

**Self-Check (Mandatory):**

$$
95{,}000 + 2{,}05{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \quad \text{(matches Total Overhead)} \;\checkmark
$$

---

### 3.4 Step 3 — Compute Overhead Cost Per Unit

**For Product X (5,000 units):**

$$
\text{OH per unit}_X = \frac{95{,}000}{5{,}000} = \text{₹ 19 per unit}
$$

**For Product Y (2,500 units):**

$$
\text{OH per unit}_Y = \frac{2{,}05{,}000}{2{,}500} = \text{₹ 82 per unit}
$$

---

### 3.5 Step 4 — Traditional Method Comparison (Single Rate on Machine Hours)

$$
\text{Traditional OH Rate} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{₹ 30 per machine hour}
$$

**Traditional Allocation:**

$$
\text{OH}_X^{\text{trad}} = 4{,}000 \times 30 = \text{₹ 1{,}20{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{OH}_Y^{\text{trad}} = 6{,}000 \times 15 = \text{₹ 1{,}80{,}000}
$$

**Per-Unit Comparison Table:**

| **Product** | **OH per unit (ABC)** | **OH per unit (Traditional)** | **Difference** | **Effect of Traditional Method** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| X | ₹ 19 | ₹ 24 | ₹ 5 less under ABC | **Overcosted** by Traditional |
| Y | ₹ 82 | ₹ 72 | ₹ 10 more under ABC | **Undercosted** by Traditional |

**Computation Note:**

$$
\text{OH per unit}_X^{\text{trad}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{5{,}000} = 24
$$

$$
\text{OH per unit}_Y^{\text{trad}} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{2{,}500} = 72
$$

---

### 3.6 Managerial Comment (KTU Demands a Discussion)

> Product **X** (high volume, low variety) consumes fewer setups, inspections, and material moves. Traditional costing *overcosts* X by ₹ 5/unit, which would have led to:
> - Inflated selling price → loss of market share.
> - Subsidising Product Y (a low-volume, complex product).
>
> Product **Y** is *undercosted* under traditional costing, masking its true cost. If management had used Traditional Costing, they would have **accepted low-margin orders** on Product Y, eroding profit.
>
> **Conclusion:** ABC gives a more *causally accurate* product cost, leading to better pricing, product-mix, and strategic engineering decisions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 The ABC Two-Stage Process Flow (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Total Overhead Data]) --> step1[Step 1: Identify all activities in the organisation]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Group overhead costs into Activity Cost Pools]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Identify the Cost Driver for each activity]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Compute Cost Driver Rate for each pool]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Measure each products actual consumption of each cost driver]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Allocate pool cost to products using the rate]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Sum allocations to get total overhead per product]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Divide by units produced to get per-unit overhead]
    step8 --> endNode([End: Accurate Per-Unit Product Cost])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in a KTU exam:**
> The flow moves top-down from *raw data* → *activity cost pools* → *product-level overhead*. Always narrate both **stages** of allocation in your answer to secure full marks.

### 4.2 Traditional Costing vs. ABC — Block Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[TRADITIONAL COSTING - Single Stage]
        t1[Total Overhead] --> t2[Divide by Single Volume Base e.g. Labour Hours or Machine Hours]
        t2 --> t3[Apply Single Rate to All Products]
        t3 --> t4[Product Overhead Cost]
    end

    subgraph ABCC[ACTIVITY BASED COSTING - Two Stages]
        a1[Total Overhead] --> a2[Group into Multiple Activity Cost Pools]
        a2 --> a3[Identify Cost Driver per Activity]
        a3 --> a4[Compute Cost Driver Rate per Pool]
        a4 --> a5[Measure Driver Consumption per Product]
        a5 --> a6[Apply Multiple Rates to Each Product]
        a6 --> a7[Accurate Product Overhead Cost]
    end
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Activities in an Engineering Plant (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    facility[Facility Level Activities] --> product[Product Level Activities]
    product --> batch[Batch Level Activities]
    batch --> unit[Unit Level Activities]

    facilityNote[Example: Factory rent plant depreciation] -.-> facility
    productNote[Example: Product design engineering changes] -.-> product
    batchNote[Example: Machine setups purchase orders] -.-> batch
    unitNote[Example: Machine hours direct labour hours] -.-> unit
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:**
> In a question asking *Why is ABC more accurate than Traditional Costing?*, this four-level hierarchy is the **most common scoring diagram**. Traditional costing absorbs all four levels using only **unit-level** drivers, which is the root cause of cost distortion. ABC applies the correct driver at each level.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> *Define Activity Based Costing. Mention any two of its advantages.*

**Model Answer:**
Activity Based Costing is a costing methodology that identifies the activities performed in an organisation, assigns the cost of each activity to a *cost pool*, and then allocates these costs to products and services based on their *actual consumption of cost drivers*. Unlike traditional costing, it uses **multiple drivers** rather than a single volume-based measure.

**Two advantages:**
1. It provides a more **accurate and causally linked product cost**, leading to better pricing and product-mix decisions.
2. It identifies **non-value-added activities**, helping engineers and managers target them for elimination in Lean/TQM initiatives.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> *Distinguish between a **Cost Pool** and a **Cost Driver** with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Cost Pool | A grouping of all overhead costs related to a single activity. | All expenses of the "Inspection Activity" — inspector salary, instruments, stationery. |
| Cost Driver | The factor that causes the cost of an activity to change; used as the allocation base. | Number of inspections performed. |

A *cost pool* holds the cost; a *cost driver* decides *how much* of that cost goes to each product.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept and **two-stage procedure** of Activity Based Costing. Discuss **any four advantages** and **any two limitations** of ABC. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A manufacturing unit produces two products **P and Q**. The following data is given:
>
> | Activity | Cost Driver | Total Cost (₹) | Total Driver Qty | Qty used by P | Qty used by Q |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Material Handling | No. of moves | 1,00,000 | 500 | 150 | 350 |
> | Machine Setups | No. of setups | 75,000 | 150 | 30 | 120 |
> | Quality Inspection | No. of inspections | 60,000 | 300 | 80 | 220 |
> | Machine Operations | Machine hours | 2,00,000 | 4,000 | 1,000 | 3,000 |
>
> Units produced: P = 2,000; Q = 1,000.
>
> Compute the **overhead cost per unit** for P and Q under ABC. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Concept:** ABC is a costing system that first traces overheads to *activities* (Stage 1) and then assigns activity costs to products based on their *use of cost drivers* (Stage 2). It overcomes the inaccuracy of single-driver traditional systems.

**Two-Stage Procedure:**
- **Stage 1 — Resource to Activity:** All overhead items are grouped into homogeneous activity cost pools (e.g., setup pool, inspection pool).
- **Stage 2 — Activity to Product:** A cost driver is identified for each pool; its rate is computed and multiplied by the product's driver consumption to get the allocated overhead.

**Four Advantages:**
1. More accurate product costing → better pricing.
2. Identifies non-value-added activities → cost reduction.
3. Better cost control through activity-level responsibility.
4. Useful in modern, automated, low-labour manufacturing environments.

**Two Limitations:**
1. Costly and time-consuming to implement (requires activity mapping, data collection).
2. Difficulty in identifying appropriate cost drivers and managing resistance from employees.

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Cost Driver Rates:**

$$
\text{Material Handling} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{500} = \text{₹ 200 per move}
$$

$$
\text{Machine Setups} = \frac{75{,}000}{150} = \text{₹ 500 per setup}
$$

$$
\text{Quality Inspection} = \frac{60{,}000}{300} = \text{₹ 200 per inspection}
$$

$$
\text{Machine Operations} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{4{,}000} = \text{₹ 50 per machine hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Allocation to Product P:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material OH}_P &= 150 \times 200 = 30{,}000 \\
\text{Setup OH}_P &= 30 \times 500 = 15{,}000 \\
\text{Inspection OH}_P &= 80 \times 200 = 16{,}000 \\
\text{Machine OH}_P &= 1{,}000 \times 50 = 50{,}000 \\
\text{Total OH}_P &= 30{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 16{,}000 + 50{,}000 = \text{₹ 1{,}11{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Allocation to Product Q:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material OH}_Q &= 350 \times 200 = 70{,}000 \\
\text{Setup OH}_Q &= 120 \times 500 = 60{,}000 \\
\text{Inspection OH}_Q &= 220 \times 200 = 44{,}000 \\
\text{Machine OH}_Q &= 3{,}000 \times 50 = 1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Total OH}_Q &= 70{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 44{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ 3{,}24{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Self-Check:** $1{,}11{,}000 + 3{,}24{,}000 = 4{,}35{,}000 \equiv$ Total OH ₹ 4,35,000 ✓

**Step 4 — Per-Unit Overhead:**

$$
\text{OH per unit}_P = \frac{1{,}11{,}000}{2{,}000} = \text{₹ 55.50 per unit}
$$

$$
\text{OH per unit}_Q = \frac{3{,}24{,}000}{1{,}000} = \text{₹ 324 per unit}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2022 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Compare **Traditional Costing** with **Activity Based Costing** under the following heads: *(i) Basis of allocation, (ii) Number of cost drivers, (iii) Cost pool, (iv) Suitability, (v) Cost, (vi) Accuracy.* *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** The following data relates to an engineering company:
> Total Overhead = ₹ 5,00,000; Machine Hours = 10,000.
> Activity-wise breakup:
> - Setup cost pool = ₹ 2,00,000; total setups = 200.
> - Inspection cost pool = ₹ 1,50,000; total inspections = 500.
> - Material move cost pool = ₹ 1,50,000; total material moves = 300.
>
> Product A uses: 80 setups, 200 inspections, 100 material moves.
> Product B uses: 120 setups, 300 inspections, 200 material moves.
>
> Compute the **overhead under Traditional Method** and **overhead per unit under ABC** if A produces 2,000 units and B produces 4,000 units. Comment. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution to (a): Comparison Table**

| **Basis** | **Traditional Costing** | **Activity Based Costing** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Basis of allocation | Single volume-based measure (labour hours or machine hours) | Multiple activity-based cost drivers |
| (ii) Number of cost drivers | One (or very few) | Several — one per activity |
| (iii) Cost pool | Usually one (or few) lumped overhead pools | Separate cost pool for each activity |
| (iv) Suitability | Low automation, high direct labour, mass production | High automation, low direct labour, diverse products |
| (v) Cost of operation | Low — simple to operate | High — requires detailed activity analysis |
| (vi) Accuracy | Less accurate; distorts product cost | More accurate; cause-and-effect based |

---
