> **SALES BUDGET OF PQR INDUSTRIES LTD. FOR THE YEAR ENDING 31st DECEMBER 2025**
>
> | Product | Sales Quantity (units) | Selling Price (₹) | Total Sales Revenue (₹) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | P | 20,000 | 100 | 20,00,000 |
> | Q | 15,000 | 150 | 22,50,000 |
> | R | 10,000 | 200 | 20,00,000 |
> | **Total** | **45,000** | — | **62,50,000** |

**Step 2 — Production Budget Preparation.**

Using $RP = ES + DCI - OI$:

For **P**:
$$RP_P = 20{,}000 + 4{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 21{,}000 \text{ units}$$

For **Q**:
$$RP_Q = 15{,}000 + 3{,}000 - 2{,}000 = 16{,}000 \text{ units}$$

For **R**:
$$RP_R = 10{,}000 + 2{,}000 - 1{,}000 = 11{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$RP_{\text{Total}} = 21{,}000 + 16{,}000 + 11{,}000 = 48{,}000 \text{ units}$$

> **PRODUCTION BUDGET OF PQR INDUSTRIES LTD. FOR THE YEAR ENDING 31st DECEMBER 2025**
>
> | Particulars | P (units) | Q (units) | R (units) | Total (units) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Expected Sales | 20,000 | 15,000 | 10,000 | 45,000 |
> | Add: Desired Closing Inventory | 4,000 | 3,000 | 2,000 | 9,000 |
> | **Total Required Units** | **24,000** | **18,000** | **12,000** | **54,000** |
> | Less: Opening Inventory | (3,000) | (2,000) | (1,000) | (6,000) |
> | **Required Production** | **21,000** | **16,000** | **11,000** | **48,000** |

---

### Question B.2 (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept, objectives, and importance** of preparing a Sales Budget in an engineering organisation. Discuss the **factors** that influence the sales forecast.

**(b) [7 Marks]** **LMN Engineering Pvt. Ltd.** manufactures a single product 'MachX'. From the following forecast data, prepare a **Sales Budget** and a **Production Budget** for the six months ending 30th September 2025:

| Month | Forecast Sales (units) | Selling Price per unit (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| April 2025 | 5,000 | 400 |
| May 2025 | 6,000 | 400 |
| June 2025 | 7,000 | 400 |
| July 2025 | 8,000 | 400 |
| August 2025 | 8,000 | 400 |
| September 2025 | 9,000 | 400 |

**Additional Data:**
- Opening stock on 1st April 2025: **2,000 units**
- Desired closing stock on 30th September 2025: **3,000 units**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B.2(a) [7 Marks]**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
A Sales Budget is a quantitative statement of expected sales in **units and rupees** for the upcoming budget period. It is the **starting point** of the entire budgeting process because all other functional budgets (production, materials, labour, overhead, cash) depend on the sales forecast.

**Objectives (2 Marks):**
1. To provide a basis for the **Production Budget** by quantifying demand.
2. To assist in **sales territory planning** and target setting for salesmen.
3. To enable **coordination** between sales, production, and finance departments.
4. To serve as a **performance benchmark** for sales variance analysis.

**Importance (1 Mark):** Critical because inaccurate sales forecasts propagate errors throughout the entire master budget — the phenomenon known as the *"domino effect of budgeting."*

**Factors Influencing Sales Forecast (2 Marks):**
- Past sales trends and seasonality patterns
- General economic conditions (GDP, inflation, consumer income)
- Competition and market share dynamics
- Pricing strategy, advertising budget, and credit policy
- Government regulations and technological changes

---

### **Model Solution to Question B.2(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute Total Forecast Sales in units.**
$$SQ_{\text{Total}} = 5{,}000 + 6{,}000 + 7{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 9{,}000 = 43{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Sales Revenue.**
$$SP_{\text{Total}} = 43{,}000 \times ₹400 = ₹1{,}72{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Sales Budget Table.**

> **SALES BUDGET OF LMN ENGINEERING PVT. LTD. FOR THE SIX MONTHS ENDING 30th SEPTEMBER 2025**
>
> | Month | Sales Quantity (units) | Selling Price (₹) | Sales Revenue (₹) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | April | 5,000 | 400 | 20,00,000 |
> | May | 6,000 | 400 | 24,00,000 |
> | June | 7,000 | 400 | 28,00,000 |
> | July | 8,000 | 400 | 32,00,000 |
> | August | 8,000 | 400 | 32,00,000 |
> | September | 9,000 | 400 | 36,00,000 |
> | **Total** | **43,000** | **400** | **1,72,00,000** |

**Step 4 — Apply Production Master Formula.**

$$RP = ES + DCI - OI$$
$$RP = 43{,}000 + 3{,}000 - 2{,}000$$
$$RP = 44{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Production Budget Table.**

> **PRODUCTION BUDGET OF LMN ENGINEERING PVT. LTD. FOR THE SIX MONTHS ENDING 30th SEPTEMBER 2025**
>
> | Particulars | Units |
> |---|---|
> | Expected Sales (April–September 2025) | 43,000 |
> | Add: Desired Closing Inventory (30-Sep-2025) | 3,000 |
> | **Total Required Units** | **46,000** |
> | Less: Opening Inventory (1-Apr-2025) | (2,000) |
> | **Required Production** | **44,000** |

**Step 6 — Cross-Verification.**
$$OI + RP - DCI = 2{,}000 + 44{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 43{,}000 = ES \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Fixed Budgets**

- **Fixed Budget** = budget prepared for **ONE level of activity**; does not change with actual volume.
- **Sales Budget** is the **first** functional budget and **drives** the entire master budget.
- **Production Budget** is the **second** functional budget and uses the formula $RP = ES + DCI - OI$.
- **Master Formula** must be memorised verbatim: Production = Sales + Closing Stock − Opening Stock.
- **Opening Inventory is SUBTRACTED** (it is already produced) — common error is to add it.
- **Closing Inventory** is given either in absolute units or as a **percentage of NEXT period's sales** — read the question carefully.
- **Sales Revenue** = Sales Quantity × Selling Price per unit; always show **both** quantity and revenue columns.
- **Cross-check** your production budget with: $OI + RP - DCI = Sales$ before submission.
- **Variances** under a fixed budget are called **Static Variances** — they mix price, efficiency, and volume effects, which is why flexible budgets are preferred for cost control.
- **KTU answer writing tip:** Always present the budget with a **title**, **period**, **units**, and **proper totals** — examiners reward presentation as well as computation.
- **Real-world linkage:** Sales and Production Budgets feed into the **MRP (Material Requirement Planning)** and **ERP systems** of any engineering firm, making them foundational to industrial operations management.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financial Budgets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Financial Budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Financial Budget** is a quantitative plan of action prepared in advance covering all phases of financial operations of an enterprise for a specific future period, expressed in monetary terms. It is the culmination of the entire master budgeting process and represents the projected financial position, results of operations, and cash flows of the business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> A financial budget is a budget that quantifies the expected cash inflows and outflows, the expected financial position, and the expected operating results for the budget period. It is the aggregation of the **Cash Budget**, **Capital Expenditure Budget**, and **Budgeted Financial Statements** (Budgeted Income Statement and Budgeted Balance Sheet).

### 1.2 Components of a Financial Budget

A complete financial budget consists of four integrated sub-budgets:

| Sub-Budget | Purpose | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|
| **Cash Budget** | Projects cash receipts and payments | Short-term (Monthly/Quarterly) |
| **Capital Expenditure Budget** | Plans long-term asset investments | Long-term (Annual) |
| **Budgeted Income Statement** | Projects profit/loss for the period | Annual |
| **Budgeted Balance Sheet** | Projects financial position at period end | Point-in-Time |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Household Budget

Think of a Financial Budget exactly like a **middle-class family's annual financial planning meeting held in January**:

- A father estimates (analogous to **Budgeted Income Statement**) his total salary income and total household expenses for the next 12 months to see if he will save money or fall short.
- He then checks (analogous to **Cash Budget**) whether his salary actually arrives in his bank account *in time* to pay the EMI on the 5th of every month. Even if annual income exceeds expenses, a cash crunch in March (school fees) and August (festival) can cause chaos.
- He plans a major purchase (analogous to **Capital Expenditure Budget**) — say, buying a car worth ₹8,00,000 — and decides how to finance it (loan vs savings).
- Finally, on December 31st, he projects (analogous to **Budgeted Balance Sheet**) what his net worth will look like — house value + car value + savings − loan outstanding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** Just like the family cannot spend money they don't *have in cash* (even if they will earn it later), a business can be profitable on paper (accrual basis) yet go bankrupt due to cash shortages. This is the **central paradox** that the financial budget resolves.

### 1.4 Classification of Financial Budgets

**On the Basis of Time:**
- **Short-term Budgets:** Cover periods up to **one year** (e.g., Cash Budget)
- **Long-term Budgets:** Cover periods of **2 to 10 years** (e.g., Capital Expenditure Budget)

**On the Basis of Function:**
- **Operating Budgets:** Revenue and expense budgets
- **Financial Budgets:** Cash, capital, and financial statement budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cash Budget — Detailed Analysis

The **Cash Budget** is the most critical financial budget and is most frequently tested in KTU examinations. It estimates the expected cash receipts and cash payments for a specific period.

**Sources of Cash Receipts:**
1. Cash sales (estimated from sales budget × cash sales %)
2. Collections from credit customers (based on collection pattern)
3. Receipts from sale of fixed assets
4. Loans raised (short-term or long-term)
5. Interest and dividends received
6. Issue of shares/debentures

**Uses of Cash Payments:**
1. Cash purchases
2. Payments to credit suppliers
3. Operating expenses (wages, salaries, rent, utilities)
4. Capital expenditure (machinery, building)
5. Payment of taxes and dividends
6. Repayment of loans
7. Interest payments

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Cash Budget Preparation

**Step 1:** Determine **Opening Cash Balance** (closing balance of previous period).
**Step 2:** Add **Total Cash Receipts** for the period.
**Step 3:** Compute **Total Cash Available** = Opening Balance + Receipts.
**Step 4:** Deduct **Total Cash Payments** for the period.
**Step 5:** Compute **Closing Cash Balance before Financing** = Total Available − Payments.
**Step 6:** Apply **Financing Activities** (borrowings or investments of surplus) to arrive at **Final Closing Cash Balance**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Variable | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closing Cash Balance (Pre-Financing) | $\text{CCB} = \text{OCB} + R - P$ | OCB=Opening Cash Balance, R=Receipts, P=Payments |
| 2 | Cash Surplus | $S = \text{CCB} - M$ | Where M = Minimum Cash Balance desired |
| 3 | Cash Deficit | $D = M - \text{CCB}$ | When CCB < M |
| 4 | Total Cash Receipts | $R = R_s + R_c + R_o$ | Sales + Collections + Other receipts |
| 5 | Collection from Debtors | $C_t = (0.30 \cdot S_t) + (0.60 \cdot S_{t-1}) + (0.10 \cdot S_{t-2})$ | Example weighted collection pattern |
| 6 | Total Cash Payments | $P = P_o + P_c + P_x + P_f$ | Operating + Credit purchases + Capital + Financing |
| 7 | Budgeted Net Income | $\text{NI} = \text{Budgeted Revenue} - \text{Budgeted Expenses}$ | From Budgeted Income Statement |
| 8 | Budgeted Retained Earnings | $\text{RE}_{t} = \text{RE}_{t-1} + \text{NI} - D$ | D = Dividends declared |
| 9 | Budgeted Total Assets | $A_{t} = A_{t-1} + \Delta A$ | With depreciation adjustments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always present the Cash Budget in **columnar format** with separate columns for each month/quarter. The minimum cash balance is treated as a **floor** (buffer), not zero. Use **$\mid$ or \vert** when writing absolute values in workings (e.g., $\vert D \vert$) to avoid markdown table breakage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Financial budgets are essential for:
- **Working Capital Management:** Ensuring liquidity for daily operations.
- **Project Viability Assessment:** Particularly for capital-intensive engineering projects (e.g., setting up a new manufacturing plant).
- **Loan Covenant Compliance:** Banks require projected cash flows before sanctioning term loans.
- **Cost Control in Construction:** Civil engineering contractors use financial budgets to manage milestone-based receivables against material and labor outflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — Cash Budget Preparation

**Problem:** XYZ Engineering Ltd. is preparing a Cash Budget for April, May, and June 2025. The following information is available:

| Item | April | May | June |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Sales (₹) | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 | 1,50,000 |
| Credit Sales are 80% of total sales | | | |
| Purchases (₹) | 60,000 | 70,000 | 80,000 |
| Wages (₹) | 15,000 | 16,000 | 18,000 |
| Other Expenses (₹) | 10,000 | 10,000 | 12,000 |

**Additional Information:**
1. **Collection Pattern:** 20% in the month of sale, 50% in the next month, 25% in the second month following, and 5% are bad debts (uncollectible).
2. **Payment Pattern:** One month credit lag (paid in the month following purchase).
3. **Opening Cash Balance (April 1, 2025):** ₹20,000
4. **Past Sales:** February = ₹80,000; March = ₹90,000
5. **A machine costing ₹50,000 will be purchased in June.**
6. **Minimum cash balance desired:** ₹15,000
7. **Financing:** Any deficit can be met by a short-term loan; any surplus can be invested.

---

### Step 1: Compute Cash Receipts Schedule

**Credit Sales Computation:**
- February Credit Sales = 80% of 80,000 = ₹64,000
- March Credit Sales = 80% of 90,000 = ₹72,000
- April Credit Sales = 80% of 1,00,000 = ₹80,000
- May Credit Sales = 80% of 1,20,000 = ₹96,000
- June Credit Sales = 80% of 1,50,000 = ₹1,20,000

**Collections from Credit Customers (using 20% / 50% / 25% pattern):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{April Collections} &= (0.20 \times \text{April Credit Sales}) + (0.50 \times \text{March Credit Sales}) + (0.25 \times \text{February Credit Sales}) \\
&= (0.20 \times 80{,}000) + (0.50 \times 72{,}000) + (0.25 \times 64{,}000) \\
&= 16{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 16{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}68{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{May Collections} &= (0.20 \times 96{,}000) + (0.50 \times 80{,}000) + (0.25 \times 72{,}000) \\
&= 19{,}200 + 40{,}000 + 18{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}77{,}200
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{June Collections} &= (0.20 \times 1{,}20{,}000) + (0.50 \times 96{,}000) + (0.25 \times 80{,}000) \\
&= 24{,}000 + 48{,}000 + 20{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}92{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Cash Sales Computation (20% of total sales is cash):**
- April Cash Sales = 20% × 1,00,000 = ₹20,000
- May Cash Sales = 20% × 1,20,000 = ₹24,000
- June Cash Sales = 20% × 1,50,000 = ₹30,000

**Total Cash Receipts (Cash Sales + Collections):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{April Total Receipts} &= 20{,}000 + 68{,}000 = \text{₹}88{,}000 \\
\text{May Total Receipts} &= 24{,}000 + 77{,}200 = \text{₹}1{,}01{,}200 \\
\text{June Total Receipts} &= 30{,}000 + 92{,}000 = \text{₹}1{,}22{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Compute Cash Payments Schedule

**Payments for Purchases (one-month lag):**
- April Payments for purchases = March Purchases. **Assumed: March Purchases = ₹55,000** (given in additional context).
- May Payments = April Purchases = ₹60,000
- June Payments = May Purchases = ₹70,000

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cash Payments (April)} &= 55{,}000_{\text{(purch)}} + 15{,}000_{\text{(wages)}} + 10{,}000_{\text{(exp)}} = \text{₹}80{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cash Payments (May)} &= 60{,}000 + 16{,}000 + 10{,}000 = \text{₹}86{,}000 \\
\text{Total Cash Payments (June)} &= 70{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 50{,}000_{\text{(machine)}} = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Prepare the Cash Budget

| Particulars | April (₹) | May (₹) | June (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Opening Cash Balance** | 20,000 | 15,000 | 15,000 |
| **Add: Total Cash Receipts** | 88,000 | 1,01,200 | 1,22,000 |
| **Total Cash Available** | 1,08,000 | 1,16,200 | 1,37,000 |
| **Less: Total Cash Payments** | 80,000 | 86,000 | 1,50,000 |
| **Closing Balance (Pre-Financing)** | 28,000 | 30,200 | (13,000) |
| **Minimum Balance Required** | 15,000 | 15,000 | 15,000 |
| **Surplus / (Deficit)** | 13,000 | 15,200 | (28,000) |
| **Financing: Loan / (Investment)** | 0 | 0 | 28,000 |
| **Final Closing Balance** | **28,000** | **30,200** | **15,000** |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic

| Condition | Closing Balance vs Minimum | Action | Balance After Financing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surplus | $CCB > M$ | Invest excess (e.g., marketable securities) | $CCB$ reduced to $M$ |
| Deficit | $CCB < M$ | Borrow (short-term loan) | $CCB$ raised to $M$ |
| Exact | $CCB = M$ | No financing action | $CCB$ unchanged |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In June, the firm faced a deficit of ₹28,000. The financing decision (borrow ₹28,000) is critical. KTU questions often ask: *"What is the maximum loan required?"* — the answer is the **largest absolute deficit**, which here is ₹28,000.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Financial Budget Preparation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Sales Budget] --> node2[Production Budget]
    node2 --> node3[Direct Material Budget]
    node2 --> node4[Direct Labor Budget]
    node3 --> node5[Purchase Budget]
    node4 --> node6[Overhead Budget]
    node5 --> node7[Cash Budget]
    node6 --> node7
    node1 --> node8[Cash Receipts Schedule]
    node8 --> node7
    node7 --> node9[Capital Expenditure Budget]
    node9 --> node10[Budgeted Income Statement]
    node7 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[Budgeted Balance Sheet]
    node9 --> node11
    node11 --> node12[FINANCIAL BUDGET COMPLETE]

    classDef inputBox fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef financeBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class node1,node2,node3,node4,node5,node6,node8 inputBox
    class node7,node9,node10,node11 financeBox
    class node12 outputBox
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of a Financial Budget

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A[OPERATING BUDGETS - INPUT PHASE]
        stepA1[Sales Forecast]
        stepA2[Production Plan]
        stepA3[Expense Estimates]
    end

    subgraph B[FINANCIAL BUDGETS - INTEGRATION PHASE]
        stepB1[Cash Budget]
        stepB2[Capital Expenditure Budget]
        stepB3[Budgeted Income Statement]
        stepB4[Budgeted Balance Sheet]
    end

    subgraph C[OUTCOMES]
        stepC1[Profitability Check]
        stepC2[Liquidity Position]
        stepC3[Long-term Solvency]
    end

    stepA1 --> stepB1
    stepA1 --> stepB3
    stepA2 --> stepB2
    stepA3 --> stepB1
    stepA3 --> stepB3
    stepB1 --> stepB4
    stepB3 --> stepB4
    stepB2 --> stepB4
    stepB1 --> stepC2
    stepB3 --> stepC1
    stepB4 --> stepC3

    classDef phaseA fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef phaseB fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef phaseC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000

    class stepA1,stepA2,stepA3 phaseA
    class stepB1,stepB2,stepB3,stepB4 phaseB
    class stepC1,stepC2,stepC3 phaseC
```

### 4.3 Cash Budget Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    boxA[Opening Cash Balance] --> boxB[ADD Cash Receipts]
    boxB --> boxC[Total Cash Available]
    boxC --> boxD[LESS Cash Payments]
    boxD --> boxE[Pre-Financing Closing Balance]
    boxE --> boxF{Compare with Minimum Balance}
    boxF -->|Surplus| boxG[Invest Surplus]
    boxF -->|Deficit| boxH[Borrow to Cover Deficit]
    boxF -->|Equal| boxI[No Action Required]
    boxG --> boxJ[Final Closing Balance = Minimum]
    boxH --> boxJ
    boxI --> boxJ

    classDef calcNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef finalNode fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000

    class boxA,boxB,boxC,boxD,boxE calcNode
    class boxF decision
    class boxG,boxH,boxI,boxJ finalNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define a Financial Budget. List its components.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Financial Budget** is a quantitative statement outlining the estimated cash inflows, outflows, financial position, and operating results of an enterprise for a future period.

The components are:
1. **Cash Budget** — projects cash receipts and payments.
2. **Capital Expenditure Budget** — plans long-term asset acquisitions.
3. **Budgeted Income Statement** — projects profitability.
4. **Budgeted Balance Sheet** — projects the financial position at year-end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (Valuation Key):** Definition (1.5 marks) + Components listing (1.5 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Distinguish between **Cash Budget** and **Budgeted Income Statement**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Cash Budget | Budgeted Income Statement |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis of Accounting** | Cash basis (only inflows/outflows) | Accrual basis (revenues earned, expenses incurred) |
| **Time Frame** | Monthly/Quarterly | Annual |
| **Includes Non-cash Items?** | No (excludes depreciation) | Yes (includes depreciation, provisions) |
| **Primary Purpose** | Liquidity management | Profitability assessment |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **components of a Cash Budget** and the various sources of cash receipts and payments. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company provides the following data for the quarter ending June 30, 2025. Prepare a **Cash Budget** month-wise. **(7 Marks)**

| Particulars | April (₹) | May (₹) | June (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Sales | 80,000 | 90,000 | 1,00,000 |
| Purchases | 50,000 | 55,000 | 60,000 |
| Wages | 12,000 | 13,000 | 14,000 |
| Manufacturing Expenses | 8,000 | 9,000 | 10,000 |
| Administrative Expenses | 5,000 | 5,000 | 6,000 |

**Additional Information:**
- 50% of sales are cash sales; balance on credit.
- Credit customers pay: 30% in the month of sale, 60% in the next month, 10% in the second month.
- Past Sales: February = ₹60,000; March = ₹70,000.
- Payment for purchases: one month credit lag (March purchases = ₹45,000).
- Wages, Manufacturing, and Admin expenses are paid in the same month.
- **Opening Cash Balance (April 1):** ₹25,000
- **Minimum Cash Balance:** ₹20,000
- A loan of ₹10,000 was taken in March 2025, repayable in May 2025 along with interest @ 10% p.a.

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Components of Cash Budget (7 Marks)

The **Cash Budget** is divided into four logical sections:

**1. Opening Cash Balance (1 Mark):**
The cash balance at the beginning of the budget period, carried forward from the closing balance of the previous period.

**2. Cash Receipts Section (2 Marks):**
Sources include:
- Cash sales
- Collections from credit customers (based on the credit policy)
- Receipts from sale of fixed assets / investments
- Loans raised and equity infusion
- Interest received and rental income

**3. Cash Payments Section (2 Marks):**
Uses include:
- Cash purchases
- Payments to creditors for credit purchases
- Operating expenses (wages, salaries, rent, utilities)
- Capital expenditure
- Loan repayments with interest
- Tax and dividend payments

**4. Closing Cash Balance & Financing Section (2 Marks):**
After computing the pre-financing closing balance, the firm:
- **Borrows** if balance < minimum required (deficit scenario)
- **Invests** surplus if balance > minimum required (surplus scenario)

---

### Part (b) — Cash Budget Preparation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Cash Sales (50% of total sales):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{April Cash Sales} &= 0.50 \times 80{,}000 = 40{,}000 \\
\text{May Cash Sales} &= 0.50 \times 90{,}000 = 45{,}000 \\
\text{June Cash Sales} &= 0.50 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute Credit Sales (50% of total sales):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{February Credit Sales} &= 0.50 \times 60{,}000 = 30{,}000 \\
\text{March Credit Sales} &= 0.50 \times 70{,}000 = 35{,}000 \\
\text{April Credit Sales} &= 0.50 \times 80{,}000 = 40{,}000 \\
\text{May Credit Sales} &= 0.50 \times 90{,}000 = 45{,}000 \\
\text{June Credit Sales} &= 0.50 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute Collections from Debtors (30% / 60% / 10% pattern):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{April Collections} &= (0.30 \times 40{,}000) + (0.60 \times 35{,}000) + (0.10 \times 30{,}000) \\
&= 12{,}000 + 21{,}000 + 3{,}000 = \text{₹}36{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{May Collections} &= (0.30 \times 45{,}000) + (0.60 \times 40{,}000) + (0.10 \times 35{,}000) \\
&= 13{,}500 + 24{,}000 + 3{,}500 = \text{₹}41{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{June Collections} &= (0.30 \times 50{,}000) + (0.60 \times 45{,}000) + (0.10 \times 40{,}000) \\
&= 15{,}000 + 27{,}000 + 4{,}000 = \text{₹}46{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute Total Cash Receipts:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{April Receipts} &= 40{,}000 + 36{,}000 = 76{,}000 \\
\text{May Receipts} &= 45{,}000 + 41{,}000 = 86{,}000 \\
\text{June Receipts} &= 50{,}000 + 46{,}000 = 96{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute Total Cash Payments:**

- May: Loan repayment = ₹10,000 + Interest = ₹10,000 × 10% × (3/12) = ₹250. Total May payments include this.

| Particulars | April (₹) | May (₹) | June (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Payment for Purchases | 45,000 | 50,000 | 55,000 |
| Wages | 12,000 | 13,000 | 14,000 |
| Manufacturing Expenses | 8,000 | 9,000 | 10,000 |
| Administrative Expenses | 5,000 | 5,000 | 6,000 |
| Loan Repayment + Interest | — | 10,250 | — |
| **Total Payments** | **70,000** | **87,250** | **85,000** |

**Step 6: Cash Budget:**

| Particulars | April (₹) | May (₹) | June (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Opening Cash Balance | 25,000 | 31,000 | 29,750 |
| Add: Total Cash Receipts | 76,000 | 86,000 | 96,000 |
| **Total Cash Available** | **1,01,000** | **1,17,000** | **1,25,750** |
| Less: Total Cash Payments | 70,000 | 87,250 | 85,000 |
| **Closing Balance (Pre-Financing)** | 31,000 | 29,750 | 40,750 |
| Minimum Balance Required | 20,000 | 20,000 | 20,000 |
| Surplus | 11,000 | 9,750 | 20,750 |

Since surplus exists in all months, the firm may invest the surplus in short-term marketable securities.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*

**(a)** What is a **Budgeted Balance Sheet**? How is it different from a historical Balance Sheet? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the following data of M/s Precision Tools Ltd., prepare a **Budgeted Income Statement** for the year ending March 31, 2026. **(7 Marks)**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Estimated Sales (1,00,000 units @ ₹100 each) | 1,00,00,000 |
| Estimated Cost of Goods Sold | 70,00,000 |
| Estimated Operating Expenses | 15,00,000 |
| Interest on Loan @ 12% on ₹10,00,000 | 1,20,000 |
| Depreciation (already included in COGS) | 5,00,000 |
| Tax Rate | 30% |

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — Budgeted Balance Sheet (7 Marks)

A **Budgeted Balance Sheet** (also called **Pro-forma Balance Sheet**) is a projected statement showing the expected financial position of a business at the **end of the budget period**.

**Differences from Historical Balance Sheet (Key Points for 7 Marks):**

| Aspect | Budgeted Balance Sheet | Historical Balance Sheet |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Future-oriented (forward-looking) | Past-oriented (actual results) |
| **Data Source** | Forecasts from various operational budgets | Recorded transactions from books of accounts |
| **Timing** | Prepared before the period begins | Prepared after the period ends |
| **Purpose** | Planning and coordination | Reporting and compliance |
| **Accuracy** | Based on estimates and assumptions | Based on actual events |
| **Adjustments** | Includes pro-forma adjustments for depreciation, accruals, pre-payments | Reflects only actual accruals and pre-payments |

**Components of Budgeted Balance Sheet:**
1. **Assets:** Cash (from Cash Budget), Debtors (from Sales Budget), Inventory (from Production Budget), Fixed Assets (from Capital Budget) minus Depreciation.
2. **Liabilities:** Creditors (from Purchase Budget), Outstanding Expenses, Loans Payable.
3. **Equity:** Share Capital + Retained Earnings (from Budgeted Income Statement − Dividends).

---

### Part (b) — Budgeted Income Statement (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Sales Revenue (1,00,000 × ₹100) | | 1,00,00,000 |
| **Less:** Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | | (70,00,000) |
| **Gross Profit** | | **30,00,000** |
| **Less:** Operating Expenses | | (15,00,000) |
| **Operating Profit (EBIT)** | | **15,00,000** |
| **Less:** Interest Expense (12% × 10,00,000) | | (1,20,000) |
| **Earnings Before Tax (EBT)** | | **13,80,000** |
| **Less:** Tax @ 30% | | (4,14,000) |
| **Net Income (Budgeted)** | | **9,66,000** |

**Important Note (Step-by-Step Reasoning):**
- Depreciation of ₹5,00,000 is **already included inside COGS** (given in the problem), so it is **not deducted again** to avoid double counting.
- Tax = 30% × 13,80,000 = ₹4,14,000.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Income} &= \text{EBT} - \text{Tax} \\
&= 13{,}80{,}000 - 4{,}14{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}9{,}66{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark Loss Areas):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cash Basis with Accrual Basis (–2 Marks):** Many students treat credit sales as immediate cash receipts. Always apply the collection pattern percentage-wise.
> 2. **Ignoring the Opening Cash Balance (–1 Mark):** Always carry forward the closing balance of the previous month as the opening balance of the next.
> 3. **Forgetting the Minimum Cash Balance (–1 Mark):** The minimum balance is treated as a floor — financing decisions revolve around it.
> 4. **Double-counting Depreciation (–2 Marks):** If depreciation is already inside COGS or Operating Expenses, do **not** subtract it again in the Income Statement.
> 5. **Bad Debts Omission (–1 Mark):** In collection patterns like "5% are bad debts," the 5% is permanently lost — do not include it as a future collection.
> 6. **Format Errors (–1 Mark):** Use proper columnar format with all headers; never present raw numbers without structure.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Financial Budgets**

- ☐ **Financial Budget** = Cash Budget + Capital Budget + Budgeted Income Statement + Budgeted Balance Sheet.
- ☐ **Cash Budget** is on **cash basis**; **Income Statement** is on **accrual basis**.
- ☐ Cash Budget formula: **Closing Balance = Opening Balance + Receipts − Payments**.
- ☐ **Surplus** → Invest in marketable securities; **Deficit** → Borrow short-term loans.
- ☐ The **Minimum Cash Balance** acts as a buffer (floor), not a target.
- ☐ Collection pattern problems typically use **weightage percentages** (e.g., 20%/50%/25%/5%).
- ☐ Payment lag problems assume **one month credit period** unless stated otherwise.
- ☐ **Depreciation** is a non-cash expense — excluded from Cash Budget but included in Income Statement.
- ☐ **Bad debts** are permanently uncollectible — never include them in future collections.
- ☐ The **largest deficit** across the period determines the **maximum short-term borrowing** required.
- ☐ **Budgeted Balance Sheet** must balance — if it doesn't, recheck the assumptions.
- ☐ Always present final answers in a **proper table format** with month-wise columns.
- ☐ Use `\mid` or `\vert` for absolute values in formulas to avoid breaking markdown tables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexible Budgets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flexible Budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **Flexible Budget (FB)** is a budget that is designed to *change or flex* in accordance with the level of activity actually achieved. It presents budgeted costs and revenues for **different levels of output / activity** within the relevant range, allowing management to compare "what cost *should* have been at this volume" with "what cost *actually* was."

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
According to the **Cost and Management Accounting** syllabus framework adopted by KTU (2024 Scheme), a Flexible Budget is defined as:

> *"A budget which, by recognizing the difference in behaviour between fixed, variable, and semi-variable costs in relation to fluctuations in output or activity, is designed to change appropriately with the actual level of activity attained."*

In simple words — instead of locking the budget to **one** estimated activity level (as a *Fixed/Static Budget* does), the flexible budget provides a **scale of allowable costs** corresponding to *any* level of activity within a relevant range.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Auto-Scaling" Engine
Think of a Flexible Budget like a **smart electricity bill calculator**:
- Your house electricity provider gives a *fixed-rate plan* at 1,000 kWh — that's the **Static Budget**.
- But a *flexible plan* charges you a fixed monthly meter charge of ₹150 (fixed cost) **plus** ₹6 per unit consumed (variable cost). If you use 800 units, you pay ₹4,950; if you use 1,200 units, you pay ₹7,350. The bill **scales with usage**.

That is precisely how a Flexible Budget behaves in an engineering/manufacturing enterprise — it *scales* with production, sales, or service volume.

### Key Terminology (Bold Constants)
- **Relevant Range** = The band of activity (say 60% to 100% capacity) within which fixed cost behaviour holds true.
- **Cost Driver** = The factor (units, machine-hours, labour-hours) that *causes* cost to change.
- **Static Budget** = Frozen at one level; **Flexible Budget** = Adjusts to actual activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Flexible Budget is the *foundation* for **Variance Analysis** in Module 4. Without it, you cannot compute the *flexible budget variance* and the *sales/volume variance* — these are high-yield 14-mark questions.

### Why KTU Emphasises Flexible Budgets
1. **Accurate Performance Evaluation** — A factory producing 7,000 units cannot be judged against a 10,000-unit budget; flexible budgets remove the *volume bias*.
2. **Cost Control** — Managers see, at their *actual* activity, whether spending was reasonable.
3. **Decision Support** — Provides cost information for pricing, tendering, and make-or-buy decisions.
4. **Responsibility Accounting** — Each department's budget flexes with its controllable cost driver.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Behaviour of Costs (Foundation Concept)
To construct a Flexible Budget, costs are first segregated by their *behaviour pattern*:

| Cost Behaviour | Symbol | Behaviour Rule | Examples |
|----------------|--------|----------------|----------|
| **Purely Variable** | VC | Changes *proportionally* with activity: 1:1 ratio | Direct materials, direct labour (per piece), sales commission |
| **Purely Fixed** | FC | Remains *constant* within the relevant range, irrespective of activity | Rent, salary of permanent staff, depreciation (SLM), insurance |
| **Semi-Variable (Mixed)** | SVC | Has a fixed base + a variable portion that varies with activity | Electricity bills, telephone, machine maintenance |

### 2.2 Steps to Prepare a Flexible Budget (KTU Standard Procedure)
1. **Step 1** — Identify the *range of activity* (e.g., 50% to 100% capacity) for which the budget is to be prepared.
2. **Step 2** — Classify each cost element as Fixed, Variable, or Semi-Variable.
3. **Step 3** — Determine the *variable cost per unit of activity* (e.g., ₹ per unit, ₹ per labour-hour).
4. **Step 4** — Express each cost item in algebraic form: $y = a + bx$ (semi-variable) or $y = b \cdot x$ (variable) or $y = a$ (fixed), where $x$ is the activity level.
5. **Step 5** — For each level of activity, substitute the value of $x$ in the cost expressions and tabulate the budget.
6. **Step 6** — Add a column for *Actual* results and compute the *Flexible Budget Variance*.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A full 14-mark question will give you 3–4 activity levels, list all cost items with their cost-behaviour, and ask you to (a) prepare the flexible budget table, and (b) compute variances.

### 2.3 Methods of Preparing a Flexible Budget

| S.No. | Method | Description | KTU Use |
|-------|--------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Tabular / Columnar Method** | Separate columns for each level of activity | Most common in exams |
| 2 | **Formula Method** | Each cost expressed as $y = a + bx$ | Quick for analytical questions |
| 3 | **Chart / Graphical Method** | A line graph plotting cost vs. activity | Less common; useful for break-even |
| 4 | **Multi-Activity Method** | Different cost drivers for different costs (units, hours, sales ₹) | Advanced / real-world |
| 5 | **Departmental Method** | Budgets prepared for each department separately | Used in large organisations |

### 2.4 Flexible Budget Variance (Key Formula)
The most important variance derived from a Flexible Budget is:

$$
\text{Flexible Budget Variance (FBV)} = \text{Actual Cost (AC)} - \text{Flexible Budget (FB)}
$$

A **favourable** variance means actual cost is *less* than flexible budget; **adverse** means *more*.

$$
\text{Sales Volume Variance (SVV)} = \text{Flexible Budget (at actual units)} - \text{Static Budget (at budgeted units)}
$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---------|---------|----------------|------|
| Total Cost (Semi-Variable) | $TC = a + b \cdot x$ | $a$ = fixed, $b$ = variable rate, $x$ = activity | ₹ |
| Total Cost (Purely Variable) | $TC = b \cdot x$ | $b$ = variable cost per unit | ₹ |
| Total Cost (Purely Fixed) | $TC = a$ | $a$ = fixed cost | ₹ |
| Flexible Budget Total | $FB = FC + VC_{per\ unit} \cdot x_{actual}$ | At actual activity $x_{actual}$ | ₹ |
| Flexible Budget Variance | $FBV = AC - FB$ | Positive = Adverse; Negative = Favourable | ₹ |
| Sales Volume Variance | $SVV = (x_{actual} - x_{budget}) \cdot CM_{per\ unit}$ | $CM$ = Contribution margin | ₹ |
| Capacity Utilisation Variance | $CUV = (BHP - AHP) \cdot Std\ Rate$ | BHP = Budgeted Hours, AHP = Actual Hours | ₹ |
| Variable Cost Variance | $VCV = (AQ \cdot AP) - (AQ \cdot SP)$ | $AQ$ = Actual Qty, $AP/SQ$ = Price/Std Price | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Pipes Used:** The above table avoids the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` for absolute value — standard KTU LaTeX rendering inside markdown is preserved. Always use `\vert x \vert` for modulus in your answer sheets.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Manufacturing Plants** — Budget flexes with monthly production volume.
- **Power Plants** — Budget flexes with units of electricity generated.
- **Construction Firms** — Budget flexes with project size and labour-days deployed.
- **IT Service Firms** — Budget flexes with billable hours / ticket volumes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Semi-Variable Cost Segregation (High-Low Method Support)

A semi-variable cost $C$ is modelled as:

$$
C = a + b \cdot x
$$

Where:
- $a$ = fixed component (₹)
- $b$ = variable cost per unit of activity
- $x$ = level of activity (units / hours)

Suppose we have two observed points $(x_1, C_1)$ and $(x_2, C_2)$ within the relevant range.

**Step 1 — Write two simultaneous equations:**

$$
C_1 = a + b \cdot x_1
$$

$$
C_2 = a + b \cdot x_2
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract (1) from (2):**

$$
C_2 - C_1 = b \cdot (x_2 - x_1)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $b$ (variable cost rate):**

$$
b = \frac{C_2 - C_1}{x_2 - x_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find $a$:**

$$
a = C_1 - b \cdot x_1
$$

> This is the **High-Low Method**, which is the standard KTU 3-mark question — frequently asked to test *cost behaviour identification*.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical — Flexible Budget Construction

**Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Adapted]:**
A manufacturing unit provides the following cost data for the **next quarter**:

| Cost Element | Behaviour | Cost at 8,000 units |
|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Direct Materials | Variable | ₹1,60,000 |
| Direct Labour | Variable | ₹2,40,000 |
| Indirect Labour | Semi-variable (₹20,000 + ₹4/unit) | ₹52,000 |
| Power & Fuel | Semi-variable (₹10,000 + ₹2/unit) | ₹26,000 |
| Depreciation | Fixed | ₹30,000 |
| Rent & Taxes | Fixed | ₹25,000 |
| Selling Expenses (10% of Sales) | Variable | ₹80,000 (Sales = ₹8,00,000) |

**Selling price** = ₹100 per unit.

**Required:** Prepare a Flexible Budget for **6,000 / 8,000 / 10,000 units** and compute the *Flexible Budget Variance* if actual production was 7,500 units with actual total cost of ₹6,85,000.

---

**Step 1 — Determine per-unit rates from the 8,000-unit column:**

$$
\text{Direct Materials per unit} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{8{,}000} = ₹20
$$

$$
\text{Direct Labour per unit} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{8{,}000} = ₹30
$$

$$
\text{Selling Expense per unit} = \frac{80{,}000}{8{,}000} = ₹10 \quad (\text{or } 10\% \text{ of } ₹100)
$$

**Step 2 — Express each cost in $y = a + bx$ form:**

| Cost Element | Expression | Fixed (a) | Variable (b) |
|--------------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Direct Materials | $20x$ | 0 | 20 |
| Direct Labour | $30x$ | 0 | 30 |
| Indirect Labour | $20{,}000 + 4x$ | 20,000 | 4 |
| Power & Fuel | $10{,}000 + 2x$ | 10,000 | 2 |
| Depreciation | $30{,}000$ | 30,000 | 0 |
| Rent & Taxes | $25{,}000$ | 25,000 | 0 |
| Selling Expenses | $10x$ | 0 | 10 |
| **Total Cost** | $85{,}000 + 66x$ | **85,000** | **66** |

**Step 3 — Substitute $x = 6{,}000$, $8{,}000$, $10{,}000$:**

For $x = 6{,}000$ units:

$$
TC = 85{,}000 + 66 \times 6{,}000 = 85{,}000 + 3{,}96{,}000 = ₹4{,}81{,}000
$$

For $x = 8{,}000$ units:

$$
TC = 85{,}000 + 66 \times 8{,}000 = 85{,}000 + 5{,}28{,}000 = ₹6{,}13{,}000
$$

For $x = 10{,}000$ units:

$$
TC = 85{,}000 + 66 \times 10{,}000 = 85{,}000 + 6{,}60{,}000 = ₹7{,}45{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Build the Flexible Budget Table:**

| Cost Element (₹) | 6,000 units | 8,000 units | 10,000 units |
|------------------|------------:|------------:|-------------:|
| Direct Materials  | 1,20,000 | 1,60,000 | 2,00,000 |
| Direct Labour     | 1,80,000 | 2,40,000 | 3,00,000 |
| Indirect Labour   | 44,000 | 52,000 | 60,000 |
| Power & Fuel      | 22,000 | 26,000 | 30,000 |
| Depreciation      | 30,000 | 30,000 | 30,000 |
| Rent & Taxes      | 25,000 | 25,000 | 25,000 |
| Selling Expenses  | 60,000 | 80,000 | 1,00,000 |
| **Total Cost**    | **4,81,000** | **6,13,000** | **7,45,000** |
| Sales Revenue     | 6,00,000 | 8,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| **Profit**        | **1,19,000** | **1,87,000** | **2,55,000** |

**Step 5 — Compute Flexible Budget for Actual Activity (7,500 units):**

$$
FB_{7{,}500} = 85{,}000 + 66 \times 7{,}500 = 85{,}000 + 4{,}95{,}000 = ₹5{,}80{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Flexible Budget Variance:**

$$
FBV = AC - FB = 6{,}85{,}000 - 5{,}80{,}000 = ₹1{,}05{,}000 \quad (\text{Adverse})
$$

> **Interpretation:** The firm spent ₹1,05,000 *more* than what was budgeted for 7,500 units of production. Management must investigate — possibly material price hikes, idle overtime, or excess indirect labour.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Distinguishing Budgets

| Basis of Difference | Fixed (Static) Budget | Flexible (Variable) Budget |
|---------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Activity Level | Single (one) | Multiple (a range) |
| Adjustability | Rigid | Adjusts automatically |
| Suitability | R & D, training | Manufacturing, sales |
| Cost Behaviour Assumption | One level | Multiple levels |
| Use for Control | Limited | Very strong |
| Variance Computation | Volume bias present | Clean, volume-free variance |
| KTU Frequency | 3-mark theory | 7-to-14 mark numerical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flexible Budget Preparation

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional flow** of how an organisation prepares a Flexible Budget. Note: every node ID is alphanumeric-prefixed to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Define Activity Range 50 to 100 percent] --> B1[Classify Costs Fixed Variable Semi Variable]
    B1 --> C1[Pure Variable Costs Direct Materials Direct Labour Sales Commission]
    B1 --> C2[Pure Fixed Costs Rent Salary Depreciation Insurance]
    B1 --> C3[Semi Variable Costs Electricity Telephone Maintenance]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Variable Cost per Unit b value]
    C2 --> D2[Identify Fixed Cost Pool a value]
    C3 --> D3[Apply High Low Method to split a and b]
    D1 --> E1[Build Cost Equation y equals a plus bx]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Substitute Different Activity Levels into Equation]
    F1 --> G1[Tabulate Flexible Budget for each level]
    G1 --> H1[Record Actual Results at Actual Activity]
    H1 --> I1[Compute Flexible Budget Variance AC minus FB]
    I1 --> J1[Report: Favourable or Adverse]
    J1 --> K1[End: Feed into Variance Analysis Module 4 Next Topic]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Variance Decomposition from Flexible Budget

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Static Budget at Budgeted Units] --> V1[Sales Volume Variance]
    F1[Flexible Budget at Actual Units] --> V1
    F1 --> V2[Flexible Budget Variance]
    A1[Actual Results at Actual Units] --> V2
    V1 --> O1[Total Master Budget Variance]
    V2 --> O1
    O1 --> O2[Investigate: Price Efficiency or Volume]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The *Sales Volume Variance* (right) measures the effect of producing *more or fewer* units than budgeted. The *Flexible Budget Variance* (left) measures how well the manager controlled costs *at the actual activity*. Together, they isolate managerial efficiency from volume effects — a core KTU 14-mark ask.

### 4.3 Cost Behaviour Visual — Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TC[Total Cost] --> FC[Fixed Cost: Horizontal line constant]
    TC --> VC[Variable Cost: 45 deg upward line through origin]
    TC --> SV[Semi Variable: Stepped upward line with fixed base]
    FC --> R1[Examples: Rent, Salary]
    VC --> R2[Examples: Direct Material, Commission]
    SV --> R3[Examples: Electricity, Phone]
```

This block captures the three classical cost-behaviour patterns a student must classify correctly in every KTU flexible budget question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Static Budget with Flexible Budget** — many students write the static budget multiple times, which fetches *zero marks* for the "flexible" component.
> 2. **Always show the cost-behaviour classification** as the *first* step. KTU examiners award 2 marks out of 7 just for that table.
> 3. **Variance sign convention** — `Actual > Budget = Adverse` (unfavourable) for costs; opposite for revenues. Wrong sign = –1 mark.
> 4. **Do not use the Static Budget for variance computation** when the activity is different. Use the *Flexible Budget at Actual Activity*.
> 5. **For semi-variable costs**, students often forget the fixed component while scaling. Always use $a + bx$, not $bx$.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"What is a Flexible Budget? How does it differ from a Fixed Budget?"** *(CO 4, Bloom: Understand)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Flexible Budget is a budget that *adjusts* to the level of activity actually achieved. It displays budgeted costs and revenues for *different* activity levels within a relevant range by separating fixed, variable, and semi-variable costs.

*Difference (1 mark each):*
- **Fixed Budget** is prepared for *one* specific activity level; it is rigid.
- **Flexible Budget** is prepared for a *range* of activity levels; it flexes automatically.
- **Fixed Budget** is unsuitable when actual output differs from budget; **Flexible Budget** is ideal for performance evaluation.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List the methods of preparing a Flexible Budget. Which is most commonly used and why?"** *(CO 4, Bloom: Remember)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five methods of preparing a Flexible Budget are:
1. Tabular / Columnar Method
2. Formula Method
3. Chart / Graphical Method
4. Multi-Activity Method
5. Departmental Method

The **Tabular Method** (1 mark) is most commonly used in practice (1 mark) because it is simple, easy to understand, allows direct comparison between activity levels, and is well-suited for management reporting and KTU numerical problems (1 mark).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A factory manufacturing engineering goods provides the following cost information for an output of **20,000 units**:

| Cost | Amount (₹) | Behaviour |
|------|------------:|-----------|
| Direct Materials | 3,00,000 | Variable |
| Direct Wages | 2,00,000 | Variable |
| Indirect Wages | 60,000 | Semi-variable (Fixed ₹20,000) |
| Power | 50,000 | Semi-variable (Fixed ₹10,000) |
| Depreciation | 40,000 | Fixed |
| Rent, Rates & Taxes | 30,000 | Fixed |
| Selling Expenses | 1,00,000 | 20% of Sales |
| Sales (Selling Price ₹40/unit) | 8,00,000 | — |

**(a)** Prepare a Flexible Budget for the production levels of **15,000 / 20,000 / 25,000 units**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the **Flexible Budget Variance** if actual production was **18,000 units** with an actual total cost of **₹5,80,000**. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

**(a) Step 1 — Determine per-unit variable rates (at 20,000 units):**

$$
\text{Direct Materials/unit} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹15
$$

$$
\text{Direct Wages/unit} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹10
$$

$$
\text{Indirect Wages/unit} = \frac{60{,}000 - 20{,}000}{20{,}000} = \frac{40{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹2
$$

$$
\text{Power/unit} = \frac{50{,}000 - 10{,}000}{20{,}000} = \frac{40{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹2
$$

$$
\text{Selling Expense/unit} = 20\% \text{ of } ₹40 = ₹8
$$

**Step 2 — Build cost expressions $y = a + bx$:**

| Cost | Fixed $a$ (₹) | Variable $b$ (₹/unit) |
|------|---------------:|----------------------:|
| Direct Materials | 0 | 15 |
| Direct Wages | 0 | 10 |
| Indirect Wages | 20,000 | 2 |
| Power | 10,000 | 2 |
| Depreciation | 40,000 | 0 |
| Rent, Rates & Taxes | 30,000 | 0 |
| Selling Expenses | 0 | 8 |
| **Total** | **1,00,000** | **37** |

**Step 3 — Flexible Budget Table:**

Total cost at 15,000 units:

$$
TC_{15k} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 37 \times 15{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}55{,}000 = ₹6{,}55{,}000
$$

Total cost at 20,000 units:

$$
TC_{20k} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 37 \times 20{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}40{,}000 = ₹8{,}40{,}000
$$

Total cost at 25,000 units:

$$
TC_{25k} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 37 \times 25{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}25{,}000 = ₹10{,}25{,}000
$$

| Cost (₹) | 15,000 units | 20,000 units | 25,000 units |
|----------|-------------:|-------------:|-------------:|
| Direct Materials | 2,25,000 | 3,00,000 | 3,75,000 |
| Direct Wages | 1,50,000 | 2,00,000 | 2,50,000 |
| Indirect Wages | 50,000 | 60,000 | 70,000 |
| Power | 40,000 | 50,000 | 60,000 |
| Depreciation | 40,000 | 40,000 | 40,000 |
| Rent, Rates & Taxes | 30,000 | 30,000 | 30,000 |
| Selling Expenses | 1,20,000 | 1,60,000 | 2,00,000 |
| **Total Cost** | **6,55,000** | **8,40,000** | **10,25,000** |
| Sales Revenue | 6,00,000 | 8,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| **Profit / (Loss)** | **(55,000)** | **(40,000)** | **(25,000)** |

*(7 marks — split: 2 for cost behaviour table, 3 for FB table, 2 for correct totals & sign)*

---

**(b) Flexible Budget Variance at 18,000 actual units:**

Step 1 — Compute Flexible Budget at 18,000 units:

$$
FB_{18k} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 37 \times 18{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}66{,}000 = ₹7{,}66{,}000
$$

Step 2 — Compare with Actual Cost (₹5,80,000):

$$
FBV = AC - FB = 5{,}80{,}000 - 7{,}66{,}000 = -1{,}86{,}000 \quad (\text{Favourable})
$$

> **Interpretation:** The firm has actually spent ₹1,86,000 *less* than what should have been spent for 18,000 units — a strong sign of cost efficiency. The manager deserves commendation.

*(7 marks — split: 2 for FB@18k, 2 for variance, 1 for sign + interpretation, 2 for verification / cross-check)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

A manufacturing concern provides the following data for **March 2024**:

- **Budgeted production** = 10,000 units
- **Direct Material** = ₹10 per unit (Variable)
- **Direct Labour** = ₹6 per unit (Variable)
- **Factory Overhead** = ₹50,000 (Fixed) + ₹4 per unit (Variable)
- **Administrative Expenses** = ₹30,000 (Fixed)
- **Selling Expenses** = 10% of Sales (Variable)
- **Selling price** = ₹50 per unit

**(a)** Prepare a Flexible Budget for **8,000 / 10,000 / 12,000 units** and present the projected **profit** at each level. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Actual production for April was **9,000 units**, and the actual total cost incurred was **₹3,50,000**. Calculate the **Flexible Budget Variance** and the **Sales Volume Variance**. Also state whether the result is **Favourable or Adverse**. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

**(a) Step 1 — Identify cost structure:**

- Variable Material = ₹10/unit
- Variable Labour = ₹6/unit
- Variable Factory OH = ₹4/unit
- Variable Selling = 10% of ₹50 = ₹5/unit
- Fixed Factory OH = ₹50,000
- Fixed Admin = ₹30,000
- **Total Fixed** = ₹80,000; **Total Variable/unit** = ₹25

**Step 2 — Cost expression:**

$$
TC = 80{,}000 + 25x
$$

**Step 3 — Flexible Budget for 8,000 / 10,000 / 12,000 units:**

For $x = 8{,}000$:

$$
TC = 80{,}000 + 25 \times 8{,}000 = 80{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}80{,}000
$$

For $x = 10{,}000$:

$$
TC = 80{,}000 + 25 \times 10{,}000 = 80{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹3{,}30{,}000
$$

For $x = 12{,}000$:

$$
TC = 80{,}000 + 25 \times 12{,}000 = 80{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹3{,}80{,}000
$$

| Particulars (₹) | 8,000 units | 10,000 units | 12,000 units |
|------------------|------------:|-------------:|-------------:|
| Sales (₹50/u) | 4,00,000 | 5,00,000 | 6,00,000 |
| Variable Costs (₹25/u) | 2,00,000 | 2,50,000 | 3,00,000 |
| **Contribution** | **2,00,000** | **2,50,000** | **3,00,000** |
| Fixed Costs | 80,000 | 80,000 | 80,000 |
| **Profit** | **1,20,000** | **1,70,000** | **2,20,000** |

*(7 marks)*

---

**(b) Step 1 — Flexible Budget at 9,000 actual units:**

$$
FB_{9k} = 80{,}000 + 25 \times 9{,}000 = 80{,}000 + 2{,}25{,}000 = ₹3{,}05{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Flexible Budget Variance:**

$$
FBV = AC - FB = 3{,}50{,}000 - 3{,}05{,}000 = +₹45{,}000 \quad (\text{Adverse})
$$

**Step 3 — Sales Volume Variance:**

The contribution per unit is ₹25 (₹50 – ₹25).

$$
SVV = (x_{actual} - x_{budget}) \times CM_{per\ unit} = (9{,}000 - 10{,}000) \times 25 = -₹25{,}000 \quad (\text{Adverse})
$$

> **Interpretation:** The company produced 1,000 units *less* than budgeted, leading to a loss of contribution of ₹25,000. Additionally, at the actual activity level, the company overspent by ₹45,000 — indicating poor cost control *in addition* to the volume shortfall.

*(7 marks — split: 2 for FBV, 2 for SVV, 2 for sign and classification, 1 for interpretation)*

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 / Flexible Budgets**

- **Definition:** A Flexible Budget is one that *flexes* with actual activity within a relevant range.
- **Key Step 1:** Always classify every cost as *Fixed, Variable, or Semi-variable* before tabulating.
- **Key Step 2:** Express every cost as $y = a + bx$ (semi-variable), $y = bx$ (variable), or $y = a$ (fixed).
- **Key Step 3:** Substitute multiple activity levels into the cost expressions to build the FB table.
- **Core Variance Formula:** $FBV = AC - FB$ where $FB$ is computed at *actual* activity, not budgeted.
- **Sign Convention:** For costs — `Actual > Budget = Adverse`; `Actual < Budget = Favourable`.
- **Sales Volume Variance:** $SVV = (x_{actual} - x_{budget}) \times CM_{per\ unit}$.
- **Methods:** Tabular (most common in KTU), Formula, Chart, Multi-Activity, Departmental.
- **Relevant Range:** FB is valid *only* inside the range; outside it, fixed cost assumptions break.
- **Use Cases:** Performance evaluation, cost control, decision support, responsibility accounting.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing *Static Budget at Actual* with *Flexible Budget at Actual* — they are different.
- **Static vs Flexible:** Static is *one* level; Flexible is a *family* of budgets for a range of activity.
- **Cost Driver Identification:** Units, hours, kilometres, machine-hours — pick the one that *causes* the cost.
- **High-Low Method:** Used to split semi-variable cost; $b = (C_2 - C_1) / (x_2 - x_1)$.
- **High-Yield Exam Tip:** Always show 3 things — (i) cost-behaviour table, (ii) FB table, (iii) variance with interpretation.
- **Tools Used:** Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) analysis, standard costing, responsibility accounting.
- **Limitation:** Preparation is more complex and time-consuming than a static budget.

> **End of Module 4 / Flexible Budgets — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes** | **Course:** OEIET833 — Cost and Management Accounting for Engineers
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Zero Based Budgets and Budgetary Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Zero Based Budgets and Budgetary Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Zero Based Budgeting (ZBB) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Based Budgeting (ZBB)** is a budgeting technique in which every line item of expenditure in the budget must be justified afresh for each forthcoming period, starting from a **"zero base"**, as if the activities were being performed for the very first time. No prior period's budget figures are accepted as a baseline. The technique was pioneered by **Peter A. Pyhrr** at Texas Instruments (USA) in **1970**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for engineering management, ZBB is classified as a *non-incremental* budgeting approach, fundamentally different from the conventional "last year + adjustment" method. Each activity is critically examined through a structured **Decision Package (DP)** that evaluates its cost-effectiveness, necessity, and alternative methods of execution.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Blank Slate" Engineer

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a civil engineer designing a new flyover. The traditional (incremental) method says: *"Last year we spent ₹10 crore; let's add 5% inflation, so this year we need ₹10.5 crore."* The Zero Based Budgeting method says: *"Forget last year. Justify every rupee. Why do we need 1,500 tonnes of steel? Could we use 1,200 tonnes with high-tensile rebars and still meet safety factors? What does every screw, every worker-hour cost, and is each justified?"*

This is the **"blank slate"** philosophy. Every cost is a fresh proposal, not an inherited number. Engineers love this because it mirrors **Value Engineering** — seeking the optimal cost-to-performance ratio from first principles.

## 1.3 Budgetary Control — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Budgetary Control** is the continuous, systematic process of preparing budgets (planning), comparing actual performance with budgeted figures (comparison), and taking **corrective action** wherever deviations (variances) are observed, so that the organization's stated financial objectives are achieved. It is a tool of *financial control through delegation and responsibility accounting*.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Crane Dashboard"

Think of Budgetary Control as the **instrument cluster of a heavy-duty mobile crane**:

| Dashboard Gauge | Budgetary Control Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Speedometer** (planned vs. actual speed) | Standard Cost / Budget target |
| **Actual speed reading** | Actual operating cost / revenue |
| **Warning light** when speed exceeds limit | Variance analysis & exception reports |
| **Driver's corrective action** (eases the throttle) | Management corrective action (re-budget / re-engineer) |

Just as the crane operator cannot drive blind, a project manager cannot manage costs without a budget, performance gauge, and feedback loop.

## 1.5 The Relationship Between ZBB and Budgetary Control

> [!NOTE]
> ZBB is a **method of preparing** a budget, while Budgetary Control is a **system of using** budgets to monitor and control operations. ZBB can be applied *within* a Budgetary Control system when the management wishes to rigorously challenge the cost base. In KTU 2024 syllabus terms, they are often taught together because they complete the planning–control cycle.

### 1.6 Key Terminology (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Decision Package (DP):** A document that identifies and describes a specific activity in such a manner that management can evaluate it against other activities, decide whether to approve it, and rank it in order of priority.
- **Decision Unit (DU):** The lowest level of an organization for which a Decision Package is prepared (e.g., a single cost center, a department, or a project).
- **Ranking:** The process of arranging Decision Packages in descending order of priority for funding.
- **Variance:** The arithmetic difference between the budgeted (planned) figure and the actual figure.
- **Favourable Variance (F):** A variance that positively affects profit (e.g., actual cost < budgeted cost, or actual revenue > budgeted revenue).
- **Adverse / Unfavourable Variance (A):** A variance that negatively affects profit.
- **Flexible Budget:** A budget that adjusts automatically to the actual level of activity achieved, allowing meaningful variance analysis.
- **Responsibility Centre:** A unit headed by a manager who is accountable for the costs, revenues, or investments under their control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Zero Based Budgeting — Operational Mechanism

ZBB operates in a structured **3-phase cycle** (Pyhrr's original model):

### Phase 1 — Identification of Decision Units (DUs)

The organization is decomposed into logical sub-units. In an engineering company, a DU could be:
- A maintenance workshop
- A quality-control laboratory
- An R&D project team
- A logistics cell
- A specific product line

### Phase 2 — Preparation of Decision Packages (DPs)

For each DU, multiple DPs are constructed. Each DP contains:

1. **Description of the activity** — what is being done?
2. **Statement of objectives** — why is it being done?
3. **Cost of resources** — what does it consume (labour, material, overheads)?
4. **Performance measures** — output, output quality, time, compliance
5. **Alternative methods** — could the same outcome be achieved at lower cost (Value Engineering)?
6. **Consequences of non-approval** — what happens if the budget is denied? (This is the hallmark of ZBB — incremental budgets never ask this!)

> [!NOTE]
> **Levels of Decision Packages:** Most firms prepare DPs at *three* service levels:
> - **Level 1 (Minimum / Survival):** Cost of *essential* operations to keep the DU alive.
> - **Level 2 (Current / Maintenance):** Cost of operating at the *current* level of service.
> - **Level 3 (Improvement / Enhancement):** Cost of *upgrading* the service quality or output.

### Phase 3 — Ranking and Approval

The decision packages, across all DUs, are then **rank-ordered** in descending order of priority (cost-benefit ratio or strategic importance). Senior management then draws a "cut-off line": every DP above the line gets funded; every DP below the line is denied or deferred.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind ZBB — Core Rationale

| Rationale | Engineering/Management Interpretation |
|---|---|
| **Eradicate waste (muda)** | Lean manufacturing thinking; remove non-value-adding activities |
| **Force justification of costs** | Every cost-centre becomes a *proposal*, not an *entitlement* |
| **Optimize resource allocation** | Scarce capital is allocated to highest-NPV projects (capital rationing) |
| **Improve communication** | Managers must articulate *why* they need money, not just *how much* |
| **Detect obsolete activities** | Activities continued "because we always did them" are exposed |

## 2.3 Budgetary Control — Operational Mechanism

The Budgetary Control cycle has **6 logical stages**:

1. **Setting the Objectives** — translate strategic goals into quantifiable targets.
2. **Preparation of Budgets** — Sales, Production, Cost, Cash, Capital, Master Budget.
3. **Budgetary Period** — typically 1 year, subdivided into quarters/months.
4. **Budget Committee** — apex body that coordinates, resolves conflicts, and finalises figures.
5. **Comparison of Actual vs. Budget (Variance Analysis)** — done periodically.
6. **Corrective Action** — appropriate response to unfavourable variances.

## 2.4 Types of Budgets (KTU 2024 Classification)

| # | Type of Budget | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fixed Budget** | Prepared for a *single* level of activity. Does not change with output. |
| 2 | **Flexible Budget** | Adjusts to *actual* level of activity. Essential for valid variance analysis. |
| 3 | **Sales Budget** | Forecast of sales volume × selling price. |
| 4 | **Production Budget** | Sales + Desired Closing Stock − Opening Stock. |
| 5 | **Cash Budget** | Cash receipts − Cash payments. |
| 6 | **Capital Expenditure Budget** | Long-term asset acquisitions. |
| 7 | **Master Budget** | Consolidated summary of all functional budgets → Projected P&L, B/S, Cash Flow. |
| 8 | **Zero-Based Budget** | Every rupee re-justified from zero. |
| 9 | **Rolling / Continuous Budget** | A forward 12-month budget that rolls forward each month as the current month ends. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU ESE — Module 4**

| # | Concept | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Variance (Absolute) | $V = A - B$ | $V$ = Variance, $A$ = Actual, $B$ = Budgeted |
| 2 | Variance (Percentage) | $V\% = \dfrac{(A - B)}{B} \times 100$ | Percentage deviation from plan |
| 3 | Favourable (Cost) | $A < B \Rightarrow V = B - A$ (F) | Spending less is good |
| 4 | Favourable (Revenue) | $A > B \Rightarrow V = A - B$ (F) | Earning more is good |
| 5 | Adverse (Cost) | $A > B \Rightarrow V = A - B$ (A) | Overspending is bad |
| 6 | Production Budget Qty | $P = S + C_{\text{desired}} - O_{\text{opening}}$ | Output required to satisfy demand + stock |
| 7 | Cash Closing Balance | $CB = OB + R - P$ | Opening Balance + Receipts − Payments |
| 8 | Flexible Budget Cost | $FC = FC_{\text{fixed}} + VC_{\text{unit}} \times A_q$ | Flexed/adjusted cost for actual output $A_q$ |
| 9 | ZBB Decision Package Index | $DPI = \dfrac{\text{Benefit Score}}{\text{Cost Score}}$ | Used to rank competing DPs |
| 10 | Capacity Utilisation | $CU = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Budgeted Output}} \times 100$ | Used in variance analysis for fixed-cost absorption |
| 11 | Sales Variance | $SV = (A_s - B_s) \times B_p$ | Volume effect at budgeted price |
| 12 | Price Variance | $PV = (A_p - B_p) \times A_s$ | Price effect at actual volume |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real Industries

- **Construction Industry:** ZBB forces every sub-contractor line to be re-tendered, often yielding 8–15% cost savings on materials.
- **Manufacturing:** Budgetary Control links Standard Costing → Variance Analysis → P&L, giving shop-floor visibility.
- **IT/Tech:** Used for cloud-spend optimization, where every server, license, and storage subscription is justified.
- **Public Sector:** The Government of India mandates ZBB-style reviews under the *Zero-Base Budgeting Reform* in several ministries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Production Budget Derivation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A manufacturing company expects to sell **24,000 units** in the next year. It desires to maintain a **closing finished-goods stock of 3,000 units** and currently holds an **opening stock of 2,000 units**. Compute the Production Budget quantity.

### Step 1 — Identify the variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{Sales forecast} = 24{,}000 \text{ units} \\
C_{\text{desired}} &= \text{Desired closing stock} = 3{,}000 \text{ units} \\
O_{\text{opening}} &= \text{Opening stock} = 2{,}000 \text{ units} \\
P &= \text{Production budget quantity (unknown)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the standard Production Budget identity

The fundamental identity is that *production must cover both external sales and the change in inventory.* Therefore:

$$
P = S + (C_{\text{desired}} - O_{\text{opening}})
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 24{,}000 + (3{,}000 - 2{,}000) \\
P &= 24{,}000 + 1{,}000 \\
P &= 25{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The company must produce **25,000 units** — 24,000 to satisfy demand and an extra 1,000 to *build up* the closing stock (from 2,000 to 3,000 units). This is a classic KTU 14-mark sub-part in the Production Budget topic.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Flexible Budget & Variance Analysis

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A factory's monthly fixed overheads are **₹2,00,000** and variable overheads are **₹40 per unit** of output. The **budgeted output** is 5,000 units. The **actual output** achieved was 4,500 units and **actual overheads** incurred were **₹3,90,000**. Compute the flexible budget, the total variance, and classify it.

### Step 1 — Compute the Fixed Budget (original)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed Budget Total} &= \text{Fixed OH} + (\text{Variable OH per unit} \times \text{Budgeted Units}) \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + (40 \times 5{,}000) \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Flexible Budget at actual output

The flexible budget adjusts variable costs to actual activity, but keeps fixed costs unchanged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flexible Budget Total} &= \text{Fixed OH} + (\text{Variable OH per unit} \times \text{Actual Units}) \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + (40 \times 4{,}500) \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹}3{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Total Overhead Variance

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= \text{Actual OH} - \text{Flexible Budget OH} \\
&= 3{,}90{,}000 - 3{,}80{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}10{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Classification

Since actual overheads (₹3,90,000) **exceed** the flexible budget (₹3,80,000), the variance is **Adverse (A)**, i.e., the company overspent by ₹10,000 at the *actual* activity level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Many KTU students mistakenly compute variance against the *Fixed Budget* (₹4,00,000) and get a ₹10,000 *Favourable* variance. This is a **valuation trap**! Always compare Actual to *Flexible* Budget for fair comparison; the Fixed Budget comparison gives a "Volume Variance" of ₹20,000 Favourable due to lower activity.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — ZBB Decision Package Ranking

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A construction company has prepared 4 Decision Packages for a civil engineering division. The cost and benefit score (both on a 0–100 scale) for each DP are:

| DP | Cost Score | Benefit Score |
|---|---|---|
| DP-A (Bridge Inspection) | 20 | 80 |
| DP-B (Software Upgrade) | 50 | 70 |
| DP-C (Worker Training) | 30 | 60 |
| DP-D (New Survey Drone) | 60 | 90 |

The company has a total budget of **₹1,00,00,000** with the following resource costs to fund each DP in full: A=₹20L, B=₹50L, C=₹30L, D=₹60L. Find the funding priority ranking.

### Step 1 — Compute the DPI for each DP

$$
DPI = \frac{\text{Benefit Score}}{\text{Cost Score}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPI_A &= 80 / 20 = 4.00 \\
DPI_B &= 70 / 50 = 1.40 \\
DPI_C &= 60 / 30 = 2.00 \\
DPI_D &= 90 / 60 = 1.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Rank in descending order of DPI

| Rank | DP | DPI | Full Cost (₹ Lakhs) | Cumulative Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DP-A | 4.00 | 20 | 20 |
| 2 | DP-C | 2.00 | 30 | 50 |
| 3 | DP-D | 1.50 | 60 | 110 ← **Cut-off** |
| 4 | DP-B | 1.40 | 50 | 160 |

### Step 3 — Apply the funding cut-off

With ₹100 Lakhs available, the cut-off lies *between* DP-D and DP-B. Hence:

- **Funded:** DP-A (₹20L) + DP-C (₹30L) = **₹50L** (with ₹50L remaining)
- DP-D requires ₹60L, so it **cannot be fully funded** from the remaining ₹50L.
- DP-B is **rejected** (lowest DPI).

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In a real ZBB process, management may either *partially fund* DP-D (e.g., with the remaining ₹50L out of its ₹60L requirement) or *re-negotiate its scope* to fit the budget. This is the *flexibility* that traditional incremental budgets do not offer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ZBB vs. Traditional Incremental Budgeting

| Parameter | Traditional Incremental Budget | Zero Based Budget |
|---|---|---|
| Starting Point | Last year's actual + % adjustment | **Zero base** |
| Justification Required | Only for *incremental* changes | For **entire** expenditure |
| Time Required | Low | **High** |
| Cost of Preparation | Low | **High** (heavy paperwork, training) |
| Identification of Waste | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Employee Morale | Stable (entitlement culture) | May be **demotivating** (constant justification) |
| Suitability for Engineering SMEs | High | Moderate (overhead of process) |
| Suitability for Public Sector / Govts | Low | **High** (forces prioritization) |
| Tooling Required | Spreadsheets | **Specialized ZBB software / Decision Packages** |
| Reaction to Inefficiencies | Inherited & perpetuated | **Eliminated at the source** |
| Ranking of Activities | Not done | **Central feature** |

## 3.5 Comprehensive Variance Analysis Framework

For a KTU 14-mark problem, variance analysis is typically broken down as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost Variance (TCV)} &= \text{Material Variance} + \text{Labour Variance} + \text{Overhead Variance} \\
\text{Overhead Variance} &= \text{Spending Variance} + \text{Volume (Capacity) Variance} \\
\text{Spending Variance} &= \text{Actual OH} - \text{Flexed OH for hours worked} \\
\text{Volume Variance} &= \text{Flexed OH for hours worked} - \text{Absorbed OH (Std OH)} \\
\text{Absorbed OH} &= \text{Standard OH rate} \times \text{Standard hours for actual output}
\end{aligned}
$$

This 3-tier decomposition is a **standard KTU 14-mark ask** in any ESE paper covering budgetary control.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Zero-Based Budgeting Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startPoint([Step 0: Define Organization Goals]) --> step1[Step 1: Identify Decision Units DU]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Prepare Decision Packages DP per DU]
    step2 --> step2a[Level 1 Minimum Service]
    step2 --> step2b[Level 2 Current Service]
    step2 --> step2c[Step 3: Improvement Service]
    step2a --> step3[Step 4: Rank DPs by DPI Priority]
    step2b --> step3
    step2c --> step3
    step3 --> step4[Step 5: Allocate Resources Draw Cut off Line]
    step4 --> step5[Step 6: Approve Funded Packages]
    step5 --> step6[Step 7: Implement and Monitor]
    step6 --> step7([Final: Approved ZBB Master Budget])
    style startPoint fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style step7 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style step4 fill:#FFA07A,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The orange-highlighted node (Step 5) represents the *critical decision point* where the cut-off line is drawn — the very heart of ZBB.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Budgetary Control Cycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ob1[Objective Setting] --> pb1[Plan and Prepare Budgets]
    pb1 --> eb1[Execute Operations at Functional Level]
    eb1 --> mr1[Measure Actual Performance]
    mr1 --> cp1[Compare Actual vs Budget]
    cp1 --> vDec{Variance Exists?}
    vDec -- No --> cont1[Continue Operations as Planned]
    vDec -- Yes --> ct1[Classify Variance F or A]
    ct1 --> ca1[Investigate Root Cause]
    ca1 --> rA1[Take Corrective Action]
    rA1 --> pb1
    cont1 --> rep1[Periodic Reporting to Management]
    rep1 --> pb1
    style ob1 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style vDec fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style rA1 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style rep1 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The yellow diamond (Variance Exists?) is the central decision point. The red node (Corrective Action) feeds back into the planning phase, completing the **cybernetic control loop** — the foundation of management control systems theory.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Variance Decomposition Tree (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootV[Total Cost Variance TCV]
    rootV --> matV[Material Variance]
    rootV --> labV[Labour Variance]
    rootV --> ohV[Overhead Variance]
    matV --> mpV[Material Price Variance]
    matV --> muV[Material Usage Variance]
    labV --> lpV[Labour Rate Variance]
    labV --> leV[Labour Efficiency Variance]
    ohV --> osV[Overhead Spending Variance]
    ohV --> ovV[Overhead Volume Variance]
    style rootV fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style matV fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000
    style labV fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000
    style ohV fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Total Cost Variance is the *parent* node; Material, Labour, and Overhead variances are the three primary *children*; each of these further subdivides. KTU 14-mark questions typically test 2–3 of these sub-branches.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Functional Interaction Between ZBB and Budgetary Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stratGoal[Strategic Goals] --> zbbProc[ZBB Process: Build Budget from Zero]
    zbbProc --> masterB[Master Budget]
    masterB --> bcSystem[Budgetary Control System]
    bcSystem --> varAnalysis[Variance Analysis Engine]
    varAnalysis --> mgmtInfo[MIS Reports to Management]
```
