```mermaid
    mgmtInfo --> decisionAct[Corrective and Strategic Decisions]
    decisionAct -.-> zbbProc
    decisionAct -.-> bcSystem
    style zbbProc fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style bcSystem fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style varAnalysis fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style mgmtInfo fill:#90EE90,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This shows the *complementarity* of ZBB (planning from zero) and Budgetary Control (monitoring & control). The dashed feedback lines show the cybernetic loop between decision-making and the two systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set-A]`
**Define Zero Based Budgeting. Mention any two of its advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Based Budgeting (ZBB)** is a budgeting technique in which each expenditure item must be justified afresh in every budget period, starting from a **zero base**, as if the activities were being performed for the first time. It was developed by **Peter Pyhrr** in 1970.

**Two Advantages (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Eliminates inefficiency and waste:** Since no cost is automatically carried forward from previous periods, obsolete and non-value-adding activities are exposed and can be discontinued.
2. **Effective resource allocation:** Decision Packages are ranked by cost-benefit, ensuring scarce capital is directed to the highest-priority activities.

*Marking Scheme: 1 mark for definition, 1.5 marks for each advantage (or 1 + 0.5 for partial).*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set-B]`
**List the essential features of an effective Budgetary Control system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The essentials of an effective Budgetary Control system are:

1. **Clearly defined objectives** that are specific, measurable, and time-bound.
2. **Well-organised budget programme** with active participation of all departmental heads.
3. **Establishment of a Budget Committee** to coordinate, consolidate, and finalise budgets.
4. **Standard costing and variance analysis** mechanism for periodic comparisons.
5. **Flexible budgets** to allow fair comparison with actual results at the achieved activity level.
6. **Prompt reporting and corrective action** when deviations occur.

*Marking Scheme: 0.5 marks per essential (any 6).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (a) In ZBB questions, students often *forget to list the alternatives* within a Decision Package. Justifying a cost is **not enough** — the DP must compare the proposed method with at least one **alternative way** to achieve the same objective. (b) In variance analysis, always compare *Actual* with the **Flexible Budget** at the **actual level of activity** — comparing with the Fixed Budget leads to a *volume-adjusted* error that costs 2–3 marks. (c) When asked to "discuss advantages and limitations," do not list only positives; KTU valuation keys *always* test the negative side for 4–5 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

> **"Zero Based Budgeting is often called a 'rebellion against the incremental tradition.' Discuss the meaning, process, advantages, and limitations of ZBB. Can it be successfully applied in Indian engineering public-sector undertakings?"**

#### Part (a) — Meaning and Process of ZBB *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Meaning (2 marks):**
Zero Based Budgeting is a non-incremental budgeting method in which all budgeted expenditures for the upcoming period must be re-justified from a *zero* baseline. It was developed by **Peter Pyhrr** at Texas Instruments in 1970 and has since been adopted by corporations and governments worldwide to optimise resource allocation.

**(ii) Process (5 marks — broken down):**

1. **Identification of Decision Units (DUs):** The organisation is divided into logical sub-units. For an engineering PSU like BHEL, a DU could be a turbine-manufacturing shop or a quality-control laboratory.
2. **Preparation of Decision Packages (DPs):** Each DU prepares one or more DPs that describe the activity, its cost, expected benefits, alternative methods of execution, and consequences of non-approval.
3. **Service-Level Definition:** Most DPs are prepared at three levels — minimum (survival), current (maintenance), and improvement (enhancement).
4. **Ranking:** All DPs are ranked company-wide in descending order of priority (typically using a Decision Package Index = Benefit Score ÷ Cost Score).
5. **Resource Allocation:** Senior management draws a "cut-off line" based on resource availability. DPs above the line are funded; those below are rejected or deferred.
6. **Implementation and Review:** The approved DPs are operationalised, and performance is monitored.

#### Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Indian PSU Applicability *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Advantages (3 marks):**

1. Forces critical evaluation of every cost, eliminating waste and obsolete activities.
2. Allocates resources to high-priority activities, improving cost-benefit ratio.
3. Improves managerial communication and decision-making at all levels.
4. Detects duplication of effort and non-value-adding functions.

**(ii) Limitations (2 marks):**

1. Time-consuming and paper-intensive; not suitable for very small firms.
2. Requires skilled managers; otherwise, subjective DPs may be poorly framed.
3. May create demotivation since every cost must be re-justified each period.
4. Difficult to apply to long-term capital projects with multi-year horizons.

**(iii) Applicability to Indian PSUs (2 marks):**

ZBB is **highly suitable** for Indian engineering PSUs (e.g., BHEL, NTPC, SAIL) because:
- They typically have **large overheads** and inertia-driven incremental budgets.
- Public accountability demands efficient, transparent resource allocation.
- The Government of India has actively pushed *Outcome Budgeting* (an evolution of ZBB) since 2005.

However, success requires:
- Trained personnel and proper IT infrastructure.
- Political and administrative will to *actually* discontinue underperforming DPs.
- A clear link between DP approval and managerial KPIs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 4]`

> **A company has provided the following data for the month of June 2024: Budgeted production = 6,000 units; Actual production = 5,200 units; Standard hours per unit = 4; Variable overhead standard rate = ₹8 per hour; Fixed overhead budget = ₹96,000; Actual variable overheads incurred = ₹1,72,000; Actual fixed overheads incurred = ₹1,00,000. Prepare a Flexible Budget, compute the Total Variable Overhead Variance and Total Fixed Overhead Variance, and explain the importance of budgetary control in manufacturing industries.**

#### Part (a) — Flexible Budget and Variance Computation *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Standard Hours for Actual Output (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Standard Hours for Actual Output} &= \text{Actual Units} \times \text{Standard Hours per Unit} \\
&= 5{,}200 \times 4 = 20{,}800 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Prepare the Flexible Budget (2 marks)**

| Element | Budgeted (6,000 units) | **Flexible Budget (5,200 units)** | Actual |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Hours | 24,000 hrs | **20,800 hrs** | 20,800 hrs |
| Variable OH @ ₹8/hr | ₹1,92,000 | **₹1,66,400** | ₹1,72,000 |
| Fixed OH | ₹96,000 | **₹96,000** | ₹1,00,000 |
| **Total OH** | ₹2,88,000 | **₹2,62,400** | ₹2,72,000 |

**Step 3 — Variable Overhead Variance (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{var}} &= \text{Actual Variable OH} - (\text{Standard Rate} \times \text{Std Hours for Actual Output}) \\
&= 1{,}72{,}000 - (8 \times 20{,}800) \\
&= 1{,}72{,}000 - 1{,}66{,}400 = \text{₹}5{,}600 \text{ (Adverse)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Fixed Overhead Variance (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{fixed}} &= \text{Actual Fixed OH} - \text{Budgeted Fixed OH} \\
&= 1{,}00{,}000 - 96{,}000 = \text{₹}4{,}000 \text{ (Adverse)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both variances are **Adverse (A)** since actuals exceed the flexed budget.

#### Part (b) — Importance of Budgetary Control in Manufacturing *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Planning and Coordination (2 marks):** Budgets integrate the sales, production, procurement, and finance functions into a unified plan, ensuring that every department works towards a common goal.

2. **Performance Evaluation (2 marks):** Variance analysis allows managers to objectively compare actual performance against standards, identifying which foremen, machines, or product lines are underperforming.

3. **Cost Control (1.5 marks):** Continuous monitoring forces managers to take corrective action before small inefficiencies snowball into major losses.

4. **Resource Optimisation (1 mark):** Budgets highlight idle capacity, over-stocking, and inefficient cash management, leading to better utilisation of working capital and fixed assets.

5. **Strategic Alignment (0.5 marks):** Budgetary control ties day-to-day operations to the long-term strategic objectives, making it the *operational spine* of a manufacturing enterprise.

*Mark Allocation Note: 5+2 split — main computation carries 5 marks and the descriptive part carries 2 marks. Adjust per KTU valuation pattern.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 4**

- ✅ **ZBB starts from ZERO** — never from "last year + 5%."
- ✅ **Three Levels of Decision Packages** — Minimum, Current, Improvement.
- ✅ **Decision Package Index (DPI) = Benefit Score / Cost Score** — used for ranking.
- ✅ **Peter Pyhrr (1970, Texas Instruments)** is the inventor — high-frequency 1-mark question.
- ✅ **Budgetary Control has 6 stages** — Objective, Budget, Period, Committee, Comparison, Corrective Action.
- ✅ **Fixed Budget = single level of activity; Flexible Budget = adjusts to actual activity.**
- ✅ **Variance must be computed against the FLEXIBLE budget, not the fixed budget**, for fairness.
- ✅ **Favourable (F)** = cost less than budget OR revenue more than budget. **Adverse (A)** = the opposite.
- ✅ **Total Cost Variance = Material + Labour + Overhead Variance** — standard KTU breakdown.
- ✅ **Production Budget Formula:** $P = S + (C_{\text{desired}} - O_{\text{opening}})$.
- ✅ **Cash Budget Identity:** $CB = OB + \text{Receipts} - \text{Payments}$.
- ✅ **ZBB disadvantages:** Time-consuming, demotivating, requires trained staff, high documentation cost.
- ✅ **ZBB is ideal for public-sector / large engineering units** where resource rationalisation is critical.
- ✅ **Rolling Budget** = continuously updated 12-month forward budget. **Master Budget** = consolidated summary.
- ✅ **Examiner's most-tested numerical:** Flexible Budget preparation + Total Overhead Variance classification. Practise this 3 times before the exam.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Budgetary Control Stages:** "**O-B-P-C-C-C**" → **O**bjectives, **B**udgets, **P**eriod, **C**ommittee, **C**omparison, **C**orrective Action.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for Cost Variance Decomposition:** "**M-L-O**" → **M**aterial (Price + Usage), **L**abour (Rate + Efficiency), **O**verhead (Spending + Volume).
- ✅ **Always label each numerical answer with the variance type** (F or A) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark if you forget this!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction of Various Budgets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET833 - COST AND MANAGEMENT ACCOUNTING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction of Various Budgets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — CONSTRUCTION OF VARIOUS BUDGETS

## 1.1 Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Budget (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A **budget** is a *quantitative financial plan*, prepared and approved *before a defined period*, expressing the planned policy of management in **operational** and/or **financial terms** for that period. It serves as a *blueprint* of the organization's projected revenues, expenses, working capital, and capital expenditure.

### Conceptual Analogy 🏠

Think of a **budget like a household monthly plan**:

- You estimate your **income** (salary, rent received) → analogous to **Sales Budget**.
- You estimate your **groceries** based on how many guests you expect → analogous to **Production Budget**.
- You decide how much to spend on **rent, EMI, bills** → analogous to **Cash Budget**.

A **budget simply converts your business intentions into rupees and units** before the month begins. If reality deviates, you compare it with the plan and take *corrective action* (this is called **budgetary control**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Budget**: The *consolidated summary* of all functional budgets (sales, production, cost, cash) projected for the budget period. It culminates in a **Budgeted Income Statement** and **Budgeted Balance Sheet**.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Budgets (KTU High-Yield Chart)

| Category | Type | Purpose |
|----------|------|---------|
| **Based on Function** | Sales Budget | Forecast revenue |
| | Production Budget | Forecast output volume |
| | Direct Material Budget | Forecast raw material needs |
| | Direct Labour Budget | Forecast workforce cost |
| | Overhead Budget | Forecast indirect costs |
| | Cash Budget | Forecast liquidity position |
| **Based on Flexibility** | Fixed Budget | One level of activity |
| | Flexible Budget | Sliding scale, multiple activity levels |
| **Based on Approach** | Zero-Based Budget (ZBB) | Every expense re-justified from "zero" |
| | Incremental Budget | Last year + % change |
| | Performance Budget | Activity-based outputs |
| **Comprehensive** | Master Budget | Complete financial + operational plan |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:**
> *"Differentiate between Fixed and Flexible Budget"* and *"What is Zero-Based Budgeting?"* — both are **3-mark short-answer favourites**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logic Flow: How a Master Budget is Built

The preparation of a master budget follows a strict **sequential dependency**:

1. **Forecast Sales Volume** (driven by market research).
2. **Determine Desired Ending Inventory** (based on policy, e.g., 20% of next month's sales).
3. **Compute Production Volume** = Sales + Closing Inventory − Opening Inventory.
4. **Estimate Material & Labour Needs** based on production.
5. **Build Overhead, Selling & Admin Cost Budgets**.
6. **Consolidate into Cost of Goods Sold**.
7. **Project Cash Inflows/Outflows → Cash Budget**.
8. **Derive Budgeted P&L and Balance Sheet**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind this sequence:**
> Sales is the *independent variable*; everything else (production, materials, labour) is a **derived requirement**. This is why a *Sales Budget* is always prepared *first* in any Master Budget cycle.

---

## 2.2 ⭐ KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 8 formulas — they solve ~90% of KTU numerical questions on budgets.**

### (a) Production Budget

$$
\text{Required Production} = \text{Budgeted Sales} + \text{Desired Closing Stock} - \text{Opening Stock}
$$

### (b) Material Purchase Budget (Quantity)

$$
\text{Material to be Purchased} = \text{Material for Production} + \text{Desired Closing Stock} - \text{Opening Stock}
$$

$$
\text{Material for Production} = \text{Units to Produce} \times \text{Standard Quantity per Unit}
$$

### (c) Material Purchase Budget (Value)

$$
\text{Purchase Value} = \text{Quantity to Purchase} \times \text{Standard Price per Unit}
$$

### (d) Cash Payments for Materials

$$
\text{Cash Paid this Month} = \text{Current Month Purchases} \times \text{Cash \%} + \text{Previous Month Purchases} \times \text{Credit \% remaining}
$$

### (e) Direct Labour Cost Budget

$$
\text{Labour Cost} = \text{Units to Produce} \times \text{Hours per Unit} \times \text{Rate per Hour}
$$

### (f) Cash Budget Equation

$$
\text{Closing Cash Balance} = \text{Opening Cash} + \text{Total Cash Receipts} - \text{Total Cash Payments}
$$

### (g) Flexible Budget Equation

$$
\text{Flexed Cost } Y = \text{Fixed Cost } (a) + \text{Variable Cost per Unit } (b) \times \text{Actual Activity Level } (x)
$$

### (h) Zero-Based Budget — Decision Package Scoring

$$
\text{Rank Score} = w_1 \cdot \text{Necessity} + w_2 \cdot \text{Cost-Benefit} + w_3 \cdot \text{Strategic Fit}
$$

where the weights $w_i$ sum to **1**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| **Manufacturing Plants** | Production budgets drive raw-material procurement and shift scheduling. |
| **Construction Firms** | Capital budgets dictate equipment acquisition and project financing. |
| **IT & Software Firms** | Flexible budgets help manage fluctuating project-based workload. |
| **Automotive (e.g., Maruti, Tata Motors)** | ZBB is used by government PSUs for cost rationalization. |
| **Startups** | Cash budgets prevent insolvency by forecasting monthly burn-rate. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic and arithmetic transition is **explicitly written out**. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Production Budget & Material Purchase Budget

### Given Data

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Sales (units): Jan, Feb, Mar | 1,000 / 1,200 / 1,500 |
| Desired closing stock | 25% of next month's sales |
| Opening stock (Jan 1) | 250 units |
| Raw material required | 4 kg per unit of finished product |
| Material opening stock (Jan 1) | 1,000 kg |
| Desired material closing stock | 20% of next month's consumption |

### Step 1 — Sales Budget

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sales Value (Jan)} &= 1{,}000 \times \text{SP (assume ₹50)} = \text{₹50,000} \\
\text{Sales Value (Feb)} &= 1{,}200 \times 50 = \text{₹60,000} \\
\text{Sales Value (Mar)} &= 1{,}500 \times 50 = \text{₹75,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reason:** Each row converts physical volume into revenue using the constant selling price.

### Step 2 — Desired Closing Stock of Finished Goods

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Closing} &= 25\% \times 1{,}200 = 300 \text{ units} \\
\text{Feb Closing} &= 25\% \times 1{,}500 = 375 \text{ units} \\
\text{Mar Closing} &= 25\% \times 1{,}600 \text{ (assumed Apr sales)} = 400 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Production Budget (Using the Formula)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Production} &= 1{,}000 + 300 - 250 = 1{,}050 \text{ units} \\
\text{Feb Production} &= 1{,}200 + 375 - 300 = 1{,}275 \text{ units} \\
\text{Mar Production} &= 1{,}500 + 400 - 375 = 1{,}525 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reason:** Add sales to the buffer you wish to keep at month-end, then subtract what you already have on hand (opening stock).

### Step 4 — Material Consumption Budget

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Consumption} &= 1{,}050 \times 4 = 4{,}200 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Feb Consumption} &= 1{,}275 \times 4 = 5{,}100 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mar Consumption} &= 1{,}525 \times 4 = 6{,}100 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Desired Material Closing Stock

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Closing} &= 20\% \times 5{,}100 = 1{,}020 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Feb Closing} &= 20\% \times 6{,}100 = 1{,}220 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mar Closing} &= 20\% \times (\text{Apr consumption assumed}) = 1{,}300 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Material Purchase Budget (Quantity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Purchases} &= 4{,}200 + 1{,}020 - 1{,}000 = 4{,}220 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Feb Purchases} &= 5{,}100 + 1{,}220 - 1{,}020 = 5{,}300 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mar Purchases} &= 6{,}100 + 1{,}300 - 1{,}220 = 6{,}180 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Material Purchase Budget (Value @ ₹10/kg)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 4{,}220 \times 10 = \text{₹42,200} \\
\text{Feb} &= 5{,}300 \times 10 = \text{₹53,000} \\
\text{Mar} &= 6{,}180 \times 10 = \text{₹61,800}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cash Budget

### Given Data

- **Opening Cash Balance (Jan 1): ₹60,000**
- Sales: Jan ₹2,00,000; Feb ₹3,00,000; Mar ₹4,00,000
- Collection pattern: **40% cash in month of sale; 30% in next month; 30% in month after that**
- Purchases: Jan ₹80,000; Feb ₹1,00,000; Mar ₹1,20,000
- **Lag in payment: 1 month** (i.e., Feb pays for Jan's purchases)
- Wages (paid same month): Jan ₹30,000; Feb ₹35,000; Mar ₹40,000
- Manufacturing overhead (paid same month): ₹20,000 per month
- Selling expenses: ₹10,000 per month
- Equipment purchase in Feb: ₹50,000
- Dividend in Mar: ₹25,000

### Step A — Schedule of Cash Receipts

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Receipts} &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = 80{,}000 \\
\text{Feb Receipts} &= (3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30) \\
                  &= 1{,}20{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 1{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{Mar Receipts} &= (4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40) + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30) \\
                  &= 1{,}60{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 3{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reason:** Track each rupee to the *month it actually hits the bank*, not the month it was billed.

### Step B — Schedule of Cash Payments

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Payments} &= \text{Wages } 30{,}000 + \text{MOH } 20{,}000 + \text{Selling } 10{,}000 = 60{,}000 \\
\text{Feb Payments} &= \text{Purchases (Jan)} 80{,}000 + \text{Wages } 35{,}000 + \text{MOH } 20{,}000 \\
                   &\quad + \text{Selling } 10{,}000 + \text{Equipment } 50{,}000 = 1{,}95{,}000 \\
\text{Mar Payments} &= \text{Purchases (Feb)} 1{,}00{,}000 + \text{Wages } 40{,}000 + \text{MOH } 20{,}000 \\
                   &\quad + \text{Selling } 10{,}000 + \text{Dividend } 25{,}000 = 1{,}95{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Cash Budget (Final Table)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan Closing Cash} &= 60{,}000 + 80{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 80{,}000 \\
\text{Feb Closing Cash} &= 80{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}95{,}000 = 65{,}000 \\
\text{Mar Closing Cash} &= 65{,}000 + 3{,}10{,}000 - 1{,}95{,}000 = 1{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reason:** Each month the closing balance becomes the next month's opening balance. The company has **positive liquidity every month** — no overdraft required.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Tested)

| Basis | Fixed Budget | Flexible Budget |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|
| Activity Level | Single (planned) | Multiple (range) |
| Behaviour of Costs | Static | Cost behaviour considered (fixed/variable split) |
| Usefulness when actual ≠ planned | Low | High |
| Variance Analysis | Not possible beyond volume variance | Enables proper **flexed budget variance** |
| Suits | Stable industries | Dynamic/variable-output businesses |
| Cost Equation | One number per line item | $y = a + bx$ formula per item |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's favourite trick:**
> *"What would be the flexible budget cost at 9,000 units if Fixed = ₹50,000 and Variable = ₹8/unit?"*
> → **Answer:** $50{,}000 + (9{,}000 \times 8) = \text{₹1,22,000}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Budget Architecture (KTU Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sales Forecast] --> B[Sales Budget]
    B --> C[Production Budget]
    C --> D[Direct Material Purchase Budget]
    C --> E[Direct Labour Budget]
    C --> F[Manufacturing Overhead Budget]
    D --> G[Cost of Goods Sold Budget]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Budgeted Income Statement]
    B --> I[Cash Receipts Schedule]
    D --> J[Cash Payments Schedule]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    K[Capital Expenditure Plan] --> J
    I --> L[Cash Budget]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Budgeted Balance Sheet]
    H --> M
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - All **operational budgets** (left side) flow into the **Income Statement**.
> - All **cash schedules** (right side) flow into the **Cash Budget**.
> - The **Master Budget** is the union of the Budgeted Income Statement + Budgeted Balance Sheet + supporting schedules.

---

## 4.2 Sequential Logic: Cash Budget Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1 - Identify Cash Receipts Sources] --> stepB[Step 2 - Apply Collection Lag]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3 - Identify Cash Payments Sources]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4 - Apply Payment Lag]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5 - Add Opening Balance]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6 - Compute Closing Balance]
    stepF --> stepG{Is Balance Negative?}
    stepG -- Yes --> stepH[Plan Borrowings - Overdraft]
    stepG -- No --> stepI[Plan Surplus Investment]
```

---

## 4.3 Zero-Based Budgeting Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    zbbA[Define Decision Units] --> zbbB[Identify Cost-Benefit Packages]
    zbbB --> zbbC[Evaluate Each Package from Zero]
    zbbC --> zbbD[Rank by Strategic Priority]
    zbbD --> zbbE[Allocate Resources Within Budget Cap]
    zbbE --> zbbF[Approve Packages Above Cut-Off Line]
    zbbE --> zbbG[Reject Packages Below Cut-Off Line]
```

> **Why ZBB matters in KTU exams:**
> It demonstrates *cost consciousness* — every rupee must justify its existence, not just inherited from last year.

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a "Flexible Budget" and state two situations where it is more useful than a Fixed Budget.**

**Model Answer:**

A **flexible budget** is a budget designed to *adjust with the actual level of activity* achieved, by separating costs into fixed and variable components and applying the cost equation:

$$
Y = a + bx
$$

where $Y$ = total cost, $a$ = fixed cost, $b$ = variable cost per unit, $x$ = activity level.

**Two situations where it is preferred over a fixed budget:**

1. **Seasonal or fluctuating demand** industries (e.g., air-conditioners, ice-cream, textile) where actual output rarely matches the planned level.
2. **Responsibility accounting & variance analysis** — flexible budgets isolate **flexed budget variance** (cost-control efficiency) from **volume variance** (sales efficiency), enabling fairer performance evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's Tip: Always mention that flexible budgets use **standard cost behaviour patterns**, not actual costs.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)? Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**Zero-Based Budgeting (ZBB)** is a budgeting technique in which *every expenditure must be justified from a "zero base"* in each budget period — i.e., previous year's allocations are *not* treated as a starting point.

**Two advantages:**

1. **Cost Rationalization** — Inefficient or non-essential activities are identified and eliminated, leading to genuine cost reduction rather than incremental creep.
2. **Resource Optimization** — Funds are allocated to activities with the *highest cost-benefit and strategic fit*, improving the efficiency of resource use.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Model Question Paper Style]**

> **Q.A (a) — 7 Marks:**
> *Explain the concept of a Master Budget. With a flowchart, describe its components.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Master Budget** is a comprehensive financial plan that consolidates all functional budgets (sales, production, cost, cash, capital) of an organization for a specific future period. It culminates in two key statements: the **Budgeted Income Statement (B/P&L)** and the **Budgeted Balance Sheet**.

**Components:**

1. **Operating Budgets:** Sales, Production, Direct Material, Direct Labour, Manufacturing Overhead, Selling & Administrative, Cost of Goods Sold.
2. **Financial Budgets:** Cash Budget, Capital Expenditure Budget, Budgeted Income Statement, Budgeted Balance Sheet.

**Flowchart (text version):**

> Sales Forecast → Sales Budget → Production Budget → Material/Labour/OH Budgets → Cost of Goods Sold → Budgeted P&L → Budgeted Balance Sheet.
>
> Parallel branch: Sales & Cost Schedules → Cash Receipts/Payments → Cash Budget → Budgeted Balance Sheet.

> **Reasoning:** The *flow* is necessary because costs are derived from production, which is derived from sales — making the **Sales Budget the cornerstone** of the entire master budget cycle.

---

> **Q.A (b) — 7 Marks:**
> *From the following data, prepare a **Production Budget** and **Material Purchase Budget** for Q1 (Jan–Mar):*

| Item | Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr (for ref.) |
|------|-----|-----|-----|----------------|
| Budgeted Sales (units) | 2,000 | 2,500 | 3,000 | 3,500 |

- Desired closing stock of finished goods = **20% of next month's sales**.
- Opening stock on Jan 1 = **400 units**.
- Raw material per unit = **3 kg**.
- Desired closing stock of raw material = **25% of next month's consumption**.
- Opening raw material stock = **1,500 kg**.
- Standard price of material = **₹8 per kg**.

**Model Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Desired Closing Stock (Finished Goods)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 20\% \times 2{,}500 = 500 \\
\text{Feb} &= 20\% \times 3{,}000 = 600 \\
\text{Mar} &= 20\% \times 3{,}500 = 700
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Production Budget

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 2{,}000 + 500 - 400 = 2{,}100 \text{ units} \\
\text{Feb} &= 2{,}500 + 600 - 500 = 2{,}600 \text{ units} \\
\text{Mar} &= 3{,}000 + 700 - 600 = 3{,}100 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Material Consumption

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 2{,}100 \times 3 = 6{,}300 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Feb} &= 2{,}600 \times 3 = 7{,}800 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mar} &= 3{,}100 \times 3 = 9{,}300 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Desired Closing Stock (Material)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 25\% \times 7{,}800 = 1{,}950 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Feb} &= 25\% \times 9{,}300 = 2{,}325 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mar} &= 25\% \times (\text{Apr consumption}) \text{ assumed based on Apr production}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Material Purchase (Quantity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 6{,}300 + 1{,}950 - 1{,}500 = 6{,}750 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Feb} &= 7{,}800 + 2{,}325 - 1{,}950 = 8{,}175 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mar} &= 9{,}300 + \text{Apr closing} - 2{,}325
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Material Purchase (Value @ ₹8/kg)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 6{,}750 \times 8 = \text{₹54,000} \\
\text{Feb} &= 8{,}175 \times 8 = \text{₹65,400} \\
\text{Mar} &= (\text{Apr closing}) \times 8
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Alternative Module Choice]**

> **Q.B (a) — 7 Marks:**
> *Explain the **format and preparation of a Cash Budget** with a suitable illustration.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Cash Budget** is a financial budget that forecasts the **cash receipts and cash payments** of an organization over a specific period, resulting in a projected **closing cash balance**.

**Format:**

| Particulars | Jan | Feb | Mar |
|-------------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Opening Cash Balance** | X | Y | Z |
| **Add: Cash Receipts** | | | |
| — Cash Sales | | | |
| — Collection from Debtors | | | |
| — Other Receipts | | | |
| **Total Cash Available (A)** | | | |
| **Less: Cash Payments** | | | |
| — Cash Purchases | | | |
| — Payment to Creditors | | | |
| — Wages & Salaries | | | |
| — Overheads | | | |
| — Capital Expenditure | | | |
| **Total Payments (B)** | | | |
| **Closing Balance (A − B)** | | | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual difference:** A Cash Budget is **NOT** the same as a Fund Flow Statement. Cash Budget covers a *short period (≤ 1 year)* in **monthly/quarterly** granularity; Fund Flow covers *long-term* sources and uses.

---

> **Q.B (b) — 7 Marks:**
> *Prepare a **Cash Budget** for Jan, Feb, Mar from the following data:*

- **Opening Cash Balance** (Jan 1): ₹60,000
- **Sales:** Jan ₹2,00,000; Feb ₹3,00,000; Mar ₹4,00,000
- **Collection pattern:** 40% in month of sale; 30% next month; 30% in the month after.
- **Purchases:** Jan ₹80,000; Feb ₹1,00,000; Mar ₹1,20,000
- **Payment lag:** 1 month (Feb pays for Jan's purchases, and so on).
- **Wages (same month):** Jan ₹30,000; Feb ₹35,000; Mar ₹40,000
- **Manufacturing Overhead (same month):** ₹20,000/month
- **Selling Expenses:** ₹10,000/month
- **Equipment Purchase:** Feb ₹50,000
- **Dividend Payment:** Mar ₹25,000

**Model Solution:**

#### Step 1 — Cash Receipts Schedule

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = 80{,}000 \\
\text{Feb} &= (3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30) = 1{,}20{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 1{,}80{,}000 \\
\text{Mar} &= (4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40) + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30) \\
           &= 1{,}60{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 3{,}10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Cash Payments Schedule

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan} &= 30{,}000 \text{ (W)} + 20{,}000 \text{ (MOH)} + 10{,}000 \text{ (SE)} = 60{,}000 \\
\text{Feb} &= 80{,}000 \text{ (Purchases)} + 35{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 50{,}000 \text{ (Equip)} = 1{,}95{,}000 \\
\text{Mar} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ (Purchases)} + 40{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 25{,}000 \text{ (Div)} = 1{,}95{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Cash Budget

| Particulars | Jan (₹) | Feb (₹) | Mar (₹) |
|-------------|---------|---------|---------|
| Opening Balance | 60,000 | 80,000 | 65,000 |
| Add: Receipts | 80,000 | 1,80,000 | 3,10,000 |
| **Total Available** | **1,40,000** | **2,60,000** | **3,75,000** |
| Less: Payments | 60,000 | 1,95,000 | 1,95,000 |
| **Closing Balance** | **80,000** | **65,000** | **1,80,000** |

> **Interpretation:** The firm maintains **positive liquidity throughout** — February shows a temporary dip (₹65,000) due to equipment purchase, which is comfortably recovered by March.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks in Budget Problems (KTU Examiner Survey):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the lag effect:** Students add this month's purchases to this month's payments without applying the 1-month credit period. **Solution:** Always read the credit terms *twice*.
> 2. **Confusing closing stock of FG vs RM:** Finished-goods closing stock is based on **sales**, raw-material closing stock is based on **consumption**.
> 3. **Ignoring the opening balance in Cash Budget:** A common blunder — forgetting that the previous month's closing balance must flow forward.
> 4. **Using actual costs in Flexible Budgets:** Flexible budgets use **STANDARD cost behaviour** ($a + bx$), not actual costs. Markers deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 5. **Missing units:** Writing "₹6,750" instead of "6,750 kg" in material purchase quantity — valuation strictly penalises unit ambiguity.

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and stick it on your study wall before the exam:**
>
> - ✅ **Budget** = Pre-approved quantitative plan.
> - ✅ **Master Budget** = All functional + financial budgets combined.
> - ✅ **Production Budget** formula: $\text{Sales} + \text{Closing FG} - \text{Opening FG}$.
> - ✅ **Material Purchase (qty)**: $\text{Consumption} + \text{Closing RM} - \text{Opening RM}$.
> - ✅ **Cash Receipts** depend on **collection lag**, not billing date.
> - ✅ **Cash Payments** depend on **payment lag** for credit purchases.
> - ✅ **Closing Cash** = Opening + Receipts − Payments (becomes next month's opening).
> - ✅ **Flexible Budget**: $Y = a + bx$ — use **standard** rates, not actual.
> - ✅ **Zero-Based Budget**: every rupee re-justified from scratch.
> - ✅ **Capital Expenditure** goes into Cash Budget under payments, not operating expenses.
> - ✅ **Always present in table format** with monthly columns and units specified.
> - ✅ **Sequence:** Sales → Production → Materials/Labour/OH → Cost of Goods Sold → P&L → Balance Sheet.
> - ✅ Cash Budget ≠ Fund Flow (short-term vs long-term distinction).
> - ✅ Variances are computed using **flexed budget**, not the original fixed budget.
> - ✅ ZBB is best suited for **discretionary cost centres** (R&D, advertising, training).
